n210011:
tn The meaning of вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (qohelet) is somewhat puzzling. The verb вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”Ц·ЧңвҖҺ (qahal) means вҖңto assemble, summonвҖқ (HALOT 1078-79 s.v. вҖҸЧ§Ч”ЧңвҖҺ), and is derived from the noun вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”ЦёЧңвҖҺ (qahal, вҖңassemblyвҖқ; HALOT 1079-80 s.v. вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”ЦёЧңвҖҺ). Thus вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ might mean: (1) convener of the assembly, (2) leader, speaker, teacher, or preacher of the assembly, or (3) member of the assembly. Elsewhere in the book, вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ is used in collocation with statements about his position as king in Jerusalem (Eccl 1:12), his proclamations about life (Eccl 1:2; 7:27; 12:8), and his teaching of wisdom and writing wise sayings (Eccl 12:9-10). Thus, вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ probably means вҖңthe leader of the assemblyвҖқ or вҖңspeaker of the assembly.вҖқ (See also the following study note.) Rabbinic literature treats вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ as a traditional surname for Solomon, that is, вҖңQoheleth,вҖқ relating it to the noun вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”ЦёЧңвҖҺ. For example, this explanation is found in rabbinic literature (Qoheleth Rabbah 1:1): вҖңWhy was his name called Qoheleth [вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ]? Because his words were proclaimed in public meeting [вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”Ц·ЧңвҖҺ], as it is written (1 Kgs 8:1).вҖқ The LXX rendered it бјҗОәОәО»О·ПғО№ОұПғП„бҪөПӮ (ekklД“siastД“s, вҖңmember of the assembly,вҖқ LSJ 509), as was the custom of relating Greek бјҗОәОәО»О·ПғОҜОұ (ekklД“sia, вҖңassemblyвҖқ) to Hebrew вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”ЦёЧңвҖҺ. The bookвҖҷs English title, вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ is simply a transliteration of the Greek term from the LXX. SymmachusвҖҷ ПҖОұПҒОҝО№ОјО№ОұПғП„бҪөПӮ (paroimiastД“s, вҖңauthor of proverbs,вҖқ LSJ 1342 s.v.) is not a translation of вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ but refers to his authorship of many proverbs (Eccl 12:9-10). In terms of the participial form, вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ is used substantively to designate the profession or title of the author. The term is used in 12:8 with the article, indicating that it is a professional title rather than a personal surname: вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч§ЦјЧ•Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (haqqohelet, вҖңthe TeacherвҖқ). Substantival participles often designate the title or profession of an individual: вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№Ч”ЦөЧҹвҖҺ (kohen), вҖңpriestвҖқ; вҖҸЧЁЦ№Ч–ЦөЧҹвҖҺ (rozen), вҖңrulerвҖқ; вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ№ЧҳЦөЧЁвҖҺ (shoter), вҖңofficerвҖқ; вҖҸЧ Ц№Ч§ЦөЧ“вҖҺ (noqed), вҖңsheep-breederвҖқ; вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ№ЧӨЦөЧҳвҖҺ (shofet), вҖңjudgeвҖқ; вҖҸЧҷЦ№ЧҰЦөЧЁвҖҺ (yotser), вҖңpotterвҖқ; вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧЁЦөЧқвҖҺ (korem), вҖңvine-dresserвҖқ; вҖҸЧҷЦ№Ч’ЦөЧ‘вҖҺ (yogev), вҖңfarmerвҖқ; вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•Ц№ЧўЦөЧЁвҖҺ (shoКҝer), вҖңgate-keeperвҖқ; вҖҸЧҰЧ•Ц№ЧЁЦөЧЈвҖҺ (tsoref), вҖңsmelterвҖқ; and вҖҸЧЁЦ№ЧӨЦөЧҗвҖҺ (rofeКҫ), вҖңdoctorвҖқ (IBHS 614-15 В§37.2a). In terms of its feminine ending with a male referent, JoГјon 1:266-67 В§89.b suggests that it is intensive, e.g., вҖҸЧһЧ•Ц№Ч“Ц·ЧўЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ (modaвҖҷat) вҖңclose relativeвҖқ from вҖҸЧһЧ•Ц№Ч“ЦёЧўвҖҺ (modaКҝ) вҖңkinsman.вҖқ The feminine ending is used similarly in Arabic in reference to a male referent, e.g., Arabic rawiyat вҖңa great narratorвҖқ from rawi вҖңnarratorвҖқ (C. P. Caspari, A Grammar of the Arabic Language, 1:233c). So вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ may mean вҖңthe leader/teacher of the assemblyвҖқ from the noun вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”ЦёЧңвҖҺ. When used in reference to a male referent, feminine forms denote a professional title or vocational office (as in Arabic, Ethiopic, and Aramaic), e.g., вҖҸЧЎЦ№ЧӨЦ¶ЧЁЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (soferet), вҖңscribeвҖқ; вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ№ЧӣЦ¶ЧЁЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (pokheret), вҖңgazelle-catcherвҖқ; вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ¶Ч—ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (pekhah), вҖңprovincial governorвҖқ; and вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ°ЧЁЦёЧўЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (peraКҝot), вҖңprincesвҖқ (GKC 393 В§122.r). Occasionally, a professional name later became a personal name, e.g., the title вҖҸЧЎЦ№ЧӨЦ¶ЧЁЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (вҖңscribeвҖқ) became the name вҖңSopherethвҖқ (Ezra 2:55; Neh 7:57), вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ№ЧӣЦ¶ЧЁЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (вҖңgazelle-catcherвҖқ) became вҖңPokerethвҖқ (Ezra 2:57; Neh 7:59), and perhaps вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (вҖңassemblerвҖқ) became the surname вҖңQohelethвҖқ (HALOT 926 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ№ЧӣЦ¶ЧЁЦ¶ЧӘ Ч”Ц·ЧҰЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧҷЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ). Many translations render вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ as a professional title: вҖңthe SpeakerвҖқ (NEB, Moffatt), вҖңthe PreacherвҖқ (KJV, RSV, YLT, MLB, ASV, NASB), вҖңthe TeacherвҖқ (NIV, NRSV), вҖңthe Leader of the AssemblyвҖқ (NIV margin), вҖңthe AssemblerвҖқ (NJPS margin). Others render it as a personal surname: вҖңKohelethвҖқ (JPS, NJPS) and вҖңQohelethвҖқ (NAB, NRSV margin).
sn The verbal root вҖҸЧ§Ч”ЧңвҖҺ means вҖңto assemble; to summonвҖқ (HALOT 1078-79 s.v. вҖҸЧ§Ч”ЧңвҖҺ). It is used of assembling or summoning various groups of people: вҖңall IsraelвҖқ (1 Chr 13:5; 15:3), вҖңthe elders of IsraelвҖқ (1 Kgs 8:1; 2 Chr 5:2), all the elders of their tribesвҖқ (Deut 31:28), вҖңall the princes of IsraelвҖқ (1 Chr 28:1), вҖңyour tribesвҖқ (Deut 31:28), вҖңall the house of JudahвҖқ (1 Kgs 12:21; 2 Chr 11:1), вҖңthe peopleвҖқ (Deut 4:10; 31:12), вҖңthe whole congregationвҖқ (Lev 8:3; Num 1:18; 16:19; 20:8), вҖңall the congregation of the sons of IsraelвҖқ (Exod 35:1; Num 8:9), вҖңthe assemblyвҖқ (Num 10:7; 20:10), and вҖңyour assemblyвҖқ (Ezek 38:13). The verb is often used in reference to summoning/assembling people for a religious occasion (Exod 35:1; Lev 8:3-4; Num 8:9; Deut 4:10; Josh 18:1; 22:12; 1 Kgs 8:1; 2 Chr 5:2-3), a political occasion (2 Sam 20:14), a military occasion (Judg 20:11; 2 Chr 11:1), or a judicial occasion (Job 11:10). The Hiphil stem is used to describe the action of the leader (usually a single individual who possesses/commands authority) summoning the people, e.g., Moses (Exod 35:1; Lev 8:3; Num 20:10), Moses and Aaron (Num 1:18), Korah (Num 16:19), King David (1 Chr 13:5; 15:3; 28:1), King Solomon (1 Kgs 8:1; 12:21; 2 Chr 5:2), and King Rehoboam (2 Chr 11:1). In almost every case, he who assembles the people is a person invested with authority; he makes a public proclamation or leads the nation in an important action. The Niphal stem is most often used to describe the people assembling at the direction of the leader (e.g., Lev 8:4; Josh 18:1; 22:12; 1 Kgs 8:2; 2 Chr 5:3). The root вҖҸЧ§Ч”ЧңвҖҺ is a denominative derived from the noun вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”ЦёЧңвҖҺ (qahal, вҖңassembly, contingentвҖқ; HALOT 1079-80 s.v. вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ”ЦёЧңвҖҺ). The noun has numerous referents: the congregated nation as a whole in the wilderness, assembled for ceremonies and instruction (Exod 16:3; Lev 4:13, 21; 16:17, 33; Num 10:7; 14:5; 15:15; 16:3; 17:12; 19:20; 20:4, 6, 10, 12; Deut 9:10; 10:4; 18:16); the congregation of Israel assembled for divine worship (Pss 22:23, 26; 26:5; 35:18; 40:10; 107:32; 149:1; Lam 1:10); the postexilic community assembled to hear the Torah and instruction (Neh 13:1); a military contingent assembled for warfare (Num 16:3; 20:4; Judg 20:2; 21:5, 8; 1 Sam 17:47; 2 Chr 28:14); people summoned to court (Ezek 16:40; 23:46-47); judicial authorities (Jer 26:17; Prov 5:14; 26:26; Sir 7:7). The term is often used as a designation for Israel: вҖңthe assembly of IsraelвҖқ (Lev 16:17; Deut 31:30; Josh 8:35; 1 Kgs 8:14, 22, 55; 12:3; 2 Chr 6:3, 12-13; Sir 50:13, 20), вҖңthe assembly of the congregationвҖқ (Exod 12:6); вҖңthe assembly of the congregation of the sons of IsraelвҖқ (Num 14:5), and вҖңthe assembly of GodвҖқ (Neh 13:1). The related noun вҖҸЧ§Ц°Ч”ЦҙЧңЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (qehillah) means вҖңassembly, communityвҖқ (HALOT 1080 s.v. вҖҸЧ§Ц°Ч”ЦҙЧңЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ), e.g., in the idiom вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӘЦ·Чҹ Ч§Ц°Ч”ЦҙЧңЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (natan qehillah) вҖңto hold an assemblyвҖқ: вҖңI called a great assembly to deal with themвҖқ (Neh 5:7).
n210012: tn Heb вҖңson of DavidвҖқ or вҖңa son of David.вҖқ
n210013: sn While 1:1 says only вҖңking in JerusalemвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦ° Ч‘ЦјЦҙЧҷЧЁЧ•ЦјЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦёЧқЦҙвҖҺ, melekh birushala[y]im), 1:12 adds вҖңking over Israel in JerusalemвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦ° ЧўЦ·ЧңЦҫЧҷЦҙЧ©ЧӮЦ°ЧЁЦёЧҗЦөЧң Ч‘ЦјЦҙЧҷЧЁЧ•ЦјЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦёЧқЦҙвҖҺ, melekh Кҝal-yisraКҫel birushala[y]im). The LXX adds вҖңIsraelвҖқ in 1:1 to harmonize with 1:12; however, the MT makes sense as it stands. Apart from David, only Solomon was вҖңking over Israel in JerusalemвҖқвҖ”unless the term вҖңIsraelвҖқ (вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧ©ЧӮЦ°ЧЁЦёЧҗЦөЧңвҖҺ, yisraКҫel) in 1:12 is used for Judah or the postexilic community. Solomon would fit the description of the author of this book, who is characterized by great wisdom (1:13, 16), great wealth (2:8), numerous servants (2:7), great projects (2:4-6), and the collection, editing, and writing of many proverbs (12:9-10). All of this generally suggests Solomonic authorship. However, many scholars deny Solomonic authorship on the basis of linguistic and historical arguments.
n210014: sn The form of the title is typical: (1) вҖңthe words of [the writer],вҖқ (2) his family name or name of his father, and (3) his social/political position in Israel (e.g., Prov 22:17; 24:23; 30:1; 31:1). Sometimes, the writerвҖҷs qualifications are given in the introduction to an OT book (e.g., Jer 1:1; Amos 1:1). Qoheleth lists his qualifications at the end of the book (12:9-12).
n210016: sn See the note on вҖңTeacherвҖқ in v. 1.
n210017: tn Heb вҖңfutility of futilities.вҖқ The phrase вҖңabsolutely futileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”ЦІЧ‘ЦөЧң Ч”ЦІЧ‘ЦёЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, havel havalim) is a superlative genitive construction (GKC 431 В§133.i). When a plural genitive follows a singular construct noun of the same root, it indicates the most outstanding example of the person or thing described. Examples: вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч“Ц¶Ч©ЧҒ Ч”Ц·Ч§ЦјЦіЧ“ЦёЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (qodesh haqqodashim, вҖңholy of holiesвҖқ), i.e., вҖңthe most holy placeвҖқ (Exod 26:33); вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧЁ Ч”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦҙЧҷЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (shir hashirim, вҖңsong of songsвҖқ), i.e., вҖңthe most excellent songвҖқ (Song 1:1); вҖҸЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦөЧҷ Ч”ЦёЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦҙЧҷЧқ Ч•Ц·ЧҗЦІЧ“Ц№Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч”Ц·ЧҗЦұЧ“Ц№Ч ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (Кҫelohe haКҫelohim vaКҫadone haКҫedonim, вҖңthe God of gods and Lord of lordsвҖқ), i.e., вҖңthe highest God and the supreme LordвҖқ (Deut 10:17). See also R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 17-18, В§80; IBHS 154 В§9.5.3j.
n210018: tn Although вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (kol, вҖңeverything, allвҖқ) is often used in an absolute or comprehensive sense (BDB 481 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ 1), it is frequently used as a synecdoche of the general for the specific, that is, its sense is limited contextually to the topic at hand (BDB 482 s.v. 2). This is particularly true of вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (hakkol, BDB 482 s.v. 2.b) in which the article particularizes or limits the referent to the contextual or previously mentioned topic (e.g., Gen 16:12; 24:1; Exod 29:24; Lev 1:9, 13; 8:27; Deut 2:36; Josh 11:19 [see 2 Sam 19:31; 1 Kgs 14:26 = 2 Chr 12:9]; 21:43; 1 Sam 30:19; 2 Sam 17:3; 23:5; 24:23; 1 Kgs 6:18; 2 Kgs 24:16; Isa 29:11; 65:8; Jer 13:7, 10; Ezek 7:14; Pss 14:3; 49:18; 1 Chr 7:5; 28:19; 29:19; 2 Chr 28:6; 29:28; 31:5; 35:7; 36:17-18; Ezra 1:11; 2:42; 8:34-35; 10:17; Eccl 5:8). Thus, вҖңallвҖқ does not always mean вҖңallвҖқ in an absolute sense or universally in comprehension. In several cases the context limits its reference to two classes of objects or issues being discussed, so вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ means вҖңbothвҖқ (e.g., 2:14; 3:19: 9:1, 2). Thus, вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ refers only to what Qoheleth characterizes as вҖңfutileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ, hevel) in the context. Qoheleth does not mean that everything in an absolute, all-encompassing sense is futile. For example, the sovereign work of God is not вҖңfutileвҖқ (3:1-4:3); fearing God is not вҖңfutileвҖқ (2:26; 3:14-15; 11:9-12:1, 9, 13-14); and enjoying life as a righteous person under the blessing of God is not вҖңfutileвҖқ (2:24-26; 11:9-10). Only those objects or issues that are contextually placed under вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ are designated as вҖңfutileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). The context of 1:3-15 suggests that 1:2 refers to the futility of secular human endeavor. The content and referent of 1:3-15 determines the referent of вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ in 1:2.
n210019:
tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel, вҖңfutileвҖқ) is repeated five times within the eight words of this verse for emphasis. The noun вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ is the key word in Ecclesiastes. The root is used in two ways in the OT, literally and figuratively. The literal, concrete sense is used in reference to the wind, manвҖҷs transitory breath, evanescent vapor (Isa 57:13; Pss 62:10; 144:4; Prov 21:6; Job 7:16). In this sense, it is often a synonym for вҖңbreathвҖқ or вҖңwindвҖқ (Eccl 1:14; Isa 57:13; Jer 10:14). The literal sense lent itself to metaphorical senses: (1) breath/vapor/wind is nonphysical, evanescent, and lacks concrete substance thus, the connotation вҖңunsubstantialвҖқ (Jer 10:15; 16:19; 51:18), вҖңprofitlessвҖқ or вҖңfruitlessвҖқ (Ps 78:33; Prov 13:11), вҖңworthlessвҖқ (2 Kgs 17:15; Jer 2:5; 10:3), вҖңpointlessвҖқ (Prov 21:6), вҖңfutileвҖқ (Lam 4:17; Eccl 1:2, 14; 2:1, 14-15), (2) breath/vapor/wind is transitory and fleetingвҖ”thus, the connotation вҖңfleetingвҖқ or вҖңtransitoryвҖқ (Prov 31:30; Eccl 6:12; 7:15; 9:9; 11:10; Job 7:16) and (3) breath/vapor/wind cannot be seen thus, the idea of вҖңobscure,вҖқ вҖңdark,вҖқ вҖңdifficult to understand,вҖқ вҖңenigmaticвҖқ (Eccl 11:10). See HALOT 236-37 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ; BDB 210-11 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ. The metaphorical sense is used with the following synonyms: вҖҸЧӘЦјЦ№Ч”Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (tohu, вҖңempty, vanityвҖқ; Isa 49:4), вҖҸЧЁЦҙЧҷЧ§вҖҺ (riq, вҖңprofitless, uselessвҖқ; Isa 30:7; Eccl 6:11), and вҖҸЧңЦ№Чҗ Ч”Ч•Ц№ЧўЦҙЧҷЧңвҖҺ (loКҫ hoКҝil, вҖңworthless, profitlessвҖқ; Is 30:6; 57:12; Jer 16:19). It is parallel to вҖңfew daysвҖқ and вҖң[days] which he passes like a shadowвҖқ (Eccl 6:12). It is used in reference to youth and vigor (11:10) and life (6:12; 7:15; 9:9), which are вҖңtransitoryвҖқ or вҖңfleeting.вҖқ The most common parallels to вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ in Ecclesiastes are the phrases вҖңchasing after the windвҖқ (вҖҸЧЁЦ°ЧўЧ•ЦјЧӘ ЧЁЧ•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ, reКҝut ruakh) in 2:11, 17, 26; 7:14 and вҖңwhat profit?вҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦ·Ч”ЦҫЧҷЦјЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ, mah yitron) or вҖңno profitвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЧҹ ЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ, Кҫen yitron) in 2:11; 3:19; 6:9. It is used in reference to enigmas in life (6:2; 8:10, 14) and to the future which is obscure (11:8). It is often used in antithesis to terms connoting value: вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңgood, benefit, advantageвҖқ) and вҖҸЧҷЦ№ЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (yoteron, вҖңprofit, advantage, gainвҖқ). Because the concrete picture of the вҖңwindвҖқ lends itself to the figurative connotation вҖңfutile,вҖқ the motto вҖңThis is futileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ–Ц¶Ч” Ч”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ, zeh hevel) is often used with the metaphor, вҖңlike striving after the windвҖқ (вҖҸЧЁЦ°ЧўЧ•ЦјЧӘ ЧЁЧ•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ, reКҝut ruakh)вҖ”a graphic picture of an expenditure of effort in vain because no one can catch the wind by chasing it (e.g., 1:14, 17; 2:11, 17, 26; 4:4, 6, 16; 6:9). Although it is the key word in Ecclesiastes, it should not be translated the same way in every place.
sn The motto Everything is futile! is the theme of the book. Its occurs at the beginning (1:2) and end of the book (12:8), forming an envelope structure (inclusio). Everything described in 1:2-12:8 is the supporting proof of the thesis of 1:2. With few exceptions (e.g., 2:24-26; 3:14-15; 11:9-12:1, 9), everything described in 1:2-12:8 is characterized as вҖңfutileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ, hevel).
n2100110: tn The term вҖңprofitвҖқ (вҖҸЧҷЦ№ЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ, yoteron) is used in Ecclesiastes to evaluate the ultimate benefit/effects of human activities, as is вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңgood, worthwhileвҖқ) as well (e.g., 2:1, 3). While some relative advantage/profit is recognized (e.g., light over darkness, and wisdom over folly), Qoheleth denies the ultimate advantage of all human endeavors (e.g., 2:11, 15).
n2100111: tn Heb вҖңthe man.вҖқ The Hebrew term could be used here in a generic sense, referring to the typical man (hence, вҖңa manвҖқ). However, it is more likely that the form is collective and that humankind in general is in view (note NIV вҖңmanвҖқ). Note the reference to вҖңa generationвҖқ coming and going in the next verse, as well as v. 13, where the phrase вҖңthe sons of manвҖқ (= humankind) appears. In this case the singular pronominal suffix and singular verb later in v. 3 reflect grammatical agreement, not individuality.
n2100112: tn The use of the relative pronoun вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ (she, вҖңwhichвҖқ)вҖ”rather than the more common вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©Ц¶ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ (Кҫasher, вҖңwhichвҖқ)вҖ”is a linguistic feature that is often used to try to date the book of Ecclesiastes. Noting that вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ is the dominant relative pronoun in Mishnaic Hebrew and that вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©Ц¶ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ does not appear as frequently (Jastrow 130 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©Ц¶ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ), many scholars conclude that вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©Ц¶ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ is early and вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ is late. They conclude that the use of вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ in Ecclesiastes points to a late date for the book. However, as Samuel-Kings suggest, the вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ versus вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©Ц¶ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ phenomena may simply be a dialectical issue: вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©Ц¶ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ is commonly used in the south, and вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ in the north. The use of вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ in Ecclesiastes may indicate that the book was written in a northern rather than a southern province, not that it is a late book. This is supported from related Akkadian terms which occur in texts from the same periods: вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©Ц¶ЧҒЧЁвҖҺ is related to aЕЎru (вҖңplaceвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ©Ц¶ЧҒвҖҺ is related to ЕЎa (вҖңwhatвҖқ).
n2100113: sn The Hebrew root вҖҸЧўЦёЧһЦёЧңвҖҺ, (Кҝamal, вҖңtoilвҖқ) is repeated here for emphasis: вҖңWhat gain does anyone have in his toil with which he toils.вҖқ For all his efforts, manвҖҷs endeavors and secular achievements will not produce anything of ultimate value that will radically revolutionize anything in the world. The term вҖңtoilвҖқ is used in a pejorative sense to emphasize that the only thing that man obtains ultimately from all his efforts is weariness and exhaustion. Due to sin, mankind has been cursed with the futility of his labor that renders work a вҖңtoilsomeвҖқ task (Gen 3:17-19). Although it was not yet revealed to Qoheleth, God will one day deliver the redeemed from this plight in the future kingdom when manвҖҷs labor will no longer be toilsome, but profitable, fulfilling, and enjoyable (Isa 65:17-23).
n2100114:
tn Heb вҖңunder the sun.вҖқ
sn This rhetorical question expects a negative answer: вҖңMan has no gain in all his toil.вҖқ Ecclesiastes often uses rhetorical questions in this manner (e.g., 2:2; 3:9; 6:8, 11, 12; see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949).
n2100115: tn The participle вҖҸЧ”Ц№ЧңЦөЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (holekh, вҖңto walk, to goвҖқ) emphasizes continual, durative, uninterrupted action (present universal use of participle). The root вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧңЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (halakh) is repeated in this section (1:4a, 6a, 6b, 7a, 7b, 7c) to emphasize the continual action and constant motion of everything in nature. Despite the continual action of everything in nature, there is no completion, attainment or rest for anything. The first use of вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧңЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ is in reference to man; all subsequent usages are in reference to natureвҖ”illustrations of the futility of human endeavor. Note: All the key terms used in 1:4 to describe the futility of human endeavor are repeated in 1:5-11 as illustrations from nature. The literary monotony in 1:4-11 mirrors the actual monotony of human action that repeats itself with no real change.
n2100116: tn The participle вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗвҖҺ (baКҫ, вҖңto go inвҖқ) emphasizes continual, durative, uninterrupted action (present universal use of participle). The term is repeated in 1:4-5 to compare the futility of secular human accomplishments with the futile actions in nature: everything is in motion, but there is nothing new accomplished.
n2100117: tn The participle вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧһЦёЧ“Ц¶ЧӘвҖҺ (Кҝomadet, вҖңto standвҖқ) emphasizes a continual, durative, uninterrupted state (present universal condition). Man, despite all his secular accomplishments in all generations, makes no ultimate impact on the earth.
n2100118: tn The term вҖңthe sameвҖқ does not appear in Hebrew, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2100119: tn The term вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ (Кҝolam) has a wide range of meanings: (1) indefinite time: вҖңlong time, duration,вҖқ often вҖңeternalвҖқ or вҖңeternityвҖқ; (2) future time: вҖңthings to comeвҖқ; and (3) past time: вҖңa long time back,вҖқ that is, the dark age of prehistory (HALOT 798-99 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ; BDB 761-63 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧңЧқвҖҺ). It may also denote an indefinite period of вҖңcontinuous existenceвҖқ (BDB 762 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧңЧқвҖҺ 2.b). It is used in this sense in reference to things that remain the same for long periods: the earth (Eccl 1:4), the heavens (Ps 148:6), ruined cities (Isa 25:2; 32:14), ruined lands (Jer 18:16), nations (Isa 47:7), families (Ps 49:12; Isa 14:20), the dynasty of Saul (1 Sam 13:13), the house of Eli (2 Sam 2:30), continual enmity between nations (Ezek 25:15; 35:5), the exclusion of certain nations from the assembly (Deut 23:4; Neh 13:1), a perpetual reproach (Ps 78:66).
n2100120: tn The Hebrew text has a perfect verbal form, but it should probably be emended to the participial form, which occurs in the last line of the verse. Note as well the use of participles in vv. 4-7 to describe what typically takes place in the natural world. The participle вҖҸЧ–Ч•Ц№ЧЁЦөЧ—Ц·вҖҺ (zoreakh, вҖңto riseвҖқ) emphasizes continual, durative, uninterrupted action (present universal use of participle): the sun is continually rising (and continually setting) day after day.
n2100121: tn Heb вҖңthe sun goes.вҖқ The participle вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗвҖҺ (baКҫ, вҖңto go inвҖқ) emphasizes continual, durative, uninterrupted action (present universal use of participle): the sun is continually rising and continually setting day after day. The repetition of вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗвҖҺ in 1:4-5 creates a comparison between the relative futility of all human endeavor (вҖңa generation comes and a generation goes [вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗвҖҺ]вҖқ) with the relative futility of the action of the sun (вҖңthe sun rises and the sun goesвҖқ [i.e., вҖңsets,вҖқ вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗвҖҺ]).
n2100122: tn Heb вҖңhastensвҖқ or вҖңpants.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧҗЦ·ЧЈвҖҺ (shaКҫaf) has a three-fold range of meanings: (1) вҖңto gasp; to pant,вҖқ (2) вҖңto pant after; to long for,вҖқ and (3) вҖңto hasten; to hurryвҖқ (HALOT 1375 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧҗЧЈвҖҺ; BDB 983 s.v. I вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧҗЦ·ЧЈвҖҺ). The related Aramaic root вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЧЈвҖҺ means вҖңto be thirsty; to be parched.вҖқ The Hebrew verb is used of вҖңgaspingвҖқ for breath, like a woman in the travail of childbirth (Isa 42:14); вҖңpantingвҖқ with eagerness or desire (Job 5:5; 7:2; 36:20; Ps 119:131; Jer 2:24) or вҖңpantingвҖқ with fatigue (Jer 14:6; Eccl 1:5). Here вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧҗЦ·ЧЈвҖҺ personifies the sun, panting with fatigue, as it hastens to its destination (BDB 983 s.v. I вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧҗЦ·ЧЈвҖҺ 1). The participle form depicts continual, uninterrupted, durative action (present universal use). Like the sun, manвҖ”for all his effortsвҖ”never really changes anything; all he accomplishes in his toil is to wear himself out.
n2100123: tn The verb вҖҸЧ–Ч•Ц№ЧЁЦөЧ—Ц·вҖҺ (zoreakh, вҖңto riseвҖқ) is repeated in this verse to emphasize that the sun is locked into a never changing, ever repeating monotonous cycle: rising, setting, rising, setting.
n2100124: tn The word вҖңagainвҖқ does not appear in Hebrew, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2100125: tn The Hebrew root вҖҸЧЎЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч‘вҖҺ (savav, вҖңto circle aroundвҖқ) is repeated four times in this verse to depict the windвҖҷs continual motion: вҖңThe wind circles around (вҖҸЧЎЧ•Ц№Ч‘ЦөЧ‘вҖҺ, sovev)вҖҰround and round (вҖҸЧЎЧ•Ц№Ч‘ЦөЧ‘ ЧЎЦ№Ч‘ЦөЧ‘вҖҺ)вҖҰits circuits (вҖҸЧЎЦ°Ч‘ЦҙЧҷЧ‘Ц№ЧӘЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ, sevivotayv).вҖқ This repetition is designed for a rhetorical purposeвҖ”to emphasize that the wind is locked into a never ending cycle. This vicious circle of monotonous action does not change anything. The participle form is used three times to emphasize continual, uninterrupted action (present universal use of participle). Despite the fact that the wind is always changing direction, nothing really new ever happens. The constant shifting of the wind cannot hide the fact that this is nothing but a repeated cycle; nothing new happens here (e.g., 1:9-10).
n2100126: tn The use of вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘вҖҺ (shav, Qal active participle masculine singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ, shuv, вҖңto returnвҖқ) creates a wordplay (paronomasia) with the repetition of вҖҸЧЎЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч‘вҖҺ (savav, вҖңto circle aroundвҖқ). The participle emphasizes continual, durative, uninterrupted action (present universal use).
n2100127: tn Heb вҖңare goingвҖқ or вҖңare walking.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ”Ц№ЧңЦ°ЧӣЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (holekhim, Qal active participle masculine plural from вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧңЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ, halakh, вҖңto walkвҖқ) emphasizes continual, durative, uninterrupted action (present universal use of participle). This may be an example of personification; this verb is normally used in reference to the human activity of walking. Qoheleth compares the flowing of river waters to the action of walking to draw out the comparison between the actions of man (1:4) and the actions of nature (1:5-11).
n2100128:
tn Heb вҖңthere they are returning to go.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (shavim, Qal active participle masculine plural from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ, shuv, вҖңto returnвҖқ) emphasizes the continual, durative action of the waters. The root вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ is repeated in 1:6-7 to emphasize that everything in nature (e.g., wind and water) continually repeats its actions. For all of the repetition of the cycles of nature, nothing changes; all the constant motion produces nothing new.
sn This verse does not refer to the cycle of evaporation or the return of water by underground streams, as sometimes suggested. Rather, it describes the constant flow of river waters to the sea. For all the action of the waterвҖ”endless repetition and water constantly in motionвҖ”there is nothing new accomplished.
n2100129: tn The word вҖңthisвҖқ is not in Hebrew, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100130: tn Heb вҖңthe things.вҖқ The Hebrew term вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (devarim, masculine plural noun from вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦёЧ‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ, davar) is often used to denote вҖңwords,вҖқ but it can also refer to actions and events (HALOT 211 s.v. вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦёЧ‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ 3.a; BDB 183 s.v. вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦёЧ‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ IV.4). Here, it means вҖңthings,вҖқ as is clear from the context: вҖңWhat has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be doneвҖқ (1:9). Here вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ can be nuanced вҖңoccurrencesвҖқ or even вҖң[natural] phenomena.вҖқ
n2100132:
tn The Hebrew text has no stated object. The translation supplies вҖңitвҖқ for stylistic reasons and clarification.
sn The statement no one can bear to describe it probably means that Qoheleth could have multiplied examples (beyond the sun, the wind, and the streams) of the endless cycle of futile events in nature. However, no tongue could ever tell, no eye could ever see, no ear could ever hear all the examples of this continual and futile activity.
n2100133: tn The term вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ (maleКҫ, вҖңto be filled, to be satisfiedвҖқ) is repeated in 1:7-8 to draw a comparison between the futility in the cycle of nature and human secular accomplishments: lots of action, but no lasting effects. In 1:7 вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЧ Ц¶Ч ЦјЧ•Цј ЧһЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ (Кҫenennu maleКҫ, вҖңit is never filledвҖқ) describes the futility of the water cycle: вҖңAll the rivers flow into the sea, yet the sea is never filled.вҖқ In 1:8 вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦ№ЧҗЦҫЧӘЦҙЧһЦјЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ (veloКҫ timmaleКҫ, вҖңit is never satisfiedвҖқ) describes the futility of human labor: вҖңthe ear is never satisfied with hearing.вҖқ
n2100134: tn Heb вҖңwhat is.вҖқ The Hebrew verbal form is a perfect. Another option is to translate, вҖңWhat has been.вҖқ See the next line, which speaks of the past and the future.
n2100135: tn The Hebrew verbal form is an imperfect.
n2100137: tn Alternately, вҖң[Even when] there is something of which someone might claimвҖҰ.вҖқ The terms вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒ Ч“ЦјЦёЧ‘ЦёЧЁ Ч©ЧҒЦ¶ЧҷЦјЦ№ЧҗЧһЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (yesh davar sheyyoКҫmar) may be an interrogative clause without an introductory interrogative particle (GKC 473 В§150.a). In questions, вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ often implies doubt about the existence of something (BDB 441 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ 2.b). The LXX rendered it as a question, as do most English versions: вҖңIs there anything of which it can be saidвҖҰ?вҖқ (KJV, ASV, RSV, MLB, NEB, NASB, NIV, NRSV). On the other hand, вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ is used elsewhere in the book of Ecclesiastes as a predication of existence (вҖңThere isвҖҰвҖқ) to assert the existence of something (2:13, 21; 4:8, 9; 5:13 [12]; 6:1, 11; 7:15; 8:6, 14; 9:4; 10:5). HALOT 443 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ 2 renders вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒ Ч“ЦјЦёЧ‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ as вҖңThere is somethingвҖҰ.вҖқ This view is taken by several translations: вҖңEven the thing of which we sayвҖҰвҖқ (NAB), вҖңMen may say of somethingвҖҰвҖқ (Moffatt), and вҖңSometimes there is a phenomena of which they sayвҖҰвҖқ (NJPS).
n2100138: tn The perfect tense verb вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҷЦёЧ”вҖҺ (hayah) refers to a past perfect situation: It describes an action that is viewed as a remote past event from the perspective of the past. This past perfect situation is brought out by the temporal adverb вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ (kevar, вҖңalreadyвҖқ; HALOT 459 s.v. I вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 460 s.v. I вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ; cf. 1:10; 2:12, 16; 3:15; 4:2; 6:10; 9:6-7). The expression вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ plus вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҷЦёЧ”вҖҺ connotes a past perfect nuance: вҖңit has already beenвҖқ (Eccl 1:10; see BDB 460 s.v.).
n2100139: sn This does not deny manвҖҷs creativity or inventiveness, only the ultimate newness of his accomplishments. For example, there is no essential difference between the first voyage to the moon and the discovery of America (different point of arrival, different vehicles of travel, but the same essential action and results).
n2100140: tn Heb вҖңin the ages long ago before us.вҖқ
n2100141: tn Heb вҖңThere is no remembrance of former things.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧЁЦҙЧҗЧ©ЧҒЦ№Ч ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (riКҫshonim, вҖңformer thingsвҖқ) is the masculine plural form of the adjective вҖҸЧЁЦҙЧҗЧ©ЧҒЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (riКҫshon, вҖңformer, first, chiefвҖқ; BDB 911 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦҙЧҗЧ©ЧҒЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ). When used in a temporal sense, the singular denotes вҖңformerвҖқ in time (BDB 911 s.v. 1.a) or вҖңfirstвҖқ in time (BDB 911 s.v. 2.a). The plural form is only used to denote вҖңformerвҖқ in time: вҖңformer persons,вҖқ i.e., ancestors, men of old (e.g., Lev 26:45; Deut 19:14; Job 18:20; Isa 61:4; Ps 79:8; Sirach 4:16) or вҖңformer things,вҖқ i.e., past events (e.g., Isa 41:22; 42:9; 43:9, 18; 46:9; 48:3). See BDB 911 s.v. 1.a, which suggests that this usage refers to вҖңformer persons.вҖқ This approach is adopted by several translations: вҖңmen of oldвҖқ (NEB, NAB, NIV, Moffatt), вҖңpeople of long agoвҖқ (NRSV), вҖңearlier onesвҖқ (NJPS), and вҖңformer generationsвҖқ (ASV). On the other hand, this Hebrew phrase may be nuanced вҖңformer thingsвҖқ or вҖңearlier thingsвҖқ (HALOT 1168 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦҙЧҗЧ©ЧҒЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ). This is adopted by some translations: вҖңformer thingsвҖқ (KJV, RSV) and вҖңearlier thingsвҖқ (NASB). Although future generations are mentioned in 1:11, what they will not remember is the past events. The context of 1:3-11 focuses on human achievement, that is, former things.
n2100142: tn The term вҖңrememberвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100143:
tn Heb вҖңand also of the last things which will be.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЦ№Ч ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (Кҫakharonim, вҖңthe future thingsвҖқ) is the masculine plural form of the adjective вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҫakharon) which means вҖңcoming afterвҖқ (BDB 30 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ) or вҖңat the backвҖқ (HALOT 36 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ). When used in a temporal sense, it may mean (1) вҖңlater one; (2) вҖңin the futureвҖқ; (3) вҖңlastвҖқ; or (4) вҖңat the lastвҖқ or вҖңin the endвҖқ (HALOT 36 s.v. 2). The plural form may be used in reference to (1) future generations, e.g., Deut 29:21; Pss 48:14; 78:4, 6; 102:19; Job 18:20; Eccl 4:16, or (2) future events, e.g., Neh 8:18 (BDB 30 s.v.). BDB 30 s.v. b suggests that this usage refers to вҖңfuture generations,вҖқ while HALOT 36 s.v. 2.c suggests future events. As mentioned in the previous note, it probably refers to future events rather than future generations.
sn The Hebrew terms translated former events and future events create a merism (two polar extremes encompass everything in between). This encompasses all secular achievements in human history past to future things yet to be done.
n2100144:
tn Heb вҖңThere will not be any remembrance of them among those who come after.вҖқ
sn According to Qoheleth, nothing new really happens under the sun (1:9). Apparent observations of what appears to be revolutionary are due to a lack of remembrance by subsequent generations of what happened long before their time in past generations (1:10-11a). And what will happen in future generations will not be remembered by the subsequent generations to arise after them (1:11b).
n2100146: tn Heb вҖңI gave my heartвҖқ or вҖңI set my mind.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (libbi, вҖңmy heartвҖқ) is an example of synecdoche of part (heart) for the whole (myself). Qoheleth uses this figurative expression frequently in the book. On the other hand, in Hebrew mentality, the term вҖңheartвҖқ is frequently associated with oneвҖҷs thoughts and reasoning; thus, this might be a metonymy of association (heart = thoughts). The equivalent English idiom would be вҖңI applied my mind.вҖқ
n2100147: tn Heb вҖңwith wisdom,вҖқ that is, with careful reflection in light of principles observed by the sages.
n2100148: tn Heb вҖңto seek and to search outвҖқ (вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ“Ц°ЧЁЧ•Ц№Ч©ЧҒ Ч•Ц°ЧңЦёЧӘЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ, lidrosh velatur). This is an example of a verbal hendiadys (the use of two synonymous verbs to state a common idea in an emphatic manner). The terms are used because they are closely related synonyms; therefore, the similarities in meaning should be emphasized rather than the distinctions in meaning. The verb вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (darash) means вҖңto inquire about; to investigate; to search out; to studyвҖқ (HALOT 233 s.v. вҖҸЧ“ЧЁЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ; BDB 205 s.v. вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ). This verb is used literally of the physical activity of investigating a matter by examining the physical evidence and interviewing eye-witnesses (e.g., Judg 6:29; Deut 13:15; 17:4, 9; 19:18), and figuratively (hypocatastasis) of mentally investigating abstract concepts (e.g., Eccl 1:13; Isa 1:17; 16:5; Pss 111:2; 119:45). Similarly, the verb вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ (tur) means вҖңto seek out, discoverвҖқ (HALOT 1708 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ 1.c; BDB 1064 вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ 2). The verb вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ is used literally of the physical action of exploring physical territory (Num 13:16-17; 14:6, 34-36; Job 39:8), and figuratively (hypocatastasis) of mentally exploring things (Eccl 1:13; 7:25; 9:1).
n2100149:
tn Heb вҖңunder heaven.вҖқ
sn Qoheleth states that he made a thorough investigation of everything that had been accomplished on earth. His position as king gave him access to records and contacts with people that would have been unavailable to others.
n2100150: tn This phrase does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is added in the translation for clarity.
n2100152: tn The phrase вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·Чҹ ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (Кҝinyan raКҝ, вҖңrotten business, grievous taskвҖқ) is used only in Ecclesiastes (1:13; 2:23, 26; 3:10; 4:8; 5:2, 13; 8:16). It is parallel with вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) вҖңfutileвҖқ in 4:8, and describes a вҖңgrave misfortuneвҖқ in 5:13. The noun вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝinyan, вҖңbusinessвҖқ) refers to something that keeps a person occupied or busy: вҖңbusiness; affair; task; occupationвҖқ (HALOT 857 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ; BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ). The related verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц·Ч”вҖҺ (Кҝanah) means вҖңto be occupied, to be busy withвҖқ (with the preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ, bet), e.g., Eccl 1:13; 3:10; 5:19 (HALOT 854 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ; BDB 775 s.v. II вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ). The noun is from the Aramaic loanword вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧ ЦёЧҗвҖҺ (КҝinyanaКҫ, вҖңconcern, careвҖқ). The verb is related to the Aramaic verb вҖңto try hard,вҖқ the Arabic verb вҖңto be busily occupied; to worry to be a matter of concern,вҖқ and the Old South Arabic root вҖңto be troubled; to strive withвҖқ (HALOT 854 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ). The phrase вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·Чҹ ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ is treated creatively by English translations: вҖңsore travailвҖқ (KJV, ASV), вҖңsad travailвҖқ (YLT), вҖңpainful occupationвҖқ (Douay), вҖңsorry businessвҖқ (NEB), вҖңsorry taskвҖқ (Moffatt), вҖңthankless taskвҖқ (NAB), вҖңgrievous taskвҖқ (NASB), вҖңtrying taskвҖқ (MLB), вҖңunhappy businessвҖқ (RSV, NRSV, NJPS), and вҖңheavy burdenвҖқ (NIV).
n2100153: tn The syntax of this line in Hebrew is intentionally redundant, e.g. (literally), вҖңIt is a grievous task [or вҖңunpleasant businessвҖқ] that God has given to the sons of man to be occupied with it.вҖқ The referent of the third masculine singular suffix on вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧўЦІЧ Ч•Ц№ЧӘ Ч‘ЦјЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (laКҝanot bo, вҖңto be occupied with itвҖқ) is вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·Чҹ ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (Кҝinyan raКҝ, вҖңa grievous task, a rotten businessвҖқ).
n2100154: tn Or вҖңthat busies them.вҖқ The verb II вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц·Ч”вҖҺ (Кҝanah, вҖңto be occupied withвҖқ) is related to the noun вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝinyan, вҖңbusiness, task, occupationвҖқ) which also occurs in this verse. The verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц·Ч”вҖҺ means вҖңto be occupied, to be busy withвҖқ (with the preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ, bet), e.g., Eccl 1:13; 3:10; 5:19 (HALOT 854 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ; BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ). The Hebrew verb is related to the Aramaic verb вҖңto try hard,вҖқ the Arabic verb вҖңto be busily occupied; to worry; to be a matter of concern,вҖқ and the Old South Arabic root вҖңto be troubled; to strive withвҖқ (HALOT 854).
n2100155: tn The phrase вҖңby manвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100157: tn As mentioned in the note on вҖңeverythingвҖқ in 1:2, the term вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (hakkol, вҖңeverythingвҖқ) is often limited in reference to the specific topic at hand in the context (e.g., BDB 482 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ 2). The argument of 1:12-15, like 1:3-11, focuses on secular human achievement. This is clear from the repetition of the root вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ (Кҝasah, вҖңdo, work, accomplish, achieveвҖқ) in 1:12-13.
n2100158: tn The phrase вҖңhe has accomplishedвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100159: tn This usage of вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) denotes вҖңfutile, profitless, fruitlessвҖқ (e.g., 2 Kgs 17:15; Ps 78:33; Prov 13:11; 21:6; Eccl 1:2, 14; 2:1, 14-15; 4:8; Jer 2:5; 10:3; Lam 4:17; see HALOT 236-37 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ; BDB 210-11 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). The term is used with the simile вҖңlike striving after the windвҖқ (вҖҸЧЁЦ°ЧўЧ•ЦјЧӘ ЧЁЧ•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ, reКҝut ruakh)вҖ”a graphic picture of an expenditure of effort in vain because no one can catch the wind by chasing it (e.g., 1:14, 17; 2:11, 17, 26; 4:4, 6, 16; 6:9; 7:14). When used in this sense, the term is often used with the following synonyms: вҖҸЧңЦ°ЧӘЦ№Ч”Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (letohu, вҖңfor nothing, in vain, for no reasonвҖқ; Isa 49:4); вҖҸЧЁЦҙЧҷЧ§вҖҺ (riq, вҖңprofitless; uselessвҖқ; Isa 30:7; Eccl 6:11); вҖҸЧңЦ№Чҗ Ч”Ч•Ц№ЧўЦҙЧҷЧңвҖҺ (вҖңworthless, profitlessвҖқ; Is 30:6; 57:12; Jer 16:19); вҖңwhat profit?вҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦ·Ч”ЦҫЧҷЦјЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹЦ№вҖҺ, mah yitron); and вҖңno profitвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЧҹ ЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ, en yitron; e.g., 2:11; 3:19; 6:9). It is also used in antithesis to terms connoting value: вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңgood, benefit, advantageвҖқ) and вҖҸЧҷЦ№ЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (yoteron, вҖңprofit, advantage, gainвҖқ). Despite everything that man has accomplished in history, it is ultimately futile because nothing on earth really changes.
n2100160: tn Heb вҖңstriving of wind.вҖқ The word вҖңlikeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text; it has been added in the translation to make the comparative notion clear.
n2100161: tn The term вҖҸЧһЦ°ЧўЦ»Ч•ЦјЦёЧӘвҖҺ, meКҝuvvat (Pual participle masculine singular from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ•Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ, Кҝavat, вҖңto bendвҖқ) is used substantively (вҖңwhat is bent; what is crookedвҖқ) in reference to irregularities in life and obstacles to human secular achievement accomplishing anything of ultimate value.
n2100162: tn A parallel statement occurs in 7:13 which employs the active form of вҖҸЧўЦёЧ•Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ, (Кҝavat, вҖңto bendвҖқ) with God as the subject: вҖңWho is able to strengthen what God bends?вҖқ The passive form occurs here: вҖңNo one is able to straighten what is bentвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦ°ЧўЦ»Ч•ЦјЦёЧӘ ЧңЦ№ЧҗЦҫЧҷЧ•ЦјЧӣЦ·Чң ЧңЦ№ЧӘЦ°Ч§Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ, meКҝuvvat loКҫ yukhal loteqon). In the light of 7:13, the personal agent of the passive form is God.
n2100163: tn The Hebrew noun вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧЎЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (khesron) is used in the OT only here and means вҖңwhat is lackingвҖқ (as an antonym to вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ [yitron], вҖңwhat is profitableвҖқ; HALOT 339 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧЎЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ; BDB 341 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧЎЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ). It is an Aramaic loanword meaning вҖңdeficit.вҖқ The related verb вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧЎЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (khasar) means вҖңto lack, to be in need of, to decrease, to lessen [in number]вҖқ; the related noun вҖҸЧ—Ц№ЧЎЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (khoser) refers to вҖңone in want ofвҖқ; and the noun вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧЎЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (kheser) means вҖңpoverty, wantвҖқ (HALOT 338 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧЎЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 341 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧЎЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ). It refers to what is absent (zero in terms of quantity) rather than what is deficient (poor in terms of quality). The LXX misunderstood the term and rendered it as бҪ‘ПғП„ОӯПҒО·ОјОұ (husterД“ma, вҖңdeficiencyвҖқ): вҖңdeficiency cannot be numbered.вҖқ It is also misunderstood by a few English versions: вҖңnor can you count up the defects in lifeвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңthe number of fools is infiniteвҖқ (Douay). However, most English versions correctly understand it as referring to what is lacking in terms of quantity: вҖңwhat is lackingвҖқ (RSV, MLB, NASB, NIV, NRSV), вҖңa lackвҖқ (NJPS), вҖңthat which is wantingвҖқ (KJV, ASV), вҖңwhat is not thereвҖқ (NEB), and вҖңwhat is missingвҖқ (NAB).
n2100164: tn Heb вҖңcannot be countedвҖқ or вҖңcannot be numbered.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ”ЦҙЧһЦјЦёЧ Ч•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (himmanot, Niphal infinitive construct from вҖҸЧһЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ, manah, вҖңto countвҖқ) is rendered literally by most translations: вҖң[cannot] be countedвҖқ or вҖң[cannot] be numberedвҖқ (KJV, ASV, RSV, MLB, NEB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, JPS, NJPS). However, the nuance вҖңcountвҖқ might function as a metonymy of effect for cause, that is, вҖңto supply.вҖқ What is absent cannot be supplied (cause) therefore, it cannot be counted as present (effect). NAB adopts this approach: вҖңwhat is missing cannot be supplied.вҖқ
n2100165: tn Heb вҖңI spoke, I, with my heart.вҖқ
n2100166: tn Heb вҖңI, look, I have made great and increased wisdom.вҖқ The expression вҖҸЧ”ЦҙЧ’Ц°Ч“ЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷ Ч•Ц°Ч”Ч•Ц№ЧЎЦ·ЧӨЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (higdalti vehosafti) is a verbal hendiadys; it means that Qoheleth had become the wisest man in the history of Jerusalem.
n2100167: tn The phrase вҖңwho ruledвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100169: tn Heb вҖңmy heartвҖқ (вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, libbi). The term вҖңheartвҖқ is a metonymy of part for the whole (вҖңmy heartвҖқ = myself).
n2100170: tn Heb вҖңMy heart has seen much wisdom and knowledge.вҖқ
n2100171: tn Heb вҖңgave my heart,вҖқ or вҖңset my mind.вҖқ See v. 13.
n2100172: tn The phrase вҖңthe benefit ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100173: tn The word вҖңoverвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100174: tn The terms вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦҙЧӣЦ°ЧңЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ (sikhlut, вҖңfollyвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ”Ч•Ц№ЧңЦөЧңЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (holelot, вҖңfoolishnessвҖқ) are synonyms. The term вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦҙЧӣЦ°ЧңЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ (alternate spelling of вҖҸЧЎЦҙЧӣЦ°ЧңЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ, sikhlut) refers to foolish behavior (HALOT 755 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЦҙЧӣЦ°ЧңЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ), while вҖҸЧ”Ч•Ц№ЧңЦөЧңЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ refers to foolish ideas and mental blindness (HALOT 242 s.v. вҖҸЧ”Ч•Ц№ЧңЦөЧңЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ). Qoheleth uses these terms to refer to foolish ideas and self-indulgent pleasures (e.g., Eccl 2:2-3, 12-14; 7:25; 9:3; 10:1, 6, 13).
n2100176: tn The term вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧқвҖҺ (gam, вҖңevenвҖқ) is a particle of association and emphasis (HALOT 195 s.v. вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧқвҖҺ).
n2100177: tn This term does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100178: tn This term does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100180: tn This term does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100181: tn This term does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100182: tn This term does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100183: tn This term does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n210021: tn Heb вҖңI said, I, in my heartвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЦёЧһЦ·ЧЁЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷ ЧҗЦІЧ ЦҙЧҷ Ч‘ЦјЦ°ЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, Кҫamarti Кҫani belibbi). The term вҖңheartвҖқ (вҖҸЧңЦөЧ‘вҖҺ, lev) is a synecdoche of part (вҖңheartвҖқ) for the whole (the whole person), and thus means вҖңI said to myselfвҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 648).
n210022: tn The Hebrew verb вҖҸЧңЦ°ЧӣЦёЧ”вҖҺ (lekhah, вҖңCome!вҖқ) is a weakened imperative, used merely as an introductory word, e.g., Gen 19:32; 31:44; Judg 19:11; 1 Sam 9:9-10; 11:14; 2 Kgs 3:7; Ps 66:5; Song 7:12; Isa 1:18; 2:3; Mic 4:2 (HALOT 246 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧңЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ 2; BDB 234 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧңЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ I.5.f.2). Whenever вҖҸЧңЦ°ЧӣЦёЧ”вҖҺ introduces an exhortation, it functions as an invitation to the audience to adopt a course of action that will be beneficial to the addressee or mutually beneficial to both the speaker and the addressee. Here, Qoheleth personifies his вҖңheartвҖқ (вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, libbi) and addresses himself. The examination of self-indulgent pleasure is designed to be beneficial to Qoheleth.
n210023: tn Or вҖңtest.вҖқ The cohortative вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ Ц·ЧЎЦјЦ°ЧӣЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҫanassekhah) emphasizes the resolve of the speaker. The term вҖҸЧ ЦёЧЎЦ·Ч”вҖҺ (nasah, вҖңto testвҖқ) means вҖңto conduct a test,вҖқ that is, to conduct an experiment (Judg 6:39; Eccl 2:1; 7:23; Dan 1:12, 14; see HALOT 702 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЧЎЧ”вҖҺ 3; BDB 650 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЦёЧЎЦёЧ”вҖҺ 1). The verb вҖҸЧ ЦёЧЎЦ·Ч”вҖҺ is often used as a synonym with вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧ—Ц·ЧҹвҖҺ (bakhan, вҖңto examineвҖқ; BDB 103 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧ—Ц·ЧҹвҖҺ and 650 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЦёЧЎЦёЧ”вҖҺ 1) and вҖҸЧңЦёЧ“Ц·ЧўЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ (ladaКҝat, вҖңto ascertainвҖқ; Deut 8:2).
n210024:
tn Heb вҖңI will test you with pleasure.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦҙЧһЦ°Ч—Ц·Ч”вҖҺ (simkhah, вҖңpleasureвҖқ) has a two-fold range of meanings: (1) it can refer to the legitimate enjoyment of life that Qoheleth affirms is good (5:17; 8:15; 9:7; 11:8, 9) and that God gives to those who please him (2:26; 5:19); or (2) it can refer to foolish pleasure, self-indulgent, frivolous merrymaking (2:1, 2; 7:4). The parallelism in 2:2 between вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦҙЧһЦ°Ч—Ц·Ч”вҖҺ and вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч—Ч•Ц№Ч§вҖҺ (sekhoq, вҖңlaughter, frivolous merrymakingвҖқ), which always appears in the context of banqueting, drinking, and merrymaking, suggests that the pejorative sense is in view in this context.
sn The statement I will try self-indulgent pleasure is a figurative expression known as metonymy of association. As 2:1-3 makes clear, it is not so much Qoheleth who is put to the test with pleasure, but rather that pleasure is put to the test by Qoheleth.
n210025: tn Heb вҖңSee what is good!вҖқ The volitive sequence of the cohortative (вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ Ц·ЧЎЦјЦ°ЧӣЦёЧ”вҖҺ, Кҫanassekhah, вҖңI will test youвҖқ) followed by vav + imperative (вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦөЧ”вҖҺ, ureКҫeh, вҖңand see!вҖқ) denotes purpose/result: вҖңI will test youвҖҰin order to seeвҖҰ.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҫah, вҖңto seeвҖқ) has a broad range of meanings (e.g., in the Qal stem 16 categories are listed in HALOT 1157-1160 s.v.). In this context it means вҖңto discover; to perceive; to discern; to understandвҖқ (HALOT 1159 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЧҗЧ”вҖҺ 13; BDB 907 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ 5).
n210026: sn The phrase вҖңto see what is goodвҖқ (вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ, raКҫah, вҖңto seeвҖқ plus вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ, tov, вҖңgoodвҖқ) is repeated twice in 2:1-3. This is the key phrase in this section of Ecclesiastes. Qoheleth sought to discover (вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ) whether merry-making offered any value (вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ) to mankind.
n210027: tn The particle вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”ЦҙЧ ЦјЦөЧ”вҖҺ (vehinneh, literally вҖңBehold!вҖқ) occurs after verbs of perception to introduce what was seen, understood or discovered (HALOT 252 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦҙЧ ЦјЦөЧ”вҖҺ 8). It is used to make the narrative graphic and vivid, enabling the reader to enter into the surprise of the speaker (BDB 244 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦҙЧ ЦјЦөЧ”вҖҺ c). This is an example of the heterosis of the deictic particle (вҖңBehold!вҖқ) for a verb of perception (вҖңI foundвҖқ). See E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 510-34.
n210028: tn This use of вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) denotes вҖңfutile, worthless, fruitless, pointlessвҖқ (HALOT 237 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ 2; BDB 210-11 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ 2). It is a synonym to вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч”Ч•Ц№ЧңЦёЧңвҖҺ (meholal, вҖңfollyвҖқ) in 2:2a and an antonym to вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңworthwhile, beneficialвҖқ) in 2:1b and 2:3c.
n210029: tn Heb вҖңlaughter.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч—Ч•Ц№Ч§вҖҺ (sekhoq, вҖңlaughterвҖқ) has a fourfold range of meanings: (1) вҖңjoyful laughterвҖқ (Ps 126:2; Prov 14:13; Job 8:21); (2) вҖңfrivolous laughter, merrymakingвҖқ (Eccl 2:2; 7:3, 6); (3) вҖңpleasure, sportвҖқ (Prov 10:23; Eccl 10:19); and (4) вҖңderision, mockery, laughingstockвҖқ (Jer 20:7; 48:26, 27, 39; Job 12:4; Lam 3:14). See HALOT 1315 s.v вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч—Ч•Ц№Ч§вҖҺ; BDB 966 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч—Ц№Ч§вҖҺ. In Ecclesiastes, вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч—Ч•Ц№Ч§вҖҺ is always used in contexts of self-indulgent banqueting, drinking, frivolous partying and merrymaking (Eccl 2:2; 7:3, 6; 10:19). It is distinct from вҖңhealthyвҖқ joy and laughter (Ps 126:2; Job 8:21). The connotation of вҖңfrivolous merrymakingвҖқ fits this context best.
n2100210: tn The term вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦҙЧһЦ°Ч—ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (simkhah, вҖңpleasureвҖқ) has a two-fold range of meanings in Ecclesiastes: (1) it can refer to the enjoyment of life that Qoheleth affirms is good (5:17; 8:15; 9:7; 11:8, 9) and that God gives to those who are pleasing to him (2:26; 5:19); and (2) it can refer to foolish pleasure, that is, frivolous merrymaking (2:1, 2; 7:4). The parallelism between вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦҙЧһЦ°Ч—ЦёЧ”вҖҺ and вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч—Ч•Ц№Ч§вҖҺ (sekhoq, вҖңlaughter, frivolous merrymakingвҖқ) in 2:2 suggests that the pejorative sense is in view here.
n2100211: tn Heb вҖңWhat does it accomplish?вҖқ The rhetorical question вҖңWhat does it accomplish?вҖқ expects a negative answer: вҖңIt accomplishes nothing!вҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949-51). See, e.g., Gen 1:19; 18:14, 17; Deut 7:17; 1 Sam 2:25; Job 40:2; Pss 56:7 [8]; 90:11; 94:16; 106:2; Eccl 3:21.
n2100212:
tn Heb вҖңIn my heart I explored.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ (tur, вҖңto seek out, to spy out, to exploreвҖқ) is used in the OT to describe: (1) the physical activity of вҖңspying outвҖқ or вҖңexploringвҖқ geographical locations (Num 13:2, 16, 17, 21, 25, 32; 14:6, 7, 34, 36, 38; Job 39:8) and (2) the mental activity of вҖңexploringвҖқ or вҖңexaminingвҖқ a course of action or the effects of an action (Eccl 1:13; 2:3; 7:25; 9:1). See BDB 1064 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ 2; HALOT 1708 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ. It was used as a synonym with вҖҸЧ“ЦёЧЁЦёЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (darash, вҖңto studyвҖқ) in 1:13: вҖңI devoted myself to study (вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ“Ц°ЧЁЧ•Ц№Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ, lidrosh) and to explore (вҖҸЧңЦёЧӘЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ, latur).вҖқ
sn As the repetition of the term вҖҸЧңЦөЧ‘вҖҺ (lev, вҖңheartвҖқ or вҖңmindвҖқ) indicates (2:1, 3), this experiment appears to have been only an intellectual exercise or a cognitive reflection: вҖңI said to myself (Heb вҖңin my heart [or вҖңmindвҖқ],вҖқ 2:1); вҖңI explored with my mind (Heb вҖңheart,вҖқ 2:3a); and вҖңmy mind (Heb вҖңheartвҖқ) guiding me with wisdomвҖқ (2:3b). Qoheleth himself did not indulge in drunkenness, but he contemplated the value of self-indulgence in his mind.
n2100213: tn The phrase вҖңthe effects ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100214: tn Or вҖңI sought to cheer my flesh with wine.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧңЦҙЧһЦ°Ч©ЧҒЧ•Ц№ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (limshokh, Qal infinitive construct from вҖҸЧһЦёЧ©ЧҒЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ, mashakh, вҖңto draw, pullвҖқ) functions in a complementary sense with the preceding verb вҖҸЧӘЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ (tur вҖңto examineвҖқ): Heb вҖңI sought to draw out my flesh with wineвҖқ or вҖңI [mentally] explored [the effects] of drawing out my flesh with wine.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧһЦёЧ©ЧҒЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ means вҖңto draw, to drag along, to leadвҖқ (BDB 604 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦёЧ©ЧҒЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ) or вҖңto draw out; to stretch out [to full length]; to drag; to pull; to seize; to carry off; to pull; to goвҖқ (HALOT 645-46 s.v. вҖҸЧһЧ©ЧҒЧҡвҖҺ). BDB suggests that this use be nuanced вҖңto draw, to attract, to gratifyвҖқ the flesh, that is, вҖңto cheerвҖқ (BDB 604 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦёЧ©ЧҒЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ 7). While this meaning is not attested elsewhere in the OT, it is found in Mishnaic Hebrew: вҖңto attractвҖқ (Qal), e.g., вҖңit is different with heresy, because it attracts [i.e., persuades, offers inducements]вҖқ (b. Avodah Zarah 27b) and вҖңto be attracted, carried away, seduced,вҖқ e.g., вҖңhe was drawn after them, he indulged in the luxuries of the palaceвҖқ (b. Shabbat 147b). See Jastrow 853-54 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч©ЧӮЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ. Here it denotes вҖңto stretch; to draw out [to full length],вҖқ that is, вҖңto revive; to restoreвҖқ the body (HALOT 646 s.v. вҖҸЧһЧ©ЧҒЧ“вҖҺ [sic] 3). The statement is a metonymy of cause (i.e., indulging the flesh with wine) for effect (i.e., the effects of self-indulgence).
n2100215: tn Heb вҖңmy flesh.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°Ч©ЧӮЦёЧЁЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (besari, вҖңmy fleshвҖқ) may function as a synecdoche of part (i.e., flesh) for the whole (i.e., whole person). See E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 642. One could translate, вҖңI sought to cheer myself.вҖқ
n2100216: tn The phrase вҖңall the whileвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100217: tn The word вҖңmeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100218: tn Heb вҖңand my heart was leading along in wisdom.вҖқ The vav + noun, вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (velibbi) introduces a disjunctive, parenthetical clause designed to qualify the speakerвҖҷs remarks lest he be misunderstood: вҖңNow my heart/mindвҖҰ.вҖқ He emphasizes that he never lost control of his senses in this process. It was a purely mental, cognitive endeavor; he never actually gave himself over to wanton self-indulgence in wine or folly.
n2100219: tn The phrase вҖңthe effects ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100220: tn Heb вҖңembracing folly.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ—ЦёЧ–вҖҺ (Кҫakhaz, вҖңto embraceвҖқ) is normally used to describe the physical action of taking hold of an object. Here is it is used metaphorically to describe a personвҖҷs choice of lifestyle, that is, adopting a particular course of moral conduct (e.g., Job 17:9); see HALOT 31-32 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЧ—Ч–вҖҺ; BDB 28 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ—Ц·Ч–вҖҺ.
n2100221: tn Or вҖңuntil.вҖқ The construction вҖҸЧўЦ·Ч“ ЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (Кҝad Кҫasher, вҖңuntilвҖқ) introduces a temporal result clause (e.g., Gen 27:44; 28:15; Num 21:35; Isa 6:11); see HALOT 787 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЦ·Ч“вҖҺ B.b. With an imperfect verb (such as вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ, КҫerКҫeh, Qal imperfect first common singular from вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ, raКҫah, вҖңto seeвҖқ), the compound construction вҖҸЧўЦ·Ч“ ЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ usually refers to future time (Gen 27:44; 29:8; Exod 23:30; 24:14; Lev 22:4; Num 11:20; 20:17; 1 Sam 22:3; Hos 5:15), but it also rarely refers to past time (Jonah 4:5; Eccl 2:3); see BDB 725 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЦ·Ч“вҖҺ II.1.a.b. JoГјon 2:370 В§113.k notes that when the compound construction вҖҸЧўЦ·Ч“ ЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ is occasionally used with an imperfect depicting past action to denote a virtual nuance of purpose: вҖңuntilвҖқ = вҖңso that,вҖқ e.g., Jonah 4:5; Eccl 2:3.
n2100222: tn Heb вҖңI might see where is the good?вҖқ The interrogative particle вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷвҖҺ (Кҫe, вҖңwhere?вҖқ) used with the demonstrative pronoun вҖҸЧ–Ц¶Ч”вҖҺ (zeh, вҖңthisвҖқ) forms an idiom: вҖңwhere [then]?вҖқ (HALOT 37-38 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷвҖҺ 2.a; see, e.g., 1 Sam 9:18; 1 Kgs 13:12; 2 Kgs 3:8; Isa 50:1; 66:1; Jer 6:16; Job 28:12, 20; 38:19, 24; Esth 7:5). The phrase вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЦҫЧ–Ц¶Ч” ЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (Кҫe zeh tov) is an indirect question that literally means, вҖңWhere is the good?вҖқ that is, вҖңwhat good?вҖқ (HALOT 38 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷвҖҺ 2.d).
n2100225: tn Heb вҖңnumber of the days.вҖқ The Hebrew noun вҖҸЧһЦҙЧЎЦ°ЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (mispar, вҖңnumber, quantityвҖқ) sometimes means вҖңfewвҖқ (e.g., Gen 34:30; Num 9:20; Deut 4:27; 33:6; Isa 10:19; Jer 44:28; Ezek 12:16; Ps 105:12; Job 16:22; 1 Chr 16:19); see HALOT 607 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦҙЧЎЦ°ЧӨЦјЦёЧЁвҖҺ 2.b; BDB 709 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦҙЧЎЦ°ЧӨЦјЦёЧЁвҖҺ 1.a. This phrase is an idiom that means, вҖңduring all their livesвҖқ (BDB 709 s.v.), вҖңduring their total [short] time of life,вҖқ that is, вҖңas long as they liveвҖқ (HALOT 608 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦҙЧЎЦ°ЧӨЦјЦёЧЁвҖҺ 3.d). Ecclesiastes often emphasizes the brevity of life (e.g., 5:17; 6:12; 9:9). The LXX rendered вҖҸЧһЦҙЧЎЦ°ЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ in a woodenly literal sense: бјҖПҒО№ОёОјПҢОҪ (arithmon, вҖңthe number [of days of their lives]вҖқ). Several English translations adopt a similar approach: вҖңall the days of their lifeвҖқ (ASV, Douay) and вҖңthe number of days of their livesвҖқ (YLT). However, this idiom is handled well by a number of English translations: вҖңduring the few days of their livesвҖқ (RSV, NRSV, NASB, NIV, Moffatt, NJPS), вҖңduring the limited days of their lifeвҖқ (NAB), and вҖңthroughout the brief span of their livesвҖқ (NEB).
n2100226:
tn Or вҖңmy worksвҖқ; or вҖңmy accomplishments.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦёЧҷвҖҺ (maКҝasay, вҖңmy worksвҖқ) has been handled in two basic ways: (1) great works or projects, and (2) possessions. The latter assumes a metonymy, oneвҖҷs effort standing for the possessions it produces. Both interpretations are reflected in the major English translations: вҖңworksвҖқ (KJV, NEB, NAB, ASV, NASB, MLB, RSV, Douay, Moffatt), вҖңprojectsвҖқ (NIV), and вҖңpossessionsвҖқ (NJPS).
sn This section (2:4-11) is unified and bracketed by the repetition of the verb вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦёЧ“Ц·ЧңвҖҺ (gadal, вҖңto increaseвҖқ) which occurs at the beginning (2:4) and end (2:9), and by the repetition of the root вҖҸЧўЧ©Ч”вҖҺ (noun: вҖңworksвҖқ and verb: вҖңto do, make, acquireвҖқ) which occurs throughout the section (2:4, 5, 6, 8, 11).
n2100227: sn The expression for myself is repeated eight times in 2:4-8 to emphasize that Qoheleth did not deny himself any acquisition. He indulged himself in acquiring everything he desired. His vast resources as king allowed him the unlimited opportunity to indulge himself. He could have anything his heart desired, and he did.
n2100229: tn The term does not refer here to vegetable gardens, but to orchards (cf. the next line). In the same way the so-called вҖңgardenвҖқ of Eden was actually an orchard filled with fruit trees. See Gen 2:8-9.
n2100230: tn The noun вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦөЧЎвҖҺ (pardes, вҖңgarden, parkland, forestвҖқ) is a foreign loanword that occurs only 3 times in biblical Hebrew (Song 4:13; Eccl 2:5; Neh 2:8). The original Old Persian term pairidaeza designated the enclosed parks and pleasure-grounds that were the exclusive domain of the Persian kings and nobility (HALOT 963 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦөЧЎвҖҺ; LSJ 1308 s.v ПҖОұПҒО¬ОҙОөО№ПғОҝПӮ). The related Babylonian term pardesu вҖңmarvelous gardenвҖқ referred to the enclosed parks of the kings (AHw 2:833 and 3:1582). The term passed into Greek as ПҖОұПҒО¬ОҙОөО№ПғОҝПӮ (paradeisos, вҖңenclosed park, pleasure-groundвҖқ), referring to the enclosed parks and gardens of the Persian kings (LSJ 1308). The Greek term has been transliterated into English as вҖңparadise.вҖқ
n2100231: tn Heb вҖңto water from them a groveвҖқ (or вҖңforest).
n2100232: tn The phrase вҖңsons of a houseвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘Ц°Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч‘Ц·ЧҷЦҙЧӘвҖҺ, vene vayit) appears to be parallel to вҖңa son of my houseвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘Ц¶ЧҹЦҫЧ‘ЦјЦөЧҷЧӘЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, ven beti) which refers to a person born into slavery from male and female servants in the masterвҖҷs possession, e.g., Eleazar of Damascus (Gen 15:3). The phrase appears to denote children born from male and female slaves already in his possession, that is, вҖңhomeborn slavesвҖқ (NASB) or вҖңother slaves who were born in my houseвҖқ (NIV). Apparently confusing the sense of the phrase with the referent of the phrase in Gen 15:3, NJPS erroneously suggests вҖңstewardsвҖқ in Eccl 2:7.
n2100234: tn The term вҖҸЧЎЦ°Ч’Ц»ЧңЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (segullah) denotes вҖңpersonal propertyвҖқ (HALOT 742 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЦ°Ч’Ц»ЧңЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ 1) or вҖңvalued property, personal treasureвҖқ (BDB 688 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЦ°Ч’Ц»ЧңЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2). Elsewhere, it refers to a kingвҖҷs silver and gold (1 Chr 27:3). It is related to Akkadian sug/kullu вҖңflockвҖқ (AHw 2:1053-54) and sikiltu вҖңprivate property [belonging to the king]вҖқ (AHw 2:1041). The term refers to the personal, private and valued possessions of kings, which do not pass into the hands of the state.
n2100235: tn Heb вҖңof kings and provinces.вҖқ This personal treasure was taken as tribute from other kings and governors. See T. Longman III, Ecclesiastes (NICOT), 92.
n2100236: tn Heb вҖңand sensual delights of the sons of man.вҖқ The noun вҖҸЧӘЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧ Ч•ЦјЧ’вҖҺ (taКҝanug) has a three-fold range of meanings: (1) вҖңluxury; comfortвҖқ (Mic 2:9; Prov 19:10; Sir 6:28; 11:27; 14:16; 37:29; 41:1); (2) вҖңpleasure; delightвҖқ of sexual love (Song 7:7); and (3) вҖңdaintiness; feminineвҖқ (Mic 1:16); see HALOT 1769 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧ Ч•ЦјЧ’вҖҺ; BDB 772 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧ Ч•ЦјЧ’вҖҺ. The related adjective вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц№Ч’вҖҺ (Кҝanog, вҖңpampered; daintyвҖқ) is used to describe a pampered woman (Deut 28:56), to personify Babylon as a delicate woman (Isa 47:1), and to ridicule delicate men (Deut 28:54); see HALOT 851 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№ЦёЧ Ц№Ч’вҖҺ; BDB 772 вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц№Ч’вҖҺ. It is related to the noun вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч Ц¶Ч’вҖҺ (Кҝoneg, вҖңpleasure; exquisite delight; daintinessвҖқ; see HALOT 851 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч Ц¶Ч’вҖҺ; BDB 772 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч Ц¶Ч’вҖҺ) and the verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц№Ч’вҖҺ which means вҖңto be soft; to be delicateвҖқ and вҖңpleasurableвҖқ (Pual) and вҖңto pamper oneselfвҖқ and вҖңto take delight or pleasure inвҖқ (HALOT 851 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ Ч’вҖҺ; BDB 772 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц№Ч’вҖҺ). The root вҖҸЧўЧ Ч’вҖҺ is paralleled with вҖҸЧЁЦ№ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (rokh, Deut 28:56), вҖҸЧЁЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (rakh, Deut 28:54), and вҖҸЧЁЦ·ЧӣЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (rakkah, Deut 28:56) with the meanings вҖңdelicate; soft; tender; weak; coddled; pampered.вҖқ The context of Eccl 2:4-11 suggests that it denotes either вҖңluxuryвҖқ as in вҖңthe luxuries of commonersвҖқ (NJPS) or вҖңpleasure; delightвҖқ as in вҖңthe delights of menвҖқ (KJV, NASB, NIV). Part of the difficulty in determining the meaning of this term is caused by the ambiguity in meaning of its referent, namely, the appositional phrase вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЦёЧ” Ч•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (shiddah veshiddot), the meaning of which is uncertain (see the note on the phrase вҖңa harem of beautiful concubinesвҖқ at the end of this verse).
n2100237:
tn The meaning of the superlative construction вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЦёЧ” Ч•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (shiddah veshiddot) is uncertain because the term вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (shiddah) occurs only here in the OT. There are four basic approaches to the phrase: (1) Most scholars suggest that it refers to a royal harem and that it is in apposition to вҖңthe sensual delights of manвҖқ (вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦ·ЧўЦІЧ Ч•ЦјЧ’Ц№ЧӘ Ч‘ЦјЦ°Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ, vetaКҝanugot bene haКҫadam). There are four variations of this approach: (a) There is a possible connection to the Ugaritic sht вҖңmistress, ladyвҖқ and the Arabic sitt вҖңladyвҖқ (HALOT 1420 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ). (b) German scholars relate it to Assyrian sadadu вҖңloveвҖқ (Delitzsch, Konig, Wildeboer, Siegfried); however, BDB questions this connection (BDB 994 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ“Ч”вҖҺ). (c) Ibn Ezra relates it to II вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ·Ч“вҖҺ (shad) вҖңplunder; spoilвҖқ or вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ“Ч”вҖҺ вҖң[women] taken by violence,вҖқ and suggests that it refers to the occupants of the royal harem. (d) BDB connects it to the Hebrew noun I вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ·Ч“вҖҺ (shad, вҖңbreastвҖқ; e.g., Isa 28:9; Ezek 16:7; 23:3, 21, 34; Hos 2:4; 9:14; Song 1:13; 4:5; 7:4, 8, 9; 8:1, 8, 10; Job 3:12) adding that вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ“Ч”вҖҺ is related to the cognate Arabic and Aramaic roots meaning вҖңbreastвҖқ (BDB 994 s.v.). This would be a synecdoche of part (i.e., breast) for the whole (i.e., woman), similar to the idiom вҖңone womb, two wombsвҖқ (вҖҸЧЁЦ·Ч—Ц·Чқ ЧЁЦ·Ч—ЦІЧһЦёЧӘЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧқвҖҺ, rakham rakhamatayim) where вҖңwombвҖқ = woman (Judg 5:30). This is the approach taken by most English versions: вҖңmany concubinesвҖқ (NASB, RSV, NRSV), вҖңa wife and wivesвҖқ (YLT), вҖңmistresses galoreвҖқ (MLB), вҖңmany a mistressвҖқ (Moffatt), and вҖңa haremвҖқ (NIV). This is the approach suggested by the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project: вҖңune femme et des femmesвҖқ = one or two women (e.g., Judg 5:30); see D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:566. (2) The NJPS connects it to the Mishnaic Hebrew noun вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ which became вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧ“ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (вҖңa strong box, chestвҖқ; Jastrow 1558 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧ“ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ) and renders the phrase вҖңcoffers and coffers of themвҖқ in apposition to the phrase вҖңthe luxuries of commonersвҖқ (вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦ·ЧўЦІЧ Ч•ЦјЧ’Ц№ЧӘ Ч‘ЦјЦ°Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ). (3) KJV and ASV take the phrase in apposition to вҖңmale and female singersвҖқ and translate it as вҖңmusical instruments.вҖқ However, there is no known Hebrew term that would justify this approach. (4) The LXX related the term to the Aramaic root вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ“ЧҗвҖҺ (вҖңto pour out [wine]вҖқ) and rendered the phrase as Оҝбј°ОҪОҝПҮПҢОҝОҪ ОәОұбҪ¶ Оҝбј°ОҪОҝПҮПҢОұПӮ (oinochoon kai oinochoas), вҖңa male-butler and female cupbearers.вҖқ Aquila took a similar approach: ОәП…О»ОҜОәО№ОҝОҪ ОәОұбҪ¶ ОәП…О»ОҜОәО№Оұ (kulikion kai kulikia), вҖңwine cups and wine vessels.вҖқ This is reflected in the Vulgate and Douay: вҖңcups and vessels to serve to pour out wine.вҖқ Although the semantic meaning of the term вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЦёЧ” Ч•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (вҖңa breast and breastsвҖқ) is uncertain, the grammatical/syntactical form of the phrase is straightforward: (1) It is in apposition to the preceding line, вҖңthe delights of the son of menвҖқ (вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦ·ЧўЦІЧ Ч•ЦјЧ’Ц№ЧӘ Ч‘ЦјЦ°Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ). (2) The phrase is a superlative construction. When the second word is plural and it follows a noun from the same root which is singular, it indicates the best or most outstanding example of the person or thing so described. In addition to the Judg 5:30 parallel cited above, see the expression вҖңa generation, generationsвҖқ in Pss 72:5; 102:25; Isa 51:8. Unlike, Eccl 2:8, this juxtapositioning of the singular and plural to express the superlative usually involves a construct form. See вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч“Ц¶Ч©ЧҒ Ч”Ц·Ч§ЦјЦіЧ“ЦёЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (qodesh haqqodashim, вҖңthe holy of holies,вҖқ i.e., the most holy placeвҖқ; Exod 26:33), вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧЁ Ч”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦҙЧҷЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (shir hashirim, вҖңthe song of songs,вҖқ i.e., вҖңthe most excellent songвҖқ; Song 1:1), вҖҸЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦөЧҷ Ч”ЦёЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦҙЧҷЧқ Ч•Ц·ЧҗЦІЧ“Ц№Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч”Ц·ЧҗЦІЧ“Ц№Ч ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (Кҫelohe haКҫelohim vaКҫadone haКҫadonim, вҖңthe God of gods and Lord of lords,вҖқ i.e., вҖңthe Highest God and the Supreme LordвҖқ; Deut 10:17), and вҖҸЧўЦ¶Ч‘Ц¶Ч“ ЧўЦІЧ‘ЦёЧ“ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (Кҝeved Кҝavadim, вҖңa slave of slaves,вҖқ i.e., вҖңthe most abject slaveвҖқ; Gen 9:25). See GKC 431 В§133.i; R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 17-18, В§80; IBHS 154 В§9.5.3j. If the semantic meaning of the terms вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЦёЧ” Ч•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦҙЧ“ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ denotes вҖңa breast (among) breastsвҖқ or вҖңa lady (among) ladiesвҖқ (Eccl 2:8, but see the previous note on the phrase вҖңa manвҖҷs sensual delightsвҖқ), the superlative construction may connote вҖңthe most beautiful breastsвҖқ (metonymy of part for the whole) or вҖңthe most beautiful woman.вҖқ This might refer to a harem of concubines or to one woman (the wife of the king?) who was the most beautiful woman in the land.
sn Concubines were slave women in ancient Near Eastern societies who were the legal property of their master, but who could have legitimate sexual relations with their master. A concubineвҖҷs status was more elevated than a mere servant, but she was not free and did not have the legal rights of a free wife. The children of a concubine could, in some instances, become equal heirs with the children of the free wife. After the period of the Judges concubines may have become more of a royal prerogative (2 Sam 21:10-14; 1 Kgs 11:3).
n2100238: tn The vav prefixed to вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч’ЦёЧ“Ц·ЧңЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (vegadalti, vav plus Qal perfect first common singular from вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦёЧ“Ц·ЧңвҖҺ, gadal, вҖңto be great; to increaseвҖқ) functions in a final summarizing sense, that is, it introduces the concluding summary of 2:4-9.
n2100239: tn Heb вҖңI became great and I surpassedвҖқ (вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”Ч•Ц№ЧЎЦ·ЧӨЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч’ЦёЧ“Ц·ЧңЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, vegadalti vehosafti). This is a verbal hendiadys in which the second verb functions adverbially, modifying the first: вҖңI became far greater.вҖқ Most translations miss the hendiadys and render the line in a woodenly literal sense (KJV, ASV, RSV, NEB, NRSV, NAB, NASB, MLB, Moffatt), while only a few recognize the presence of hendiadys here: вҖңI became greater by farвҖқ (NIV) and вҖңI gained moreвҖқ (NJPS).
n2100240: tn Heb вҖңyet my wisdom stood for me,вҖқ meaning he retained his wise perspective despite his great wealth.
n2100241: tn Heb вҖңall which my eyes asked for, I did not withhold from them.вҖқ
n2100242: tn Heb вҖңI did not refuse my heart any pleasure.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (libbi, вҖңmy heartвҖқ) is a synecdoche of part (i.e., heart) for the whole (i.e., whole person); see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 648. The term is repeated twice in 2:10 for emphasis.
n2100243: tn Heb вҖңSo my heart was joyful from all my toil.вҖқ
n2100244: tn Heb вҖңand this was my portion from all my toil.вҖқ
n2100245: tn Heb вҖңall my works that my hands had done.вҖқ
n2100246: tn Heb вҖңand all the toil with which I had toiled in doing it.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧўЦёЧһЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (Кҝamal, вҖңtoilвҖқ) is repeated to emphasize the burden and weariness of the labor which Qoheleth exerted in his accomplishments.
n2100248: tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (hakkol, вҖңeverythingвҖқ or вҖңallвҖқ) must be qualified and limited in reference to the topic that is dealt with in 2:4-11. This is an example of synecdoche of general for the specific; the general term вҖңallвҖқ is used only in reference to the topic at hand. This is clear from the repetition of вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (kol, вҖңeverythingвҖқ) and (вҖңall these thingsвҖқ) in 2:11.
n2100249: tn The phrase вҖңachievements and possessionsвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in translation for clarity.
n2100250: tn The term вҖңultimatelyвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100251: tn The parallelism with вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (yitron), вҖңprofit; advantage; gainвҖқ) indicates that вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) should be nuanced as вҖңprofitless, fruitless, futileвҖқ in this context. While labor offers some relative and temporal benefits, such as material acquisitions and the enjoyment of the work of oneвҖҷs hands, there is no ultimate benefit to be gained from secular human achievement.
n2100252: tn The noun вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (yitron, вҖңprofitвҖқ) has a two-fold range of meanings: (1) вҖңwhat comes of [something]; resultвҖқ (Eccl 1:3; 2:11; 3:9; 5:8, 15; 7:12; 10:10) and (2) вҖңprofit; advantageвҖқ (Eccl 2:13; 10:11); see HALOT 452-53 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҷвҖҺ. It is derived from the noun вҖҸЧҷЦ¶ЧӘЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (yeter, вҖңwhat is left behind; remainderвҖқ; HALOT 452 s.v. I вҖҸЧҷЦ¶ЧӘЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ). The related verb вҖҸЧҷЦёЧӘЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (yatar) denotes вҖңto be left over; to surviveвҖқ (Niphal) and вҖңto have left overвҖқ (Hiphil); see HALOT 451-52 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЧӘЧЁвҖҺ. When used literally, вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ refers to what is left over after expenses (gain or profit); when used figuratively, it refers to what is advantageous or of benefit. Though some things have relative advantage over others (e.g., light over darkness, and wisdom over folly in 2:13), there is no ultimate profit in manвҖҷs labor due to death.
n2100253: tn The phrase вҖңfrom themвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100256: sn See 1:17 for the same expression. Throughout 2:1-11, Qoheleth evaluated the merits of merrymaking (2:1-3), accomplishing grand things (2:4-6), amassing great wealth (2:7-8), and secular acquisitions and accomplishments (2:9-10). Now, he reflects on the benefit in life in living wisely and not giving oneself over to frivolous self-indulgence.
n2100257: tc The Hebrew text reads вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЧ•ЦјЧ”Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (Кҝasuhu, вҖңthey have done itвҖқ; Qal perfect third person masculine plural from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ [Кҝasah] plus third person masculine singular suffix). However, many medieval Hebrew mss read вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (Кҝasahu, вҖңhe has doneвҖқ; Qal perfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ), reflected in the LXX and Syriac. The error was caused by dittography (вҖҸЧ•вҖҺ, vav, written twice) or by orthographic confusion between вҖҸЧ•вҖҺ and вҖҸЧ”вҖҺ (hey) in вҖҸЧ”Ч•Ч•вҖҺ (confused as вҖҸЧ•Ч”Ч•Ч•вҖҺ) at the end of 2:12 and beginning of 2:13. The third person masculine singular referent of вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЧ•ЦјЧ”Ч•ЦјвҖҺ вҖңwhat he has doneвҖқ is the king, that is, Qoheleth himself. The referent (the king) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100258: tn Heb вҖңand I saw that there is profit for wisdom more than folly.вҖқ
n2100259: tn Heb вҖңhas his eyes in his head.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝayin, вҖңeyeвҖқ) is used figuratively in reference to mental and spiritual faculties (BDB 744 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧҹвҖҺ 3.a). The term вҖңeyeвҖқ is a metonymy of cause (eye) for effect (sight and perception).
n2100260: sn The common fate to which Qoheleth refers is death.
n2100261: tn The term вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ»ЧңЦјЦёЧқвҖҺ (kullam, вҖңall of themвҖқ) denotes вҖңboth of them.вҖқ This is an example of synecdoche of general (вҖңall of themвҖқ) for the specific (вҖңboth of them,вҖқ that is, both the wise man and the fool).
n2100262: tn The emphatic use of the first person common singular personal pronoun вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҫani, вҖңmeвҖқ) with the emphatic particle of association вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧқвҖҺ (gam, вҖңeven, as well asвҖқ; HALOT 195-96 s.v. вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧқвҖҺ) appears to emphasize the first person common singular suffix on вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧ§Ц°ЧЁЦөЧ ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (yiqreni) вҖңit will befall [or вҖңhappen toвҖқ] meвҖқ (Qal imperfect third person masculine singular plus first person common singular suffix from вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧЁЦёЧ”вҖҺ, qarah, вҖңto befall; to happen toвҖқ); see GKC 438 В§135.e. Qoheleth laments not that the fate of the wise man is the same as that of the fool, but that even he himselfвҖ”the wisest man of allвҖ”would fare no better in the end than the most foolish.
n2100263: tn The adjective вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧӘЦөЧЁвҖҺ (yoter) means вҖңtoo much; excessive,вҖқ e.g., 7:16 вҖңexcessively righteousвҖқ (HALOT 404 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧӘЦөЧЁвҖҺ 2; BDB 452 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧӘЦөЧЁвҖҺ). It is derived from the root вҖҸЧҷЦ¶ЧӘЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (yeter, вҖңwhat is left overвҖқ); see HALOT 452 s.v. I вҖҸЧҷЦ¶ЧӘЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ. It is related to the verbal root вҖҸЧҷЧӘЧЁвҖҺ (Niphal вҖңto be left overвҖқ; Hiphil вҖңto have left overвҖқ); see HALOT 451-52 s.v. I вҖҸЧҷЧӘЧЁвҖҺ. The adjective is related to вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (yitron, вҖңadvantage; profitвҖқ) which is a key-term in this section, creating a word-play: The wise man has a relative вҖңadvantageвҖқ (вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ) over the fool (2:13-14a); however, there is no ultimate advantage because both share the same fate, i.e., death (2:14b-15a). Thus, QohelethвҖҷs acquisition of tremendous wisdom (1:16; 2:9) was вҖңexcessiveвҖқ because it exceeded its relative advantage over folly: it could not deliver him from the same fate as the fool. He had striven to obtain wisdom, yet it held no ultimate advantage.
n2100264: tn Heb вҖңAnd why was I wise (to) excess?вҖқ The rhetorical question is an example of negative affirmation, expecting a negative answer: вҖңI gained nothing!вҖқ (E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949).
n2100265: tn Heb вҖңSo I said in my heart.вҖқ
n2100266: tn Heb вҖңand also this,вҖқ referring to the relative advantage of wisdom over folly.
n2100267: tn The word вҖңultimatelyвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100268: tn The preposition вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ (Кҝim, вҖңwithвҖқ) may occasionally function in a comparative sense, meaning вҖңtogether with; even as; likeвҖқ (e.g., Eccl 1:11; 2:16; 7:11; Job 9:26; 1 Chr 14:10: 20:6; 25:8; see HALOT 839 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ 2). When used to describe a common lot, it connotes вҖңtogether withвҖқ (Gen 18:23, 25; 1 Chr 24:5; Job 3:14, 15; 30:1; Pss 26:9; 28:3; 69:29; Isa 38:11), hence вҖңlikeвҖқ (Pss 73:5; 106:6; Eccl 2:16; see BDB 767-68 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ 1.e).
n2100269: tn As HALOT 798-99 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ and BDB 762-64 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ note, вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ (Кҝolam) has a wide range of meanings: (1) indefinite time: вҖңlong time; duration,вҖқ (2) unlimited time: вҖңeternal; eternity,вҖқ (3) future time: вҖңthings to come,вҖқ and (4) past time: вҖңa long time back,вҖқ that is, the dark age of prehistory. The context here suggests the nuance вҖңa long time.вҖқ
n2100270: tn The preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) on вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦ¶ЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ (beshekkevar, the adverb вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ [kevar, вҖңalreadyвҖқ] plus relative pronoun вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶вҖҺ [she] plus preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ) is probably best classified as causal: вҖңBecauseвҖҰalready.вҖқ
n2100271: tn The verb вҖҸЧ ЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧӣЦјЦёЧ—вҖҺ (nishkakh) is a future perfectвҖ”it describes an event that is portrayed as a past event from the perspective of the future: вҖңthey will have been forgotten.вҖқ The emphasis of the past perfect is not simply that the future generations will begin to forget him, but that he will already have been forgotten long ago in the past by the time of those future generations. This past perfect situation is brought out by the emphatic use of the temporal adverb вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ (kevar) вҖңalreadyвҖқ (HALOT 459 s.v. I вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 460 s.v. I вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ); see, e.g., Eccl 1:10; 2:12, 16; 3:15; 4:2; 6:10; 9:6-7.
n2100272: tn The particle вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (Кҫekh, вҖңAlas!вҖқ) is an exclamation of lamentation and mourning (e.g., 2 Sam 1:19; Isa 14:4, 12; Jer 2:21; 9:18; Ezek 26:17; Mic 2:4); see HALOT 39 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЧҡЦ°вҖҺ 5; BDB 32 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЧҡЦ°вҖҺ 2; also E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 955.
n2100273: tn The preposition вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ (Кҝim, вҖңwithвҖқ) may occasionally function in a comparative sense, meaning вҖңtogether with; even as; likeвҖқ (e.g., Eccl 1:11; 2:16; 7:11; Job 9:26; 1 Chr 14:10: 20:6; 25:8); see HALOT 839 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ 2. When used to describe a common lot, it connotes вҖңtogether withвҖқ (Gen 18:23, 25; 1 Chr 24:5; Job 3:14, 15; 30:1; Pss 26:9; 28:3; 69:29; Isa 38:11), hence вҖңlikeвҖқ (Pss 73:5; 106:6; Eccl 2:16); see BDB 767-68 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ 1.e.
n2100275: tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч—Ц·ЧҷЦјЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (hakhayyim, вҖңlifeвҖқ) functions as a metonymy of association, that is, that which is associated with life, that is, the profitlessness and futility of human secular achievement.
