n2100276: tn Heb вҖңthe deed that is done.вҖқ The root вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҝasah, вҖңto doвҖқ) is repeated in вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧһЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦ¶Ч” Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч ЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”вҖҺ (hammaКҝaseh shennaКҝasah, вҖңthe deed that is doneвҖқ) for emphasis. Here, the term вҖңdeedвҖқ does not refer to human accomplishment, as in 2:1-11, but to the fact of death that destroys any relative advantage of wisdom over folly (2:14a-16). Qoheleth metaphorically describes death as a вҖңdeedвҖқ that is вҖңdoneвҖқ to man.
n2100278: tn Heb вҖңall,вҖқ referring here to the relative advantage of wisdom.
n2100279: tn The phrase вҖңthe fruit ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity (see the following note on the phrase вҖңhard laborвҖқ).
n2100280: tn Heb вҖңI hated all my toil for which I had toiled.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧўЦІЧһЦёЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҝamali, вҖңmy toilвҖқ) is repeated throughout 2:18-21. In each case, it functions as a metonymy of cause (i.e., toil) for effect (i.e., fruit of labor). See, e.g., Ps 105:44; BDB 765 s.v вҖҸЧўЦёЧһЦ·ЧңвҖҺ 3. The metonymy is indicated by several factors: (1) The third person masculine singular suffix (вҖңitвҖқ) on вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч ЦјЦҙЧҷЧ—Ц¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (Кҫannikhennu, вҖңI must leave itвҖқ) in 2:18, and on вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦјЦ°Ч Ц¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (yittenennu, вҖңI must give itвҖқ) in 2:21 refer to his wealth, that is, the fruit of his labor. (2) In 2:21 the third person masculine singular suffix on вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧңЦјЦ№Чҗ ЧўЦёЧһЦ·ЧңЦҫЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (shelloКҫ Кҝamal bo, вҖңwho did not work for itвҖқ) refers to the inheritance that Qoheleth must turn over to his successor, namely, the fruit of his labor. (3) While he himself enjoyed the fruit of his labor, he despaired that he had to turn the fruit of his labor over to his successor: вҖңSo I loathed all the [fruit of] my laborвҖқ (2:18a) and вҖңI began to despair about the [fruit of] my laborвҖқ (2:20a). Although most translations render вҖҸЧўЦІЧһЦёЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ as вҖңmy toilвҖқ in 2:18, the metonymy is recognized by several English translations: вҖңSo I hated all the fruit of my labor for which I had laboredвҖқ (NASB); вҖңSo I detested all the fruits of my laborвҖқ (NAB); вҖңI hated all the things I had toiled forвҖқ (NIV); and вҖңSo I loathed all the wealth that I was gainingвҖқ (NJPS).
n2100281: tn Qoheleth uses an internal cognate accusative construction (accusative noun and verb from the same root) for emphasis: вҖҸЧўЦІЧһЦёЧңЦҙЧҷ Ч©ЧҒЦ¶ЧҗЦІЧ ЦҙЧҷ ЧўЦёЧһЦөЧңвҖҺ (Кҝamali sheКҫani Кҝamel, вҖңmy toil for which I had toiledвҖқ). See IBHS 167 В§10.2.1g.
n2100283: tn The relative pronoun вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶вҖҺ (she) on вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧҗЦ·Ч ЦјЦҙЧҷЧ—Ц¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (sheКҫannikhennu, relative pronoun вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶вҖҺ plus Hiphil imperfect first person common singular from вҖҸЧ Ч•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ, nuakh, вҖңto leaveвҖқ plus third person masculine singular suffix) is causal: вҖңBecause I must leave it behind.вҖқ
n2100284: tn The third person masculine singular suffix on вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч ЦјЦҙЧҷЧ—Ц¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (Кҫannikhennu, вҖңI must leave itвҖқ) refers to QohelethвҖҷs wealth, that is, the fruit of his labor (see the note on the phrase вҖңhard laborвҖқ in 2:18). The suffix is rendered literally by nearly all translations; however, a few make its referent explicit: вҖңI have to leave its fruitsвҖқ (NEB), вҖңI must leave them [= all the fruits of my labor]вҖқ (NAB).
n2100285: tn The verb вҖҸЧ Ч•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ (nuakh, вҖңto restвҖқ) denotes вҖңto leave [something] behindвҖқ in the hands of someone (e.g., Ps 119:121; Eccl 2:18); see HALOT 680 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ч•Ч—вҖҺ B.2.c. The imperfect functions in a modal sense of obligation or necessity. At death, Qoheleth will be forced to pass on his entire estate and the fruit of his labors to his successor.
n2100286: tn Heb вҖңto a man who will come after me.вҖқ
n2100287: tn The vav on вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҷЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ (veyishlat, conjunction plus Qal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ, shalat, вҖңto be masterвҖқ) is adversative (вҖңyetвҖқ).
n2100288: tn The phrase вҖңthe fruit ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity (see the following note on the word вҖңlaborвҖқ).
n2100289: tn Heb вҖңmy labor.вҖқ As in 2:18, the term вҖҸЧўЦІЧһЦёЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҝamali, вҖңmy laborвҖқ) is a metonymy of cause (i.e., my labor) for effect (i.e., fruit of my labor). The metonymy is recognized by several translations: вҖңhe will control all the wealth that I gainedвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңhe will have control over all the fruits of my laborвҖқ (NAB); вҖңhe will have mastery over all the fruits of my laborвҖқ (NEB); вҖңhe will have control over all the fruit of my laborвҖқ (NASB); вҖңhe will be master over all my possessionsвҖқ (MLB).
n2100290: tn An internal cognate accusative construction (accusative and verb from same root) is used for emphasis: вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧўЦёЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ вҖҸЧўЦІЧһЦёЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҝamali sheКҝamalti, вҖңmy toil for which I had toiledвҖқ); see IBHS 167 В§10.2.1g. The two verbs вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧўЦёЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷ Ч•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч—ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧһЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (sheКҝamalti veshekhakhamti, вҖңfor which I had labored and for which I had acted wiselyвҖқ) form a verbal hendiadys (two separate verbs used in association to communicate one idea): вҖңfor I had labored so wisely.вҖқ The second verb is used adverbially to modify the first verb, which functions in its full verbal sense.
n2100292: tn Heb вҖңI turned aside to allow my heart despair.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (libbi, вҖңmy heartвҖқ) is a synecdoche of part (i.e., heart) for the whole (i.e., whole person); see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 648.
n2100293: tn The phrase вҖңthe fruit ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity (see the following note on the word вҖңlaborвҖқ).
n2100294: tn Heb вҖңall my toil.вҖқ As in 2:18-19, the term вҖҸЧўЦІЧһЦёЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҝamali, вҖңmy laborвҖқ) is a metonymy of cause (i.e., my labor) for effect (i.e., the fruit of my labor). The metonymy is recognized by several translations: вҖңall the fruits of my laborвҖқ (NAB); вҖңall the fruit of my laborвҖқ (NASB); вҖңall the gains I had madeвҖқ (NJPS).
n2100295: tn Here the author uses an internal cognate accusative construction (accusative noun and verb from the same root) for emphasis: вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧўЦёЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ вҖҸЧ”Ц¶ЧўЦёЧһЦёЧңвҖҺ (heКҝamal sheКҝamalti, вҖңthe toil for which I had toiledвҖқ); see IBHS 167 В§10.2.1g.
n2100297:
tn Heb вҖңhe must give.вҖқ The third person masculine singular suffix on вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦјЦ°Ч Ц¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (yittenennu, Qal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӘЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ, natan, вҖңto giveвҖқ plus third person masculine singular suffix) refers back to вҖҸЧўЦІЧһЦёЧңЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (Кҝamalo, вҖңhis laborвҖқ) which is treated in this line as a metonymy of cause for effect, that is, вҖңhe must give itвҖқ = вҖңhe must give his laborвҖқ = вҖңhe must give the fruit of his labor.вҖқ
sn As in 2:18-19, Qoheleth laments the injustice that a person who works diligently in wisdom must one day hand over the fruit of his labor (i.e., his fortune and the care of his achievements) to his successor. There is no guarantee that oneвҖҷs heir will be wise and be a good steward of this wealth, or be foolish and squander itвҖ”in which case, the former manвҖҷs entire lifeвҖҷs work would be in vain.
n2100298: tn Heb вҖңitвҖқ; the referent (вҖңthe fruit of his laborвҖқ) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100299: tn Or вҖңhe must turn over an inheritanceвҖқ; or вҖңhe must turn it over, namely, an inheritance.вҖқ There are two approaches to the syntax of вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧңЦ°Ч§Ч•Ц№вҖҺ (khelqo, вҖңhis inheritanceвҖқ): (1) The third person masculine singular suffix is a subjective genitive: вҖңhis inheritanceвҖқ = the inheritance which he must give to his heir. The referent of the third person masculine singular suffix is Qoheleth in 2:21a who worked hard to amass the fortune. The noun вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧңЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ (kheleq, вҖңinheritanceвҖқ) functions as an adverbial accusative of state (GKC 372 В§118.a) or a predicate accusative (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 12-13, В§57): вҖңHe must give it [i.e., his fortune] as an inheritance.вҖқ (2) The third person masculine singular suffix is an objective genitive: вҖңhis inheritanceвҖқ = the inheritance which the heir will receive from Qoheleth. The referent of the third person masculine singular suffix is the heir in 2:21b. The noun вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧңЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ (вҖңinheritanceвҖқ) functions as the accusative direct object in apposition (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 15-16, В§71) to the third person masculine singular suffix on вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦјЦ°Ч Ц¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (yittenennu, вҖңhe must give itвҖқ; Qal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӘЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ, natan, plus third person masculine singular suffix): вҖңHe must give it, namely, his inheritance, to one who did not work for it.вҖқ
n21002100:
tn The noun вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah, вҖңevilвҖқ) probably means вҖңmisfortuneвҖқ (HALOT 1263 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 4) or вҖңinjustice; wrongвҖқ (HALOT 1262 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2.b). The phrase вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ” ЧЁЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah rabbah) connotes вҖңgrave injusticeвҖқ or вҖңgreat misfortuneвҖқ (e.g., Eccl 2:17; 5:12, 15; 6:1; 10:5). It is expressed well as: вҖңThis too isвҖҰa great misfortuneвҖқ (NAB, NIV, MLB) and вҖңutterly wrong!вҖқ (NEB).
sn Verses 18-21 are arranged into two sub-units (2:18-19 and 2:20-21). Each contains a parallel structure: (1) Introductory lament: вҖңI hated all my toilвҖқ and вҖңI began to despair about all my toil.вҖқ (2) Reason for the lament: вҖңI must turn over the fruit of my labor to the hands of my successorвҖқ and вҖңhe must hand over the fruit of his work as an inheritance.вҖқ (3) Description of successor: вҖңwho knows whether he will be a wise man or a fool?вҖқ and вҖңhe did not work for it.вҖқ (4) Concluding statement: вҖңThis also is fruitless!вҖқ and вҖңThis also is profitless and an awful injustice!вҖқ
n21002101: tn Heb вҖңunder the sun.вҖқ The rhetorical question is an example of negative affirmation, expecting a negative answer: вҖңMan acquires nothingвҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949-51).
n21002103: tn The syntax of this verse has been interpreted in two different ways: (1) The phrase вҖңall his daysвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦёЧңЦҫЧҷЦёЧһЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ, khol yamayv) is the subject of a verbless clause, and the noun вҖңpainвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧӣЦ°ЧҗЦ№Ч‘ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, makhКҫovim) is a predicate nominative or a predicate of apposition (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 15-16, В§71). Likewise, the noun вҖңhis workвҖқ (вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧ Ч•Ц№вҖҺ, Кҝinyano) is the subject of a second verbless clause, and the vexationвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧЎвҖҺ, khaКҝas) is a predicate nominative: вҖңAll his days are pain, and his work is vexation.вҖқ (2) The noun вҖңhis workвҖқ (вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧ Ч•Ц№вҖҺ) is the subject of both nouns, вҖңpain and vexationвҖқ (вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧЎвҖҺ вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧӣЦ°ЧҗЦ№Ч‘ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, makhКҫovim vakhaКҝas), which are predicate nominatives, while the phrase вҖңall his daysвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦёЧңЦҫЧҷЦёЧһЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ) is an adverbial accusative functioning temporally: вҖңAll day long, his work is pain and vexation.вҖқ The latter option is supported by the parallelism between вҖңeven at nightвҖқ and вҖңall day long.вҖқ This verse draws out an ironic contrast/comparison between his physical toil/labor during the day and his emotional anxiety at night. Even at night, he has no break!
n21002104: tn Heb вҖңhis heart (i.e., mind) does not rest.вҖқ
n21002105: tn The preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) on вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (baКҫadam) has been taken in two ways: (1) locative with вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңgoodвҖқ) in reference to manвҖҷs moral nature: вҖңThere is nothing [inherently] good in man.вҖқ (2) advantage with вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (вҖңgoodвҖқ) in reference to the enjoyment theme of 2:24-26: вҖңThere is nothing better for a man thanвҖҰвҖқ (this assumes a comparative вҖҸЧһЦҙЧҹвҖҺ, min, on вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦјЦ¶ЧҷЦјЦ№ЧҗЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ, misheyyoКҫkhal); see text critical note on the word вҖңthanвҖқ below). The latter is preferred for two reasons: (1) The preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ is used with a similar idiom in 3:12 in collocation with the particle phrase вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷ ЧҗЦҙЧқвҖҺ (ki Кҫim, вҖңexceptвҖқ): вҖңThere is nothing betterвҖҰthan to rejoice/be happyвҖқ (NASB, NIV). (2) The theme of 2:1-26 focuses on the futility of human toil, concluding that the only real reward that man has in his labor is to find enjoyment in it (e.g., 2:10, 24-26). The section says nothing about manвҖҷs inherent sinful nature.
n21002107: tc The MT reads вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧҷЦјЦ№ЧҗЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (sheyyoвҖҷkhal, вҖңthat he should eatвҖқ; Qal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧҗЦёЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ, Кҫakhal, вҖңto eat,вҖқ with relative pronoun вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶вҖҺ, she, вҖңthatвҖқ). However, the variant textual tradition of вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦјЦ¶ЧҷЦјЦ№ЧҗЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (misheyyoКҫkhal, вҖңthan he should eatвҖқ (comparative preposition вҖҸЧһЦҙЧҹвҖҺ, min, вҖңthanвҖқ plus Qal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧҗЦёЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ вҖңto eatвҖқ) is reflected in the LXX, Coptic, Syriac, Aramaic Targum, Old Latin, and Jerome. The textual error, an example of haplography, arose from a single writing of вҖҸЧһвҖҺ (mem) from вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқ ЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦјЦ¶ЧҷЦјЦ№ЧҗЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (baКҫadam misheyyoКҫkhal). The same idiom appears in the expanded form вҖҸЧҗЦөЧҷЧҹ ЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ followed by вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷ ЧҗЦҙЧқвҖҺ (Кҫen tovвҖҰki Кҫim, вҖңthere is nothing better for man thanвҖҰвҖқ) in Eccl 3:12; 8:15.
n21002108: tn Heb вҖңto cause his soul to see good.вҖқ The idiom вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ” ЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (raКҫah tov, вҖңto see goodвҖқ) is a metonymy of association, meaning вҖңto find enjoymentвҖқ (e.g., 3:13; 5:17; 6:6). In 3:12-13 and 5:17-18 it is in collocation and/or parallelism with вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) plus вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦёЧһЦ·Ч—вҖҺ (samakh, вҖңto rejoice in,вҖқ or вҖңto find satisfaction or pleasure inвҖқ something). Here, it is used in collocation with вҖҸЧ—Ч•ЦјЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (khush, вҖңto enjoyвҖқ). The term вҖҸЧ Ц·ЧӨЦ°Ч©ЧҒЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (nafsho, вҖңhis soulвҖқ) is a metonymy of part (i.e., soul) for the whole (i.e., whole person), e.g., Num 23:10; Judg 16:30; Pss 16:10; 35:13; 103:1 (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 640-41).
n21002110: tn The phrase вҖңability to find enjoymentвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n21002111:
tn Heb вҖңis from the hand of God.вҖқ
sn The phrase вҖңfrom the hand of GodвҖқ is an anthropomorphism (depicting God, who is an invisible spirit, in the form of man with hands) or anthropopatheia (depicting God performing human-like actions). The вҖңhand of GodвҖқ is a figure often used to portray GodвҖҷs sovereign providence and benevolence (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 878). The phrase вҖңthe hand of GodвҖқ is often used to connote the favor or grace of God (2 Chr 30:12; Ezra 7:9; 8:18; Neh 2:8, 18; see BDB 390 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ“вҖҺ 1.e.2).
n21002112: tn Heb вҖңFor who canвҖҰ?вҖқ The rhetorical question is an example of negative affirmation, expecting a negative answer: вҖңNo one can!вҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949-51).
n21002113: tn The phrase вҖңand drinkвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for stylistic harmonization with v. 24.
n21002114: tn The verb II вҖҸЧ—Ч•ЦјЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (khush, вҖңto enjoyвҖқ) is a hapax legomenon which BDB defines as вҖңto feel; to enjoy [with the senses]вҖқ on the basis of the context, and the cognates: Arabic вҖңto feel; to perceive [by senses]вҖқ; Aramaic вҖҸЧ—Ч•Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ вҖңto feel pain,вҖқ and New Hebrew вҖҸЧ—Ч•Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ вҖңto feel painвҖқ (BDB 301 s.v. II вҖҸЧ—Ц№Ч•ЦјЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ). HALOT relates the Hebrew root to Akkadian havavu вҖңto be delighted withвҖқ (HALOT 300 s.v. II вҖҸЧ—Ч•Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ 1). The Vulgate renders this term as вҖңto enjoy.вҖқ The Greek versions (LXX, Theodotion) and the Syriac Peshitta, however, did not understand this hapax; they rendered it as вҖңto drink,вҖқ making some sense of the line by filling out the parallelism вҖңto eat [and drink]вҖқ (e.g., Eccl 8:15).
n21002115: tc The MT reads вҖҸЧһЦҙЧһЦјЦ¶Ч ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (mimmenni, вҖңmore than IвҖқ). However, an alternate textual tradition of вҖҸЧһЦҙЧһЦјЦ¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ (mimmennu, вҖңapart from him [= God]вҖқ) is preserved in several medieval Hebrew mss, and is reflected in most of the versions (LXX, Syriac, Syro-Hexapla, and Jerome). The textual deviation is a case of simple orthographic confusion between вҖҸЧҷвҖҺ (yod) and вҖҸЧ•вҖҺ (vav) as frequently happened, e.g., MT вҖҸЧҰЧ• ЧңЧҰЧ• ЧҰЧ• ЧңЧҰЧ•вҖҺ (tsv ltsv tsv ltsv) versus 1QIsaa 28:10 вҖҸЧҰЧҷ ЧңЧҰЧҷ ЧҰЧҷ ЧңЧҰЧҷвҖҺ (tsy ltsy ts ltsy); see P. K. McCarter, Jr., Textual Criticism, 47. It is difficult to determine which reading is original here. The MT forms a parenthetical clause, where Qoheleth refers to himself: no one had more of an opportunity to experience more enjoyment in life than he (e.g., 2:1-11). The alternate textual tradition is a causal clause, explaining why the ability to enjoy life is a gift from God: no one can experience enjoyment in life вҖңapart from him,вҖқ that is, apart from вҖңthe hand of GodвҖқ in 2:24. It is possible that internal evidence supports the alternate textual tradition. In 2:24-26, Qoheleth is not emphasizing his own resources to enjoy life, as he had done in 2:1-11, but that the ability to enjoy life is the gift of God. On the other hand, the Jerusalem Hebrew Bible project retains the MT reading with a вҖңBвҖқ rating; see D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:570. The English versions are split on the textual problem: a few retain MT вҖҸЧһЦҙЧһЦјЦ¶Ч ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (вҖңmore than IвҖқ), e.g., KJV, ASV, YLT, Douay, NJPS, while others adopt the alternate reading вҖҸЧһЦҙЧһЦјЦ¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ, вҖңapart from himвҖқ (NEB, NAB, MLB, NASB, RSV, NRSV, NIV, Moffatt).
n21002116: tn Heb вҖңfor to a man who is good before him.вҖқ
n21002117: sn The phrase the task of amassing wealth (Heb вҖңthe task of gathering and heaping upвҖқ) implicitly compares the work of the farmer reaping his crops and storing them up in a barn, to the work of the laborer amassing wealth as the fruit of his labor. However, rather than his storehouse being safe for the future, the sinner is deprived of it.
n21002118: tn The word вҖңwealthвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n21002119: sn The three-fold repetition of the Hebrew word translated вҖңgiveвҖқ in the first part of this verse creates irony: God вҖңgivesвҖқ the righteous the ability to prosper and to find enjoyment in his work, but to the wicked He вҖңgivesвҖқ the task of вҖңgivingвҖқ his wealth to the righteous.
n21002120: tn The word вҖңitвҖқ (an implied direct object) does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n21002121: tn The antecedent of the demonstrative pronoun вҖҸЧ–Ц¶Ч”вҖҺ (zeh, вҖңthisвҖқ) is debated: (1) Some refer it to the enjoyment which Qoheleth had just commended in 2:24-26. However, this is inconsistent with the enjoyment theme found elsewhere in the book. It also ignores the fact that 2:24-26 states that such enjoyment is a good gift from God. (2) Others refer it to the term вҖңtoilвҖқ (вҖҸЧўЦёЧһЦёЧңвҖҺ, Кҝamal) which is repeated throughout 2:18-26. However, Qoheleth affirmed that if one is righteous, he can find enjoyment in his toil, even though so much of it is ultimately futile. (3) Therefore, it seems best to refer it to the grievous вҖңtaskвҖқ (вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ, Кҝinyan) God has given to the sinner in 2:26b. Consistent with the meaning of вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel, вҖңfutile; profitless; fruitlessвҖқ), 2:26b emphasizes that the вҖңtaskвҖқ of the sinner is profitless: he labors hard to amass wealth, only to see the fruit of his labor given away to someone else. The righteous manвҖҷs enjoyment of his work and the fruit of his labor under the blessing of God (2:24-26a) is not included in this.
n21002122: tn The phrase вҖңtask of the wickedвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n210031: tn Verse 1 is arranged in an ABBвҖҷAвҖҷ chiasm (вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧӣЦјЦ№Чң Ч–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹ Ч•Ц°ЧўЦөЧӘ ЧңЦ°ЧӣЦёЧңЦҫЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ, lakkol zeman veКҝet lekhol khefets): (A) вҖңfor everythingвҖқ; (B) вҖңa seasonвҖқ; (BвҖҷ) вҖңa timeвҖқ; (AвҖҷ) вҖңfor every matter.вҖқ The terms вҖңseasonвҖқ (вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ, zeman) and вҖңtimeвҖқ (вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ, Кҝet) are parallel. In the light of its parallelism with вҖңevery matterвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦёЧңЦҫЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ, khol khefets), the term вҖңeverythingвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦёЧңвҖҺ, khol) must refer to events and situations in life.
n210032:
tn The noun вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ (zeman) denotes вҖңappointed timeвҖқ or вҖңappointed hourвҖқ (HALOT 273 s.v. вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ; BDB 273 s.v. вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ; see Eccl 3:1; Esth 9:27, 31; Neh 2:6; Sir 43:7), e.g., the appointed or designated time for the Jewish feasts (Esth 9:27, 31), the length of time that Nehemiah set for his absence from Susa (Neh 2:6), and the appointed times in the Jewish law for the months to begin (Sir 43:7). It is used in parallelism with вҖҸЧһЧ•ЧўЧ“вҖҺ (вҖңappointed timeвҖқ), i.e., вҖҸЧһЧ•ЧўЧ“ ЧҷЧЁЧ—вҖҺ (вҖңthe appointed time of the moonвҖқ) parallels вҖҸЧ–ЧһЧ Чҷ Ч—Ч§вҖҺ (вҖңthe appointed times of the lawвҖқ; Sir 43:7). The related verb, a Pual of вҖҸЧ–ЦёЧһЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ (zaman), means вҖңto be appointedвҖқ (HALOT 273 s.v. вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ); e.g. Ezra 10:14; Neh 10:35; 13:31. These terms may be related to the noun I вҖҸЧ–ЦҙЧһЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (zimmah, вҖңplan; intentionвҖқ; Job 17:11; HALOT 272 s.v. I вҖҸЧ–ЦҙЧһЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ) and вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч–ЦҙЧһЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (mezimmah, вҖңpurpose; plan; projectвҖқ), e.g., the purposes of God (Job 42:2; Jer 23:20; 30:24; 51:11) and manвҖҷs plan (Isa 5:12); see HALOT 566 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч–ЦҙЧһЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ; BDB 273 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦ°Ч–ЦҙЧһЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ.
sn Verses 1-8 refer to GodвҖҷs appointed time-table for human activities or actions whose most appropriate time is determined by men. Verses 9-15 state that God is ultimately responsible for the time in which events in human history occur. This seems to provide a striking balance between the sovereignty of God and the responsibility of man. Man does what God has willed, but man also does what he вҖңpleasesвҖқ (see note on the word вҖңmatterвҖқ in 3:1).
n210033: tn The noun вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ (Кҝet, вҖңpoint in timeвҖқ) has a basic two-fold range of meanings: (1) вҖңtime of an eventвҖқ and (2) вҖңtime for an eventвҖқ (BDB 773 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ). The latter has subcategories: (a) вҖңusual time,вҖқ (b) вҖңthe proper, suitable or appropriate time,вҖқ (c) вҖңthe appointed time,вҖқ and (d) вҖңuncertain timeвҖқ (Eccl 9:11). Here it connotes вҖңa proper, suitable time for an eventвҖқ (HALOT 900 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 6; BDB s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 2.b). Examples: вҖңthe time for rainвҖқ (Ezra 10:13), вҖңa time of judgment for the nationsвҖқ (Ezek 30:3), вҖңan appropriate time for every occasionвҖқ (Eccl 3:1), вҖңthe time when mountain goats are bornвҖқ (Job 39:1), вҖңthe rain in its seasonвҖқ (Deut 11:14; Jer 5:24), вҖңthe time for the harvestвҖқ (Hos 2:11; Ps 1:3), вҖңfood in its seasonвҖқ (Ps 104:27), вҖңno one knows his hour of destinyвҖқ (Eccl 9:12), вҖңthe right momentвҖқ (Eccl 8:5); cf. HALOT 900 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 6.
n210034: tn The noun вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ (khefets, here вҖңmatter, businessвҖқ) has a broad range of meanings: (1) вҖңdelight; joy,вҖқ (2) вҖңdesire; wish; longing,вҖқ (3) вҖңthe good pleasure; will; purpose,вҖқ (4) вҖңprecious stonesвҖқ (i.e., jewelry), i.e., what someone takes delight in, and (5) вҖңmatter; business,вҖқ as a metonymy of adjunct to what someone takes delight in (Eccl 3:1, 17; 5:7; 8:6; Isa 53:10; 58:3, 13; Pss 16:3; 111:2; Prov 31:13); see HALOT 340 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ 4; BDB 343 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ 4. It is also sometimes used in reference to the вҖңgood pleasureвҖқ of God, that is, his sovereign plan, e.g., Judg 13:23; Isa 44:28; 46:10; 48:14 (BDB 343 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ). While the theme of the sovereignty of God permeates Eccl 3:1-4:3, the content of 3:1-8 refers to human activities that are planned and purposed by man. The LXX translated it with ПҖПҒО¬ОіОјОұП„О№ (pragmati, вҖңmatterвҖқ). The term is translated variously by modern English versions: вҖңevery purposeвҖқ (KJV, ASV), вҖңevery eventвҖқ (NASB), вҖңevery delightвҖқ (NASB margin), вҖңevery affairвҖқ (NAB), вҖңevery matterвҖқ (RSV, NRSV), вҖңevery activityвҖқ (NEB, NIV), вҖңevery projectвҖқ (MLB), and вҖңevery experienceвҖқ (NJPS).
n210036: tn The verb вҖҸЧҷЦёЧңЦёЧ“вҖҺ (yalad, вҖңto bearвҖқ) is used in the active sense of a mother giving birth to a child (HALOT 413 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЧңЧ“вҖҺ; BDB 408 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧңЦёЧ“вҖҺ). However, in light of its parallelism with вҖңa time to die,вҖқ it should be taken as a metonymy of cause (i.e., to give birth to a child) for effect (i.e., to be born).
n210037: sn In 3:2-8, Qoheleth uses fourteen sets of merisms (a figure using polar opposites to encompass everything in between, that is, totality), e.g., Deut 6:6-9; Ps 139:2-3 (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 435).
n210038: tn The term вҖҸЧңЦ°ЧҗЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦөЧ“вҖҺ (leКҫabbed, Piel infinitive construct from вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ, Кҫavad, вҖңto destroyвҖқ) means вҖңto loseвҖқ (e.g., Jer 23:1) as the contrast with вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧ§Ц·Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (baqash, вҖңto seek to findвҖқ) indicates (HALOT 3 s.v. I вҖҸЧҗЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ; BDB 2 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ 3). This is the declarative or delocutive-estimative sense of the Piel: вҖңto view something as lostвҖқ (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 28, В§145; IBHS 403 В§24.2g).
n210039: tn The term вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧўЧ•Ц№Ч©ЧӮЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ (haКҝoseh, article plus Qal active participle ms from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ, Кҝasah, вҖңto doвҖқ) functions substantively (вҖңthe workerвҖқ); see BDB 794 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ II.1. This is a figurative description of man (metonymy of association), and plays on the repetition of вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ (verb: вҖңto do,вҖқ noun: вҖңworkвҖқ) throughout the passage. In the light of GodвҖҷs orchestration of human affairs, manвҖҷs efforts cannot change anything. It refers to man in general with the article functioning in a generic sense (see IBHS 244-45 В§13.5.1f; JoГјon 2:511 В§137.m).
n2100310: sn This rhetorical question is an example of negative affirmation, expecting a negative answer: вҖңMan gains nothing from his toil!вҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949-51). Any advantage that man might gain from his toil is nullified by his ignorance of divine providence.
n2100312: sn The Hebrew adjective translated beautifully functions as a metonymy of effect (i.e., to appear beautiful) for cause (i.e., to make it fit): вҖңto fit beautifully.вҖқ It is used in parallelism with QohelethвҖҷs term for evaluation: вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңgoodвҖқ) in 5:17.
n2100313: tn The word вҖңbutвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100314: tn Heb вҖңdarknessвҖқ; perhaps вҖңeternityвҖқ or вҖңthe future.вҖқ The meaning of the noun вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ (Кҝolam) is debated. It may mean: (1) вҖңignoranceвҖқ; (2) a time reference: (a) вҖңeternityвҖқ or (b) вҖңthe futureвҖқ; or (3) вҖңknowledgeвҖқ (less likely). The arguments for these options may be summarized: (1) Most suggest that вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ is the defectively written form of вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ вҖңduration; eternityвҖқ (e.g., Eccl 1:4; 2:16; 3:14; 9:6; 12:5); see BDB 762 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2.k. Within this school of interpretation, there are several varieties: (a) BDB 762 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2.k suggests that here it denotes вҖңage [i.e., duration] of the world,вҖқ which is attested in postbiblical Hebrew. The term III вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ вҖңeternityвҖқ = вҖңworldвҖқ (Jastrow 1084 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧңЦ·ЧқвҖҺ III) is used in this sense in postbiblical Hebrew, mostly in reference to the Messianic age, or the world to come (e.g., Tg. Genesis 9:16; Tg. Onq. Exodus 21:6; Tg. Psalms 61:7). For example, вҖңthe world (вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ) shall last six thousand years, and after one thousand years it shall be laid wasteвҖқ (b. Rosh HaShanah 31a) and вҖңthe world (вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ) to comeвҖқ (b. Sotah 10b). The LXX and the Vulgate took the term in this sense. This approach was also adopted by several English translations: вҖңthe worldвҖқ (KJV, Douay, ASV margin). (b) HALOT 799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 5 and THAT 2:242 suggest that the term refers to an indefinite, unending future: вҖңeternity futureвҖқ or вҖңenduring state referring to past and futureвҖқ (see also BDB 762 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2.i). In this sense, the noun вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ functions as a metonymy of association: вҖңa sense of eternity,вҖқ but not in a philosophical sense (see J. Barr, Biblical Words for Time [SBT], 117, n. 4). This approach is supported by three factors: (i) the recurrence of вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ (вҖңeternityвҖқ) in 3:14, (ii) the temporal qualification of the statement in the parallel clause (вҖңfrom beginning to endвҖқ), and (iii) by the ordinary meaning of the noun as вҖңeternityвҖқ (HALOT 798-799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ). The point would be that God has endowed man with an awareness of the extra-temporal significance of himself and his accomplishments (D. R. Glenn, вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 984). This is the most frequent approach among English versions: вҖңthe timelessвҖқ (NAB), вҖңeternityвҖқ (RSV, MLB, ASV, NASB, NIV, NJPS), вҖңa sense of time past and time futureвҖқ (NEB), and вҖңa sense of past and futureвҖқ (NRSV). (c) Other scholars suggest that вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ simply refers to the indefinite future: вҖңthe future,вҖқ that is, things to come (e.g., HALOT 799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2; BDB 762 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2.a; THAT 2:241). The plural вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧһЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (Кҝolamim, вҖңthings to comeвҖқ) was used in this sense in Eccl 1:10 (e.g., 1 Kgs 8:13 = 2 Chr 6:2; Pss 61:5; 77:8; 145:13; Dan 9:24; cf. HALOT 799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2). The point would simply be that God has not only ordained all the events that will take place in manвҖҷs life (3:1-8), but also preoccupies man with the desire to discover what will happen in the future in terms of the orchestration or timing of these events in his life (3:9-11). This fits well with the description of GodвҖҷs orchestration of human events in their most appropriate time (3:1-10) and the ignorance of man concerning his future (3:11b). Elsewhere, Qoheleth emphasizes that man cannot learn what the future holds in store for him (e.g., 8:7, 17). This approach is only rarely adopted: вҖңthe futureвҖқ (NJPS margin). (2) The second view is that вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ is not defectively written вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ (вҖңeternityвҖқ) but the segholate noun II вҖҸЧўЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧқвҖҺ (Кҝelem) that means вҖңdarkвҖқ (literal) or вҖңignorance; obscurity; secrecyвҖқ (figurative). The related noun вҖҸЧӘЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧңЦ»ЧһЦёЧ”вҖҺ (taКҝalumah) means вҖңhidden thing; secret,вҖқ and the related verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧңЦ·ЧқвҖҺ (Кҝalam) means вҖңto hide; to concealвҖқ (BDB 761 s.v. I вҖҸЧўЦёЧңЦ·ЧқвҖҺ; HALOT 834-35 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧңЧқвҖҺ). This is related to the Ugaritic noun вҖңdarkвҖқ and the Akkadian verb вҖңto be black; to be darkвҖқ (see HALOT 834-35 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧңЧқвҖҺ). In postbiblical Hebrew the root II вҖҸЧўЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧқвҖҺ means (i) вҖңsecretвҖқ and (ii) вҖңforgetfulnessвҖқ (Jastrow 1084 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧқвҖҺ I). Thus the verse would mean that God has вҖңobscuredвҖқ manвҖҷs knowledge so that he cannot discover certain features of GodвҖҷs program. This approach is adopted by Moffatt which uses the word вҖңmystery.вҖқ Similarly, the term may mean вҖңforgetfulness,вҖқ that is, God has plagued man with вҖңforgetfulnessвҖқ so that he cannot understand what God has done from the beginning to the end (e.g., Eccl 1:11). (3) The third view (Delitzsch) is to relate вҖҸЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ to a cognate Arabic root meaning вҖңknowledge.вҖқ The point would be that God has endowed man with вҖңknowledge,вҖқ but not enough for man to discover GodвҖҷs eternal plan. This approach is only rarely adopted: вҖңknowledgeвҖқ (YLT).
n2100315: tn Heb вҖңin their heart.вҖқ The Hebrew term translated heart functions as a metonymy of association for manвҖҷs intellect, emotions, and will (BDB 524-25 s.v. вҖҸЧңЦөЧ‘вҖҺ 3-6, 9). Here, it probably refers to manвҖҷs intellectual capacities, as v. 11 suggests.
n2100316: tn The compound preposition вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (mibbeli, preposition вҖҸЧһЦҙЧҹвҖҺ [min] + negative particle вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ [beli]) is used as a conjunction here. Elsewhere, it can express cause: вҖңbecause there is no [or is not]вҖқ (e.g., Deut 9:28; 28:55; Isa 5:13; Ezek 34:5; Lam 1:4; Hos 4:6), consequence: вҖңso that there is no [or is not]вҖқ (e.g., Ezek 14:5; Jer 2:15; 9:9-11; Zeph 3:6), or simple negation: вҖңwithoutвҖқ (e.g., Job 4:11, 20; 6:6; 24:7-8; 31:19). BDB 115 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ 3.c.ОІ suggests the negative consequence: вҖңso that not,вҖқ while HALOT 133 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧңЦҙЧҷвҖҺ 5 suggests the simple negation: вҖңwithout the possibility of.вҖқ
n2100318: tn Heb вҖңthe work that God has done.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ”Ц·ЧһЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦ¶Ч” ЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁЦҫЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҫet hammaКҝaseh Кҫasher Кҝasah, вҖңthe work which he [i.e., God] has doneвҖқ) is an internal cognate accusative (direct object and verb are from the same root), used for emphasis (see IBHS 167 В§10.2.1g). The repetition of the verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ (вҖңto doвҖқ) in 3:11 and 3:14 suggests that this phrase refers to GodвҖҷs foreordination of all the events and timing of human affairs: God has вҖңmadeвҖқ ( = вҖңforeordainedвҖқ; вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ) everything appropriate in his sovereign timing (3:11a), and all that God has вҖңdoneвҖқ ( = вҖңforeordainedвҖқ; вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ) will come to pass (3:14). Thus, the verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ functions as a metonymy of effect (i.e., GodвҖҷs actions) for cause (i.e., GodвҖҷs sovereign foreordination). The temporal clause вҖңfrom beginning to endвҖқ (3:11) supports this nuance.
n2100319: tn Traditionally, вҖңwhat God has done from the beginning to the end.вҖқ The temporal clause вҖҸЧһЦөЧЁЦ№ЧҗЧ©ЧҒ Ч•Ц°ЧўЦ·Ч“ЦҫЧЎЧ•Ц№ЧЈвҖҺ (meroКҫsh veКҝad-sof, вҖңfrom the beginning to the endвҖқ) is traditionally taken in reference to вҖңeternityвҖқ (the traditional understanding of вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ [haКҝolam] earlier in the verse; see the note on вҖңignoranceвҖқ), e.g., KJV, NEB, NAB, ASV, NASB, NIV, RSV, NRSV. However, if вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧўЦ№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ simply denotes вҖңthe futureвҖқ (e.g., HALOT 799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2; BDB 762 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ 2.a; THAT 2:241), this temporal clause would refer to the events God has ordained to transpire in an individualвҖҷs life, from beginning to end. This approach is adopted by one English version: вҖңbut without man ever guessing, from first to last, all the things that God brings to passвҖқ (NJPS). This would fit well in the context begun in 3:1 with the fourteen merisms encompassing manвҖҷs life, starting with вҖңa time to be bornвҖқ (i.e., from the beginning in 3:11) and concluding with вҖңa time to dieвҖқ (i.e., to the end in 3:11). This approach is also supported by the admonition of 3:12-13, namely, since no one knows what will happen to him in the future days of his life, Qoheleth recommends that man enjoy each day as a gift from God.
n2100320: tn The phrase вҖңof their livesвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100322: tn Heb вҖңfor themвҖқ; the referent (people, i.e., mankind) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100323: tn Qoheleth uses the exceptive particle вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷ ЧҗЦҙЧқвҖҺ (ki Кҫim, вҖңexceptвҖқ) to identify the only exception to the futility within manвҖҷs life (BDB 474 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ 2).
n2100324: tn Heb вҖңto do good.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЧ•Ц№ЧӘ ЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (laКҝasot tov) functions idiomatically for вҖңto experience [or see] happiness [or joy].вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ (Кҝasah) probably denotes вҖңto acquire; to obtainвҖқ (BDB 795 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ II.7), and вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov) means вҖңgood; pleasure; happiness,вҖқ e.g., Eccl 2:24; 3:13; 5:17 (BDB 375 s.v. вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ 1).
n2100325: tn Heb вҖңfor it.вҖқ The referent of the third person feminine singular independent person pronoun (вҖңitвҖқ) is probably the preceding statement: вҖңto eat, drink, and find satisfaction.вҖқ This would be an example of an anacoluthon (GKC 505-6 В§167.b). Thus the present translation uses вҖңthese thingsвҖқ to indicate the reference back to the preceding.
n2100326: tn The phrase вҖңto do againвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100327: tn Heb вҖңGod will seek that which is driven away.вҖқ The meaning of вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч‘Ц·Ч§ЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒ ЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ ЦҙЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦёЧЈвҖҺ (yevaqqesh Кҫet nirdaf) is difficult to determine: вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч‘Ц·Ч§ЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (yevaqqesh) is Piel imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧ§Ц·Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (baqash, вҖңto seekвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ ЦҙЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦёЧЈвҖҺ (nirdaf) is a Niphal participle third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧЁЦёЧ“Ц·ЧЈвҖҺ (radaf, вҖңto drive awayвҖқ). There are several options: (1) God watches over the persecuted: вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч‘Ц·Ч§ЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (вҖңseeksвҖқ) functions as a metonymy of cause for effect (i.e., to protect), and вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ ЦҙЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦёЧЈвҖҺ (вҖңwhat is driven awayвҖқ) refers to вҖңthose who are persecuted.вҖқ But this does not fit the context. (2) God will call the past to account: вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч‘Ц·Ч§ЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ functions as a metonymy of cause for effect (i.e., to hold accountable), and вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ ЦҙЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦёЧЈвҖҺ is a metonymy of attribute (i.e., the past). This approach is adopted by several English translations: вҖңGod requires that which is pastвҖқ (KJV), вҖңGod will call the past to accountвҖқ (NIV) and вҖңGod summons each event back in its turnвҖқ (NEB). (3) God finds what has been lost: вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч‘Ц·Ч§ЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ functions as a metonymy of cause for effect (i.e., to find), and вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ ЦҙЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦёЧЈвҖҺ refers to what has been lost: вҖңGod restores what would otherwise be displacedвҖқ (NAB). (4) God repeats what has already occurred: вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч‘Ц·Ч§ЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ functions as a metonymy of effect (i.e., to repeat), and вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧ ЦҙЧЁЦ°Ч“ЦјЦёЧЈвҖҺ is a metonymy (i.e., that which has occurred). This fits the context and provides a tight parallel with the preceding line: вҖңThat which is has already been, and that which will be has already beenвҖқ (3:15a) parallels вҖңGod seeks [to repeat] that which has occurred [in the past].вҖқ This is the most popular approach among English versions: вҖңGod restores that which has pastвҖқ (Douay), вҖңGod seeks again that which is passed awayвҖқ (ASV), вҖңGod seeks what has passed byвҖқ (NASB), вҖңGod seeks what has been driven awayвҖқ (RSV), вҖңGod seeks out what has passed byвҖқ (MLB), вҖңGod seeks out what has gone byвҖқ (NRSV), and вҖңGod is ever bringing back what disappearsвҖқ (Moffatt).
n2100328: tn The phrase вҖңin the pastвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100331: tn The phrase вҖңa time of judgmentвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100332: tn The phrase вҖңit isвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100333: tn Heb вҖңthe sons of man.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧңЦҫЧ“ЦјЦҙЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЦ·ЧӘ Ч‘ЦјЦ°Ч ЦөЧҷ Ч”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (Кҝal divrat bene haКҫadam) is handled variously: (1) introduction to the direct discourse: вҖңI said to myself concerning the sons of menвҖқ (NASB), (2) direct discourse: вҖңI thought, вҖҳAs for men, God tests themвҖҷвҖқ (NIV), (3) indirect discourse: вҖңI said in my heart concerning the estate of the sons of menвҖқ (KJV), and (4) causal conjunction: вҖңI said, вҖҳ[It is] for the sake of the sons of men.вҖқ Since the phrase вҖңsons of menвҖқ is contrasted with вҖңanimalsвҖқ the translation вҖңhumansвҖқ has been adopted.
n2100334: tn The meaning of вҖҸЧңЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦёЧқвҖҺ (levaram, preposition plus Qal infinitive construct from вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ, barar, plus third person masculine plural suffix) is debated because the root has a broad range of meanings: (1) вҖңto test; to prove; to sift; to sort outвҖқ (e.g., Dan 11:35; 12:10); (2) вҖңto choose; to selectвҖқ (e.g., 1 Chr 7:40; 9:22; 16:41; Neh 5:18); (3) вҖңto purge out; to purifyвҖқ (e.g., Ezek 20:38; Zeph 3:9; Job 33:3); and (4) вҖңto cleanse; to polishвҖқ (Isa 49:2; 52:11); see HALOT 163 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 141 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ. The meanings вҖңto proveвҖқ (Qal), as well as вҖңto cleanse; to polishвҖқ (Qal), вҖңto keep cleanвҖқ (Niphal), and вҖңto cleanseвҖқ (Hiphil) might suggest the meaning вҖңto make clearвҖқ (M. A. Eaton, Ecclesiastes [TOTC], 85-86). The meaning вҖңto make clear; to proveвҖқ is well attested in postbiblical Mishnaic Hebrew (Jastrow 197-98 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ). For example, вҖңthey make the fact as clear (bright) as a new garmentвҖқ (b. Ketubbot 46a) and вҖңthe claimant must offer clear evidenceвҖқ (b. Sanhedrin 23b). The point would be that God allows human injustice to exist in the world in order to make it clear to mankind that they are essentially no better than the beasts. On the other hand, the LXX adopts the nuance вҖңto judge,вҖқ while Targum and Vulgate take the nuance вҖңto purge; to purify.вҖқ BDB 141 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ 4 suggests вҖңto test, prove,вҖқ while HALOT 163 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ 2 prefers вҖңto select, choose.вҖқ
n2100335: tn The two infinitives вҖҸЧңЦ°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦёЧқвҖҺ (levaram, вҖңto make it clear to themвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦҙЧЁЦ°ЧҗЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (velirКҫot, вҖңand to showвҖқ) function as a verbal hendiadys (the two verbs are associated with one another to communicate a single idea). The first verb functions adverbially and the second retains its full verbal force: вҖңto clearly show them.вҖқ
n2100337: tn Heb вҖңthe spirit of the sons of man.вҖқ
n2100341: tn Heb вҖңwhat will be after himвҖқ (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV) or вҖңafterwardвҖқ (cf. NJPS).
n210041:
tn The prefixed vav on вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (veshavti, vav plus perfect first person common singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ, shuv, вҖңto turnвҖқ) might be: (1) introductory (and left untranslated): вҖңI observed againвҖқ; (2) consequence of preceding statement: вҖңSo I observed againвҖқ; or (3) continuation of preceding statement: вҖңAnd I observed again.вҖқ
sn This section is closely related to the preceding: QohelethвҖҷs observation of oppression (4:1-3) links back to his previous observation of oppression and injustice (3:16). It stands in stark contrast with his admonition for man to enjoy life on earth as the reward for oneвҖҷs work (3:22). Now, Qoheleth turns his attention to consider the sorry fate of those who are not able to enjoy life on earth and their work because of oppression (4:1-3), over-obsessive competitiveness (4:4-6), and loneliness (4:7-12).
n210042: tn Heb вҖңI turned and I saw.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧҗЦ¶ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ¶Ч”вҖҺвҖҰвҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (veshavtiвҖҰvaКҫerКҫeh, вҖңI turned and I sawвҖқ) is a verbal hendiadys (the two verbs represent one common idea). Normally in a verbal hendiadys, the first verb functions adverbially, modifying the second verb which retains its full verbal force. The verb вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (vav plus perfect first person common singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ вҖңto turnвҖқ) is used idiomatically to denote repetition: вҖңto return and doвҖқ = вҖңto do againвҖқ (e.g., Gen 26:18; 30:31; 43:2) or вҖңto do repeatedlyвҖқ (e.g., Lam 3:3); see HALOT 1430 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•Ч‘вҖҺ 5; BDB 998 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ 8; GKC 386 В§120.e: вҖңI observed againвҖқ or вҖңI repeatedly observed.вҖқ On the other hand, the shift from the perfect вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ to the preterite вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧҗЦ¶ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ (vav plus Qal preterite first person common singular from вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ, raКҫah, вҖңto seeвҖқ) might indicate a purpose clause: вҖңI turned [my mind] to consider.вҖқ The preterite вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧҗЦ¶ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ follows the perfect вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ. When a wayyiqtol form (vav plus preterite) follows a perfect in reference to a past-time situation, the preterite also represents a past-time situation. Its aspect is based on the preceding perfect. In this context, the perfect and preterite may denote definite past or indefinite past action (вҖңI turned and consideredвҖқ as hendiadys for вҖңI observed againвҖқ or вҖңI repeatedly observedвҖқ) or past telic action (вҖңI turned [my mind] to considerвҖқ). See IBHS 554-55 В§33.3.1a.
n210043: tn Heb вҖңall the oppressionsвҖқ or вҖңall the oppressionвҖқ; alternately, вҖңall the various kinds of oppression.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ (Кҝosheq) denotes вҖңoppression,вҖқ e.g., Jer 6:6; 22:17; Ezek 18:18; 22:7, 12, 29; Pss 73:8; 119:134 (see HALOT 897 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ 1; BDB 799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ 1). It occurs several times in the book, always in reference to personal rather than national oppression (4:1; 5:8 ET [5:7 HT]; 7:7). The noun вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧўЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ»Ч§ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (haКҝashuqim) is plural and articular (Heb вҖңthe oppressionsвҖқ). The article indicates a generic class (вҖңoppressionвҖқ). The plural may be classified in one of two ways: (1) a plural of number, which refers to specific kinds of oppression that occur on earth: вҖңthe various kinds of oppressionвҖқ; (2) an abstract plural, which is used to refer to abstract concepts: вҖңthe oppressionвҖқ; or (3) a plural of intensity, which describes the oppression at hand as particularly grievous: вҖңawful oppressionвҖқ or вҖңsevere oppression.вҖқ The LXX renders it as a plural of number: ПғП…ОәОҝПҶОұОҪП„ОҜОұПӮ (sukophantias, вҖңoppressionsвҖқ), as does the Vulgate. Most English versions treat it as a plural of number: вҖңthe oppressionsвҖқ (KJV, ASV, NAB, RSV, NRSV, MLB, YLT); however, a few treat it as an abstract plural: вҖңthe oppressionвҖқ (NJPS, NIV, Moffatt).
n210044: tn Heb вҖңis done.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ Ц·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (naКҝasim, Niphal participle mpl from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ, Кҝasah, вҖңto doвҖқ) is a probably a verbal use of the participle rather than a substantival use (NEB вҖңall the acts of oppressionвҖқ). This verbal use of the participle depicts durative or universal gnomic action. It emphasizes the lamentable continuity of oppression throughout human history. The English versions translate it variously: вҖң[all the oppressions that] are doneвҖқ (KJV, ASV, Douay, YLT), вҖң[all the oppression] that goes onвҖқ (NJPS, Moffatt), вҖң[all the oppressions] that are practicedвҖқ (RSV, NRSV), вҖң[all the oppressions] that occurвҖқ (MLB), вҖң[all the acts of oppression] which were being doneвҖқ (NASB), вҖң[all the oppressions] that take placeвҖқ (NAB), вҖң[all the oppression] that was taking placeвҖқ (NIV).
n210046: tn Heb вҖңand behold.вҖқ The deictic particle вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”ЦҙЧ ЦјЦөЧ”вҖҺ (vehinneh, вҖңand behold!вҖқ) often occurs after verbs of perceiving, such as вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ, raКҫah, вҖңto seeвҖқ (e.g., Gen 19:28; 22:13; Exod 3:2; Lev 13:8). It introduces the content of what the character or speaker saw (HALOT 252 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦҙЧ ЦјЦөЧ”вҖҺ 8). It is used for rhetorical emphasis, to draw attention to the following statement (e.g., Gen 1:29; 17:20; Num 22:32; Job 1:19; cf. HALOT 252 s.v. 5). It often introduces something surprising or unexpected (e.g., Gen 29:6; Num 25:6; cf. HALOT 252 s.v. 6).
n210047: tn The term вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧўЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ»Ч§ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (haКҝashuqim, Qal passive participle masculine participle of вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧҒЦ·Ч§вҖҺ, Кҝashaq, вҖңto oppressвҖқ) is a passive form, emphasizing that they are the objects of oppression at the hands of their oppressors. The participle functions as a noun, emphasizing the durative aspect of their condition and that this was the singular most characteristic attribute of this group of people: Their lives were marked by oppression.
n210048: tn Heb вҖңthe tear of the oppressed.вҖқ Alternately, вҖңthe oppressed [were in] tears.вҖқ The singular noun вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧһЦ°ЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (dimКҝah, вҖңtearвҖқ) is used as a collective for вҖңtearsвҖқ (2 Kgs 20:5; Isa 16:9; 25:8; 38:5; Jer 8:23 HT [9:1 ET]; 9:7 HT [9:18 ET]; 13:17; 14:17; 31:16; Ezek 24:16; Mal 2:13; Pss 6:7; 39:13; 42:4; 56:9; 80:6; 116:8; 126:5; Lam 1:2; 2:18; Eccl 4:1); see HALOT 227 s.v. вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧһЦ°ЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ; BDB 199 s.v. вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧһЦ°ЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ. It is often used in reference to lamentation over calamity, distress, or oppression (e.g., Ps 6:7; Lam 1:2; 2:11; Jer 9:17; 13:17; 14:17). The LXX translated it as singular ОҙО¬ОәПҒОҝП…ОҪ (dakroun, вҖңthe tearвҖқ); however, the Vulgate treated it as a collective (вҖңthe tearsвҖқ). Apart from the woodenly literal YLT (вҖңthe tearвҖқ), the major English versions render this as a collective: вҖңthe tearsвҖқ or вҖңtearsвҖқ (KJV, ASV, NEB, NAB, NASB, RSV, NRSV, NJPS, MLB, NIV). The term вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧһЦ°ЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ functions as a metonymy of association for вҖңweepingвҖқ (e.g., Isa 16:9; Jer 8:23 HT [9:1 ET]): вҖңthe oppressed [were weeping with] tears.вҖқ The genitive construct вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧһЦ°ЧўЦ·ЧӘ Ч”ЦёЧўЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ»Ч§ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (dimКҝat haКҝashuqim, literally, вҖңtear of the oppressedвҖқ) is a subjective genitive construction, that is, the oppressed are weeping. The singular вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧһЦ°ЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (dimКҝah, вҖңtearвҖқ) is used as a collective for вҖңtears.вҖқ This entire phrase, however, is still given a woodenly literal translation by most English versions: вҖңthe tears of the oppressedвҖқ (NEB, NAB, ASV, NASB, RSV, NRSV, MLB, NIV, NJPS). Some paraphrases attempt to fill out the meaning, e.g., вҖңthe oppressed were in tearsвҖқ (Moffatt).
n210049: tn Heb вҖңcomforts.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ ЦёЧ—Ц·ЧқвҖҺ (nakham, вҖңto comfortвҖқ) is used as a metonymy of effect (i.e., comfort) for cause (i.e., deliverance), e.g., it is used in parallelism with вҖҸЧ’ЦёЧҗЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (gaКҫal, вҖңto deliverвҖқ) in Isa 52:9 (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 560-67).
n2100410: tn Heb вҖңfrom the hand of their oppressors is power.вҖқ
n2100411: tn The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ (shavakh) has a two-fold range of meaning: (1) вҖңto praise; to laudвҖқ; and (2) вҖңto congratulateвҖқ (HALOT 1387 s.v. I вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ‘Ч—вҖҺ; BDB 986 s.v. II вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ). The LXX translated it as бјҗПҖбҝ„ОҪОөПғОұ (epД“nesa, вҖңI praisedвҖқ). The English versions reflect the range of possible meanings: вҖңpraisedвҖқ (KJV, ASV, Douay); вҖңcongratulatedвҖқ (MLB, NASB); вҖңdeclared/judged/accounted/thoughtвҖҰfortunate/happyвҖқ (NJPS, NEB, NIV, RSV, NRSV, NAB).
n2100412: tn Heb вҖңthe dead who had already died.вҖқ
n2100414: tn The word вҖңbornвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100417: tn Heb вҖңall the toil and all the skill.вҖқ This Hebrew clause (вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧӣЦјЦёЧңЦҫЧўЦёЧһЦёЧң Ч•Ц°ЧҗЦөЧӘ ЧӣЦјЦёЧңЦҫЧӣЦјЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ, Кҫet kol Кҝamal veКҫet kol kishron) is a nominal hendiadys (a figurative expression in which two independent phrases are used to connote the same thing). The second functions adverbially, modifying the first, which retains its full nominal function: вҖңall the skillful work.вҖқ
n2100418: tn The phrase вҖңnothing more thanвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100419: tn The noun вҖҸЧ§ЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҗЦ·Ч”вҖҺ (qinКҫah, вҖңcompetitionвҖқ) has a wide range of meanings: вҖңzeal; jealousy; envy; rivalry; competition; suffering; animosity; anger; wrathвҖқ (HALOT 1110 s.v.; BDB 888 s.v.). Here, as in 9:6, it denotes вҖңrivalryвҖқ (BDB 888 s.v. 1) or вҖңcompetitive spiritвҖқ (HALOT 1110 s.v. 1.b). The LXX rendered it О¶бҝҶО»ОҝПӮ (zД“los, вҖңenvy; jealousyвҖқ). The English versions reflect this broad range: вҖңrivalryвҖқ (NEB, NAB, NASB), вҖңenvyвҖқ (KJV, ASV, RSV, NRSV, MLB, NIV, NJPS), and вҖңjealousyвҖқ (Moffatt).
n2100421: tn The word вҖңlikeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100422: tn Heb вҖңthe fool folds his hands.вҖқ The Hebrew idiom means that he does not work (e.g., Prov 6:10; 24:33). In the translation the words вҖңand does no workвҖқ (which do not appear in the Hebrew text) have been supplied following the idiom to clarify what is meant.
n2100423: tn Heb вҖңand eats his own flesh.вҖқ Most English versions render the idiom literally: вҖңand eats/consumes his fleshвҖқ (KJV, AS, NASB, NAB, RSV, NRSV, NJPS). However, a few versions attempt to explain the idiom: вҖңand lets life go to ruinвҖқ (Moffatt), вҖңand wastes awayвҖқ (NEB), вҖңand ruins himselfвҖқ (NIV).
n2100424: sn Qoheleth lists three approaches to labor: (1) the competitive workaholic in 4:4, (2) the impoverished sluggard in 4:5, and (3) the contented laborer in 4:6. The balanced approach rebukes the two extremes.
n2100425: tn The prefixed vav on вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (veshavti, vav + perfect first person common singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ, shuv, вҖңto turnвҖқ) might be: (1) introductory (and left untranslated): вҖңI observed againвҖҰвҖқ; (2) consequence of preceding statement: вҖңSo I observed againвҖҰвҖқ; or (3) continuation of preceding statement: вҖңAnd I observed againвҖҰ.вҖқ
n2100426: tn Heb вҖңI turned and I sawвҖҰвҖқ; or вҖңI again considered.вҖқ The Hebrew phrase вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧҗЦ¶ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ¶Ч”вҖҺвҖҰвҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (veshavtiвҖҰvaКҫerКҫeh, вҖңI turned and I sawвҖқ) is a verbal hendiadys (the two verbs represent one common idea). Normally in a verbal hendiadys, the first verb functions adverbially, modifying the second verb which retains its full verbal force. The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ (shuv, вҖңto turnвҖқ) is used idiomatically to denote repetition: вҖңto return and doвҖқ = вҖңto do againвҖқ (e.g., Gen 26:18; 30:31; 43:2) or вҖңto do repeatedlyвҖқ (e.g., Lam 3:3); see HALOT 1430 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•Ч‘вҖҺ 5; BDB 998 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ 8; GKC 386 В§120.e: вҖңI observed againвҖқ or вҖңI repeatedly observed.вҖқ On the other hand, the shift from the perfect вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (vav plus perfect first person common singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ, вҖңto turnвҖқ) to the preterite вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧҗЦ¶ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ (vav plus Qal preterite first person common singular from вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ, raКҫah, вҖңto seeвҖқ) might indicate a purpose clause: вҖңI turned [my mind] to considerвҖҰ.вҖқ The preterite вҖҸЧ•ЦёЧҗЦ¶ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ follows the perfect вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ. When a wayyiqtol form (vav plus preterite) follows a perfect in reference to a past-time situation, the preterite also represents a past-time situation. Its aspect is based on the preceding perfect. In this context, the perfect and preterite may denote definite past or indefinite past action (вҖңI turned and consideredвҖҰвҖқ as hendiadys for вҖңI observed againвҖқ or вҖңI repeatedly observedвҖқ) or past telic action (вҖңI turned [my mind] to considerвҖҰвҖқ). See IBHS 554-55 В§33.3.1a.
n2100427: tn The word вҖңanotherвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100429: tn Heb вҖңThere is one and there is not a second.вҖқ
n2100430: tn Heb вҖңson nor brother.вҖқ The terms вҖңsonвҖқ and вҖңbrotherвҖқ are examples of synecdoche of specific (species) for the general (genus). The term вҖңsonвҖқ is put for offspring, and вҖңbrotherвҖқ for siblings (e.g., Prov 10:1).
n2100431: tn Heb вҖңhis eye.вҖқ The term вҖңeyeвҖқ is a synecdoche of part (i.e., the eye) for the whole (i.e., the whole person); see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 647.
n2100432: tn The phrase вҖңhe lamentsвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity. The direct discourse (вҖңFor whom am I toiling and depriving myself of pleasure?вҖқ) is not introduced with an introductory structure. As in the LXX, some translations suggest that these words are spoken by a lonely workaholic, e.g., вҖңHe saysвҖҰвҖқ (NAB, NEB, ASV, NIV, NRSV). Others suggest that this is a question that he never asks himself, e.g., вҖңYet he never asks himselfвҖҰвҖқ (KJV, RSV, MLB, YLT, Douay, NASB, Moffatt).
n2100434: tn This rhetorical question is an example of negative affirmation, that is, it expects a negative answer: вҖңNo one!вҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949-51).
n2100435: tn The adjective вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (raКҝ, вҖңevilвҖқ) here means вҖңmisfortuneвҖқ (HALOT 1263 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 4) or вҖңinjustice, wrongвҖқ (HALOT 1262 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2.b). The phrase вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·Чҹ ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (Кҝinyan raКҝ, вҖңunhappy business; rotten business; grievous taskвҖқ) is used only in Ecclesiastes (1:13; 2:23, 26; 3:10; 4:8; 5:2, 13; 8:16). It is parallel with вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel, вҖңfutileвҖқ) in 4:8, and describes a вҖңgrave misfortuneвҖқ in 5:13. The noun вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝinyan, вҖңbusinessвҖқ) refers to something that keeps a person occupied or busy: вҖңbusiness; affair; task; occupationвҖқ (HALOT 857 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ; BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ). The related verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҝanah) means вҖңto be occupied; to be busy with (вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ, bet),вҖқ e.g., Eccl 1:13; 3:10; 5:19 (HALOT 854 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ; BDB 775 s.v. II вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ). The noun is from the Aramaic loanword вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧ ЦёЧҗвҖҺ (КҝinyanaКҫ, вҖңconcern; care.вҖқ The verb is related to the Aramaic verb вҖңto try hard,вҖқ the Arabic verb вҖңto be busily occupied; to worry; to be a matter of concern,вҖқ and the Old South Arabic root вҖңto be troubled; to strive withвҖқ (HALOT 854 s.v. III вҖҸЧўЧ Ч”вҖҺ). HALOT 857 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ renders the phrase as вҖңunhappy businessвҖқ here. The phrase вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·Чҹ ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ, is treated creatively by English versions: KJV, ASV вҖңsore travailвҖқ; YLT вҖңsad travailвҖқ; Douay вҖңgrievous vexationвҖқ; RSV, NRSV, NJPS вҖңunhappy businessвҖқ; NEB, Moffatt вҖңsorry businessвҖқ; NIV вҖңmiserable businessвҖқ; NAB вҖңworthless taskвҖқ; NASB вҖңgrievous taskвҖқ; MLB вҖңsorry situationвҖқ; NLT вҖңdepressing.вҖқ
n2100439: tn The verbal root вҖҸЧӘЦјЦёЧ§Ц·ЧЈвҖҺ (taqaf) means вҖңto overpower; to prevail overвҖқ e.g., Job 14:20; 15:24; Eccl 4:12; 6:10 (HALOT 1786 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЧ§ЧЈвҖҺ).
n2100440: tn Heb вҖңcame from the house of bonds.вҖқ
n2100441: tn The phrase вҖңwhat would becomeвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity. However, it is not altogether clear whether the third person masculine singular suffix (вҖңhisвҖқ) on вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧӣЧ•ЦјЧӘЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (bemalkhuto, вҖңhis kingdomвҖқ) refers to the old foolish king or to the poor but wise youth of 4:13.
n2100443: tn Heb вҖңthe second youth.вҖқ It is not clear whether вҖңthe secondвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦөЧ ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, hasheni) refers to the young man who succeeds the old king or a second youthful successor.
n2100444: tn The verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧһЦ·Ч“вҖҺ (Кҝamad, вҖңto standвҖқ) may denote вҖңto arise; to appear; to come on the sceneвҖқ (e.g., Ps 106:30; Dan 8:22, 23; 11:2-4; 12:1; Ezra 2:63; Neh 7:65); cf. BDB 764 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧһЦ·Ч“вҖҺ 6.a; HALOT 840 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧһЧ“вҖҺ 1.a.
n2100445: tn Heb вҖңthe people.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧқвҖҺ (Кҝam, вҖңpeopleвҖқ) can refer to the subjects of the king (BDB 766 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧқвҖҺ 2).
n2100447: tn Heb вҖңthose coming after.вҖқ The Hebrew term вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЧ•Ц№Ч ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (haКҫakharonim, вҖңthose coming afterвҖқ) is derived from the preposition вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ (Кҫakhar, вҖңbehindвҖқ). When used in reference to time, it refers to future generations (e.g., Deut 29:21; Pss 48:14; 78:4, 6; 102:19; Job 18:20; Eccl 1:11; 4:16); cf. HALOT 36 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ B.3; BDB 30 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦ·Ч—Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ 2.b).
n2100448: tn The word вҖңlikeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n210051: sn Beginning with 5:1, the verse numbers through 5:20 in the English Bible differ by one from the verse numbers in the Hebrew text (BHS), with 5:1 ET = 4:17 HT, 5:2 ET = 5:1 HT, etc., through 5:20 ET = 5:19 HT. Beginning with 6:1 the verse numbers in the English Bible and the Hebrew Bible are again the same.
n210052:
tn Heb вҖңGuard your feet.вҖқ The Kethib is the plural вҖҸЧЁЦ·Ч’Ц°ЧңЦ¶ЧҷЧҡЦёвҖҺ (raglekha, вҖңyour feetвҖқ), while the Qere is the singular вҖҸЧЁЦ·Ч’Ц°ЧңЦ°ЧҡЦёвҖҺ (raglekha, вҖңyour footвҖқ), which is preserved in several medieval Hebrew mss and is reflected in the versions (LXX, Aramaic Targum, Vulgate, Syriac Peshitta). For example, the LXX reads ПҖПҢОҙОұ ПғОҝП… (poda sou, вҖңyour footвҖқ) which reflects вҖҸЧЁЦ·Ч’Ц°ЧңЦ°ЧҡЦёвҖҺ.
sn The exhortation, вҖңGuard your feetвҖқ is an idiom for вҖңWatch your steps,вҖқ i.e., вҖңBe careful what you do.вҖқ This is a compound figure: вҖңfootвҖқ is a metonymy for вҖңstep,вҖқ and вҖңstepвҖқ is a metonymy for вҖңactionвҖқ (e.g., Job 12:5; 23:11; 31:5; Ps 119:59, 101, 105; Prov 1:16; 3:23; 4:26-27; 6:18; 19:2; Isa 58:13; 59:7; Jer 14:10). For example, вҖңI have refrained my feet from every evil wayвҖқ (Ps 119:101); see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 648.
n210053: tn Heb вҖңthe house of God.вҖқ The term вҖңhouseвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧӘвҖҺ, bayit) is a synecdoche of general (i.e., house) for specific (i.e., temple), e.g., 1 Kgs 6:3; 7:12; 1 Chr 9:11; 2 Chr 3:8; 28:11. See E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 620.
n210054: tn Alternately, вҖңto obey.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧңЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧһЦ№ЧўЦ·вҖҺ (lishmoaКҝ, preposition plus Qal infinitive construct from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦ·ЧўвҖҺ, shamaКҝ, вҖңto hearвҖқ) may be taken in one of two ways: (1) literal: вҖңto listenвҖқ in contrast to speak or (2) figurative (metonymy of cause for effect) вҖңto obeyвҖқ in contrast to sacrifice (HALOT 1572 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧһЧўвҖҺ 4; BDB 1033-34 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦ·ЧўвҖҺ). The LXX took the term in the literal sense: П„ОҝбҝҰ бјҖОәОҝПҚОөО№ОҪ (tou akouein, вҖңto listenвҖқ). The English versions reflect both literal and figurative options: вҖңobedienceвҖқ (NJPS, Douay, NAB, NEB) versus вҖңto hear [or listen]вҖқ (KJV, ASV, YLT, MLB, RSV, NASB, NIV, NRSV). The section warns against rash vows therefore, the nuance вҖңto listenвҖқ is more appropriate: the wise man will be slow to speak and quick to listen in the presence of God; however, the fool is unrestrained and speaks rashly.
n210055: tn The term вҖңsacrificeвҖқ (вҖҸЧ–Ц¶Ч‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ, zevakh) is the general term that refers to the thank offering and free will offering (Lev 7:12, 16). This section focuses on making vows in prayer and fulfilling them, such as the vow offering. The term вҖңsacrificeвҖқ functions as a synecdoche of general (i.e., sacrifice) for specific (i.e., vow offering).
n210056: tn Heb вҖңthe fools, a sacrifice.вҖқ The term вҖңfoolsвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ°ЧЎЦҙЧҷЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, hakkesilim) is an adverbial accusative of comparison (e.g., GKC 375 В§118.r): вҖңrather than giving a sacrifice like foolsвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦҙЧӘЦјЦөЧӘ Ч”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ°ЧЎЦҙЧҷЧңЦҙЧҷЧқ Ч–ЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ, mittet hakkesilim zavakh). Contextually, the вҖңsacrificeвҖқ is a rash vow made to God that is not fulfilled. The rash vow is referred to in 5:2 as the вҖңvoice of a fool.вҖқ Qoheleth admonishes the fool against making a rash vow that is not paid: вҖңWhen you make a vow to God, do not delay in paying it; for God takes no pleasure in fools: Pay what you vow! It is better for you not to vow than to vow and not pay itвҖқ (vv. 4-5 [3-4 HT]).
n210057: tn The term вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝinyan) means вҖңbusiness; affair; task; occupationвҖқ (HALOT 857 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ; BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ). HALOT nuances вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧЁЦ№Ч‘вҖҺ (berov Кҝinyan) as вҖңexcessive activityвҖқ (HALOT 857 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ). Here, it is used as a metonymy of cause (i.e., tasks) for effect (i.e., cares). The term is nuanced variously: (1) literal sense: вҖңbusinessвҖқ (KJV, ASV, YLT, NEB, RSV) and вҖңeffortвҖқ (NASB), and (2) metonymical: вҖңcaresвҖқ (NAB, NIV, NRSV), вҖңconcernsвҖқ (MLB, Douay), вҖңworriesвҖқ (Moffatt) and вҖңbroodingвҖқ (NJPS). The LXX mistakenly related вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ to the root II вҖҸЧўЦёЧ Ц·Ч”вҖҺ (Кҝanah) вҖңto afflict,вҖқ and rendered it as ПҖОөО№ПҒОұПғОјОҝбҝҰ (peirasmou, вҖңtrialвҖқ).
n210058: tn The juxtaposition of the two lines joined by vav (вҖңjust asвҖҰsoвҖҰвҖқ) suggests a comparison (BDB 253 s.v. вҖҸЧ•вҖҺ 1.j); see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 71, В§437.
n210059: tn Heb вҖңvoice.вҖқ The Hebrew term вҖҸЧ§Ч•Ц№ЧңвҖҺ (qol, вҖңvoiceвҖқ) is used as a metonymy of cause (i.e., voice) for the contents (i.e., the thing said), e.g., Gen 3:17; 4:23; Exod 3:18; 4:1, 9; Deut 1:45; 21:18, 20; 1 Sam 2:25; 8:7, 9; 2 Sam 12:18); see HALOT 1084 s.v. вҖҸЧ§Ч•Ц№ЧңвҖҺ 4.b; BDB 877 s.v. вҖҸЧ§Ч•Ц№ЧңвҖҺ 3.a; also E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 545-46. Contextually, this refers to a rash vow made by a fool who made a mistake in making it because he is unable to fulfill it.
n2100510: tn The word вҖңoccursвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100511: tn Heb вҖңvow a vow.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧӘЦјЦҙЧ“ЦјЦ№ЧЁ Ч Ц¶Ч“Ц¶ЧЁвҖҺ (tiddor neder, вҖңto vow a vowвҖқ) is a Hebrew idiom in which the root вҖҸЧ Ч“ЧЁвҖҺ is repeated for emphasis. The construction is a cognate accusative (see IBHS 166-67 В§10.2.1f). The verb вҖҸЧ ЦёЧ“Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ (nadar, вҖңto vowвҖқ) refers to the action of making a solemn promise to the Lord to perform an action or offer a sacrifice, e.g., Lev 27:8; Num 6:21; 30:11; Deut 23:23-24; Jonah 2:10; Mal 1:14; Pss 76:12; 132:2; see HALOT 674 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ч“ЧЁвҖҺ. The noun вҖҸЧ Ц¶Ч“Ц¶ЧЁвҖҺ (вҖңvowвҖқ) was a gift or offering promised to be given to the Lord (Num 30:3; Deut 12:11; 23:19; Isa 19:12; Nah 2:1 HT [1:15 ET]; Ps 61:6, 9 HT [61:5, 8 ET]); see HALOT 674-75 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЦөЧ“Ц¶ЧЁвҖҺ. It usually was a sacrifice or free-will offering (Deut 12:6; Ps 66:13) that was often promised during times of pressure (Judg 11:30; 1 Sam 1:11; 2 Sam 15:7-8; Pss 22:25; 66:13; 116:14, 18; Jonah 2:9).
n2100512: tn The term вҖҸЧңЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦ·ЧңЦјЦ°ЧһЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (leshallemo, preposition plus Piel infinitive construct from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦ·ЧқвҖҺ, shalam plus third person masculine singular suffix) is derived from the root вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧңЧқвҖҺ which is used in a general sense of paying a debt (2 Kgs 4:7; Ps 37:21; Prov 22:27; Job 41:3), and more specifically of fulfilling a vow to the Lord (Deut 23:22; 2 Sam 15:7; Pss 22:26 HT [22:25 ET]; 50:14; 61:9 HT [61:8 ET]; 66:13; 76:12 HT [76:11 ET]; 116:14, 18; Prov 7:14; Job 22:27; Isa 19:21; Jonah 2:10; Nah 2:1); see HALOT 1535 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧңЧқвҖҺ 3a; BDB 1022 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦөЧқвҖҺ 4. An Israelite was never required to make a vow, but once made, it had to be paid (Lev 22:18-25; 27:1-13; Num 15:2-10; Nah 1:15 [2:1 HT]).
n2100513: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (вҖңGodвҖқ) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100514: tn The word вҖңitвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100515: tn Heb вҖңyour flesh.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧЁвҖҺ (basar, вҖңfleshвҖқ) is a synecdoche of part (i.e., flesh) for the whole (i.e., whole person), e.g., Gen 2:21; 6:12; Ps 56:4 [5]; 65:2 [3]; 145:21; Isa 40:5, 6; see HALOT 164 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧЁвҖҺ; E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 642.
n2100516:
tc The MT reads вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧһЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧҗЦёЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (hammalКҫakh, вҖңmessengerвҖқ), while the LXX reads П„ОҝбҝҰ ОёОөОҝбҝҰ (tou theou, вҖңGodвҖқ) which reflects an alternate textual tradition of вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (haКҫelohim, вҖңGodвҖқ). The textual problem was caused by orthographic confusion between similarly spelled words. The LXX might have been trying to make sense of a difficult expression. The MT is preferred as the original. All the major translations follow the MT except for Moffatt (вҖңGodвҖқ).
tn Heb вҖңthe messenger.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧҗЦёЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (malКҫakh, вҖңmessengerвҖқ) refers to a temple priest (e.g., Mal 2:7; cf. HALOT 585 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧҗЦёЧҡЦ°вҖҺ 2.b; BDB 521 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧңЦ°ЧҗЦёЧҡЦ°вҖҺ 1.c). The priests recorded what Israelite worshipers vowed (Lev 27:14-15). When an Israelite delayed in fulfilling a vow, a priest would remind him to pay what he had vowed. Although the traditional rabbinic view is that Qoheleth refers to an angelic superintendent over the temple, Rashi suggested that it is a temple-official. Translations reflect both views: вҖңhis representativeвҖқ (NAB), вҖңthe temple messengerвҖқ (NIV), вҖңthe messengerвҖқ (RSV, NRSV, NASB, MLB, NJPS), вҖңthe angelвҖқ (KJV, ASV, Douay) and вҖңthe angel of GodвҖқ (NEB).
n2100517: tn The Hebrew noun вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч’ЦёЧ’ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (shegagah) denotes вҖңerror; mistakeвҖқ and refers to a sin of inadvertence or unintentional sin (e.g., Lev 4:2, 22, 27; 5:18; 22:14; Num 15:24-29; 35:11, 15; Josh 20:3, 9; Eccl 5:5; 10:5); see HALOT 1412 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч’ЦёЧ’ЦёЧ”вҖҺ; BDB 993 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч’ЦёЧ’ЦёЧ”вҖҺ. In this case, it refers to a rash vow thoughtlessly made, which the foolish worshiper claims was a mistake (e.g., Prov 20:25).
n2100518: tn Heb вҖңat your voice.вҖқ This is an example of metonymy (i.e., your voice) of association (i.e., you).
n2100519: tn The syntax of this verse is difficult. Perhaps the best approach is to classify the vav on вҖҸЧ•Ц·Ч”ЦІЧ‘ЦёЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (vahavalim, вҖңfutilitiesвҖқ) as introducing the predicate (e.g., Gen 40:9; 2 Sam 23:3; Prov 10:25; Isa 34:12; Job 4:6; 36:26); BDB 255 s.v. вҖҸЧ•вҖҺ 5.c.Оі: вҖңThere is futilityвҖҰ.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЦ№Ч‘ Ч”ЦІЧңЦ№ЧһЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (verob halomot) is an adverbial modifier (вҖңin many dreamsвҖқ), as is вҖҸЧ“Ц°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқ Ч”Ц·ЧЁЦ°Ч‘ЦјЦөЧ”вҖҺ (devarim harbeh, вҖңmany wordsвҖқ). The vav prefixed to вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧ“Ц°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (udevarim) and the juxtaposition of the two lines suggests a comparison: вҖңjust asвҖҰso alsoвҖҰвҖқ (BDB 253 s.v. вҖҸЧ•вҖҺ 1.j). The English versions reflect a variety of approaches: вҖңIn the multitude of dreams and many words there are also diverse vanitiesвҖқ (KJV); вҖңIn the multitude of dreams there are vanities, and in many wordsвҖқ (ASV); вҖңWhen dreams increase, empty words grow manyвҖқ (RSV); вҖңIn many dreams and follies and many wordsвҖқ (MLB); вҖңIn the abundance of dreams both vanities and words aboundвҖқ (YLT); вҖңWhere there are many dreams, there are many vanities, and words without numberвҖқ (Douay); вҖңMany dreams and words mean many a vain follyвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңMuch dreaming leads to futility and to superfluous talkвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңIn many dreams and in many words there is emptinessвҖқ (NASB); вҖңMuch dreaming and many words are meaninglessвҖқ (NIV); вҖңWith many dreams comes vanities and a multitude of wordsвҖқ (NRSV).
n2100520: tn Alternately, вҖңoppression.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ (Кҝosheq) has a basic two-fold range of meaning: (1) вҖңoppression; brutalityвҖқ (e.g., Isa 54:14); and (2) вҖңextortionвҖқ (e.g., Ps 62:11); see HALOT 897 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ; BDB 799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ. The LXX understands the term as вҖңoppression,вҖқ as the translation ПғП…ОәОҝПҶОұОҪП„ОҜОұОҪ (sukophantian, вҖңoppressionвҖқ) indicates. Likewise, HALOT 897 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ 1 classifies this usage as вҖңoppressionвҖқ against the poor. However, the context of 5:8-9 [7-8 HT] focuses on corrupt government officials robbing people of the fruit of their labor through extortion and the perversion of justice.
n2100521: tn Heb вҖңrobbery.вҖқ The noun вҖҸЧ’ЦөЧ–Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (gezel, вҖңrobberyвҖқ) refers to the wrestling away of righteousness or the perversion of justice (HALOT 186 s.v. вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦөЧ–Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). The related forms of the root вҖҸЧ’Ч–ЧңвҖҺ mean вҖңto rob; to lootвҖқ (HALOT 186 s.v. вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦөЧ–Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). The term вҖңrobberyвҖқ is used as a figure for the perversion of justice (hypocatastasis): just as a thief robs his victims through physical violence, so corrupt government officials вҖңrobвҖқ the poor through the perversion of justice.
n2100523: tn The word вҖңofficialвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100524: sn And there are higher ones over them! This may describe a corrupt system of government in which each level of hierarchy exploits its subordinates, all the way down to the peasants: вҖңSet in authority over the people is an official who enriches himself at their expense; he is watched by a more authoritative governor who also has his share of the spoils; and above them are other officers of the State who likewise have to be satisfiedвҖқ; see A. Cohen, The Five Megilloth (SoBB), 141.
n2100525: tn The phrase вҖңis seizedвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100526: tn The function of the term вҖҸЧ Ц¶ЧўЦұЧ‘ЦёЧ“вҖҺ (neКҝevar, Niphal participle ms from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ, Кҝavar, вҖңto serveвҖқ) has been understood in four ways: (1) adjectival use of the participle, modifying the noun вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ“Ц¶Ч”вҖҺ (sadeh, вҖңfieldвҖқ): вҖңcultivated fieldвҖқ (RSV, NRSV, NJPS, NAB); (2) adjectival use of the participle, modifying вҖҸЧһЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (melekh, вҖңkingвҖқ): вҖңthe king who cultivatesвҖқ (NASB); (3) verbal use of the participle, taking вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ“Ц¶Ч”вҖҺ (вҖңfieldвҖқ) as the subject: вҖңfield is cultivatedвҖқ (NEB); and (4) verbal use of the participle, taking вҖҸЧһЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (вҖңkingвҖқ) as the subject: вҖңthe king is servedвҖқ (KJV, NASB); also вҖңthe king profitsвҖқ (NIV). BDB 713 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ 2 lists both the adjectival and verbal options: вҖңa king for [i.e., devoted to] the cultivated fieldвҖқ and вҖңa king that makes himself servant to the field [i.e., devoted to agriculture].вҖқ HALOT 774 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ suggests the line be rendered as вҖңa king who serves the land.вҖқ In the Qal stem the verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ (Кҝavar) is sometimes used in reference to tribute imposed upon a kingвҖҷs subjects (e.g., Jer 25:14; 27:7; 30:8; Ezek 34:27) and in reference to subjects serving a king (e.g., Judg 9:28, 38; 1 Sam 11:1; 1 Kgs 5:1; 2 Sam 22:44; Jer 27:7; 28:14; 2 Kgs 25:24); cf. BDB 713 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ 3; HALOT 773 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ 3. Likewise, it is also used in reference to tilling the ground (e.g., Gen 2:5; 4:2, 12; 2 Sam 9:10; Isa 30:24; Jer 27:11; Zech 13:5; Prov 12:11; 28:19) and a vineyard or garden (Gen 2:15; Deut 28:39); cf. HALOT 773 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ 3; BDB 713 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ 3.
n2100527: tn The syntax and exegesis of the line is difficult. There are three basic interpretive options: (1) the king takes care of the security of the cultivated land: вҖңin any case, the advantage of a country is that there is a king for the cultivated landвҖқ; (2) the king is in favor of a prosperous agricultural policy: вҖңin any case, the advantage of a country is that there is a king who is obeyed for the sake of the agricultureвҖқ; and (3) the king exploits the poor farmers: вҖңthe produce of the land is [seized] by all, even the king is served by the fields.вҖқ Perhaps the best option in the light of the context is to take the referent of вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (kol, вҖңallвҖқ) to the government officials of 5:8 rather than to the people as a whole. The verse depicts the exploitation of the poor farmers by corrupt government officials. This is reflected in two English versions: вҖңthe increase from the land is taken by all; the king himself profits from the fieldsвҖқ (NIV); вҖңthe profit of the land is among all of them; a cultivated field has a kingвҖқ (RSV margin). On the other hand, the LXX treated the syntax so that the king is viewed in a neutral sense: вҖңThe abundance of the earth is for everyone; the king is dependent on the tilled field.вҖқ. Most English versions deal with the syntax so that the king is viewed in a neutral or positive sense: вҖңthe profit of the earth is for all; the king himself is served by the fieldвҖқ (KJV); вҖңa king who cultivates the field is an advantage to the landвҖқ (NASB); вҖңthis is an advantage for a land: a king for a plowed fieldвҖқ (NRSV); вҖңthe greatest advantage in all the land is his: he controls a field that is cultivatedвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңa country prospers with a king who has controlвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңa king devoted to the field is an advantage to the landвҖқ (MLB); вҖңa king is an advantage to a land with cultivated fieldsвҖқ (RSV); вҖңthe best thing for a country is a king whose own lands are well tilledвҖқ (NEB); and вҖңan advantage for a country in every respect is a king for the arable landвҖқ (NAB). See D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:576-77.
n2100528: tn Heb вҖңsilver.вҖқ The Hebrew term вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ¶ЧЎЦ¶ЧЈвҖҺ (kesef, вҖңsilverвҖқ) refers to вҖңmoneyвҖқ (HALOT 490-91 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ¶ЧЎЦ¶ЧЈвҖҺ 3). It is a synecdoche of specific (i.e., silver) for the general (i.e., money); see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 625-29.
n2100529: sn The Hebrew term вҖңsilverвҖқ (translated вҖңmoneyвҖқ) is repeated twice in this line for rhetorical emphasis.
n2100530: tn The term вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧһЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (hamon, вҖңabundance; wealthвҖқ) has a wide range of meanings: (1) agitation; (2) turmoil; (3) noise; (4) pomp; (5) multitude; crowd = noisy crowd; and (6) abundance; wealth (HALOT 250 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧһЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ 1-6). Here, it refers to abundant wealth (related to вҖңpompвҖқ); cf. HALOT 250 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧһЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ 6, that is, lavish abundant wealth (Ezek 29:19; 30:4; 1 Chr 29:16).
n2100531: tn The phrase вҖңwill never be satisfiedвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity. Note the previous line.
n2100532: tn The word вҖңhisвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100533: tn The word вҖңsomeoneвҖҷsвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100534: tn The term вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (tovah, вҖңgoodвҖқ) connotes вҖңprosperityвҖқ (Deut 23:7; Job 9:25; 21:25; Ps 106:5; Lam 3:17; Eccl 4:8; 5:10, 17; 6:3, 6; 7:14; 9:18; Neh 2:10; Sir 6:11; 41:13); cf. HALOT 372 s.v. вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘ЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2. The related term вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңgoodвҖқ) connotes вҖңprosperityвҖқ as well (Prov 11:10; Job 20:21; 21:16); cf. HALOT 372 s.v. вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘ЦёЧ”вҖҺ 1.b. Here, it refers to the possessions and wealth a person acquires as the fruit of his labors. This nuance is well reflected in several English versions: вҖңThe more a man gains, the more there are to spend itвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңWhen riches multiply, so do those who live off themвҖқ (NEB); вҖңAs his substance increase, so do those who consume itвҖқ (NJPS); and вҖңWhere there are great riches, there are also many to devour themвҖқ (NAB). The line does not describe the economic law of вҖңsupply and demand,вҖқ as some versions seem to imply, e.g., вҖңAs goods increase, so do those who consume themвҖқ (NIV); вҖңWhen goods increase, those who eat them increaseвҖқ (NRSV); cf. also KJV, ASV, RSV, MLB, NASB.
n2100535: tn The form is plural in the Hebrew text, but the plural is one of intensification; it is used here to emphasize the ownerвҖҷs authority over his wealth. See GKC 399 В§124.i. See v 13 as well.
n2100536: tn The rhetorical question is an example of negative affirmation, expecting a negative answer: вҖңThere is no ultimate advantage!вҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 947-48).
n2100537: tn Heb вҖңthere is.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (yesh, вҖңthere isвҖқ) is often used in aphorisms to assert the existence of a particular situation that occurs sometimes. It may indicate that the situation is not the rule but that it does occur on occasion, and may be nuanced вҖңsometimesвҖқ (e.g., Prov 11:24; 13:7, 23; 14:12; 16:25; 18:24; 20:15; Eccl 2:21; 4:8; 5:12; 6:1; 7:15 [2x]; 8:14 [3x]).
n2100538: tn The noun вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah, вҖңevilвҖқ) probably means вҖңmisfortuneвҖқ (HALOT 1263 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 4) or вҖңinjustice, wrongвҖқ (HALOT 1262 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2.b). The phrase вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ” ЧЁЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah rabbah) connotes вҖңgrave injusticeвҖқ or вҖңgreat misfortuneвҖқ (Eccl 2:17; 5:12, 15; 6:1; 10:5).
n2100540: tn Or вҖңthrough a bad business deal.вҖқ The basic meaning of вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝinyan) is вҖңbusiness; affairвҖқ (HALOT 857 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ) or вҖңoccupation; taskвҖқ (BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ). The term is used in a specific sense in reference to business activity (Eccl 8:16), as well as in a more general sense in reference to events that occur on earth (Eccl 1:13; 4:8).
