BDB suggests that the phrase вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·Чҹ ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (Кҝinyan raКҝ) in 5:13 refers to a bad business deal (BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ); however, HALOT suggests that it means вҖңbad luckвҖқ (HALOT 857 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ). The English versions reflect the same two approaches: (1) bad luck: вҖңsome misfortuneвҖқ (NAB, NIV) and (2) a bad business deal: вҖңa bad investmentвҖқ (NASB), вҖңa bad ventureвҖқ (RSV, NRSV, MLB), вҖңsome unlucky ventureвҖқ (Moffatt, NJPS), вҖңan unlucky ventureвҖқ (NEB), вҖңan evil adventureвҖқ (ASV).
n2100541: tn Heb вҖңthere is nothing in his hand.вҖқ
n2100542: tn See the note on the phrase вҖңmisfortuneвҖқ in v. 13.
n2100543: tn Heb вҖңall his days.вҖқ The phrase вҖңof his lifeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100544: tn Heb вҖңBehold, that which I have seen, I, good which is beautiful.вҖқ The phrase вҖңfor peopleвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100545: sn The phrase вҖңto eat and to drinkвҖқ is a common idiom in Ecclesiastes for a person enjoying the fruit of his labor (e.g., 2:24; 3:13).
n2100546: tn Heb вҖңhis,вҖқ and three times later in the verse.
n2100549: tn The term вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧңЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ (kheleq, вҖңlotвҖқ) has a wide range of meanings: (1) вҖңshare of spoilsвҖқ (Gen 14:24; Num 31:36; 1 Sam 30:24), (2) вҖңportion of foodвҖқ (Lev 6:10; Deut 18:8; Hab 1:16), (3) вҖңportion [or tract] of landвҖқ (Deut 10:9; 12:12; Josh 19:9), (4) вҖңportionвҖқ or вҖңpossessionвҖқ (Num 18:20; Deut 32:9), (5) вҖңinheritanceвҖқ (2 Kgs 9:10; Amos 7:4), (6) вҖңportionвҖқ or вҖңawardвҖқ (Job 20:29; 27:13; 31:2; Isa 17:14) or вҖңprofit; rewardвҖқ (Eccl 2:10, 21; 3:22; 5:17-18; 9:6, 9); see HALOT 323 s.v. II вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧңЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ; BDB 324 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧңЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ. Throughout Ecclesiastes, the term is used in reference to manвҖҷs temporal profit from his labor, his reward from God (e.g., Eccl 3:22; 9:9).
n2100550: tn The syntax of this verse is difficult. The best approach is to view вҖҸЧ”ЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧңЦҙЧҷЧҳЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (hishlito, вҖңhe has given him the abilityвҖқ) as governing the three following infinitives: вҖҸЧңЦ¶ЧҗЦұЧӣЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (leКҫekhol, вҖңto eatвҖқ), вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦёЧ©ЧӮЦөЧҗЧӘвҖҺ (velaseКҫt, вҖңand to liftвҖқ = вҖңto accept [or receive]вҖқ), and вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦҙЧ©ЧӮЦ°ЧһЦ№Ч—Ц·вҖҺ (velismoakh, вҖңand to rejoiceвҖқ). This statement parallels 2:24-26 which states that no one can find enjoyment in life unless God gives him the ability to do so.
n2100551: tn Heb вҖңthis.вҖқ The feminine singular demonstrative pronoun вҖҸЧ–Ц№Ч”вҖҺ (zoh, вҖңthisвҖқ) refers back to all that preceded it in the verse (e.g., GKC 440-41 В§135.p), that is, the ability to enjoy the fruit of oneвҖҷs labor is the gift of God (e.g., Eccl 2:24-26). The phrase вҖңthese thingsвҖқ is used in the translation for clarity.
n2100552: tn The verb вҖҸЧ–ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (zakhar, вҖңto rememberвҖқ) may be nuanced вҖңto call to mind; to think about,вҖқ that is, вҖңto reflect uponвҖқ (e.g., Isa 47:7; Lam 1:9; Job 21:6; 36:24; 40:32 HT [41:8 ET]; Eccl 11:8); cf. BDB 270 s.v. вҖҸЧ–ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ 5; HALOT 270 s.v. I вҖҸЧ–ЧӣЧЁвҖҺ 2.
n2100553: tn The word вҖңfleetingвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100554: tn The term вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧўЦІЧ ЦөЧ”вҖҺ (maКҝaneh, Hiphil participle ms from II вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ, Кҝanah, вҖңto be occupiedвҖқ) refers to activity that keeps a person physically busy and mentally preoccupied, e.g., Eccl 1:13; 3:10; 5:19 (HALOT 854; BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ II). The related noun вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝinyan, вҖңbusiness; occupation; taskвҖқ) refers to activity that keeps man busy and occupies his time, e.g., Eccl 1:13; 2:26; 3:10 (HALOT 857; BDB 775 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧ Ц°ЧҷЦёЧҹвҖҺ). The participle form is used to emphasize durative, uninterrupted, continual action.
n2100555: tn Heb вҖңwith the joy of his heart.вҖқ The words вҖңhe derives from his activityвҖқ do not appear in the Hebrew, but they are added to clarify the TeacherвҖҷs point in light of what he says right before this.
n210061: tn The term вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (yesh, вҖңthere isвҖқ) is often used in aphorisms to assert the existence of a particular situation that occurs sometimes. It may indicate that the situation is not the rule but that it does occur on occasion, and may be nuanced вҖңsometimesвҖқ (Prov 11:24; 13:7, 23; 14:12; 16:25; 18:24; 20:15; Eccl 2:21; 4:8; 5:12; 6:1; 7:15 [2x]; 8:14 [3x]).
n210062: tn The noun вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah, вҖңevilвҖқ) probably means вҖңmisfortuneвҖқ (HALOT 1263 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 4) or вҖңinjustice, wrongвҖқ (HALOT 1262 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2.b); see, e.g., Eccl 2:17; 5:12, 15; 6:1; 10:5.
n210064: tn The word вҖңweighsвҖқ does not appear in Hebrew, but is added in the translation for smoothness.
n210065: tn Heb вҖңit is great upon men.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧЁЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦёЧ” Ч”ЦҙЧҷЧҗ ЧўЦ·ЧңЦҫЧ”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (verabbah hiКҫ Кҝal-haКҫadam) is taken in two basic ways: (1) commonality: вҖңit is common among menвҖқ (KJV, MLB), вҖңit is prevalent among menвҖқ (NASB), вҖңthat is frequent among menвҖқ (Douay). (2) oppressiveness: вҖңit lies heavy upon menвҖқ (RSV, NRSV), вҖңit weighs heavily upon menвҖқ (NEB, NAB, NIV), вҖңit presses heavily on menвҖқ (Moffatt), вҖңit is heavy upon menвҖқ (ASV), and вҖңa grave one it is for manвҖқ (NJPS). The preposition вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (Кҝal, вҖңuponвҖқ) argues against the first in favor of the second; the notion of commonality would be denoted by the preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet, вҖңamongвҖқ). The singular noun вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (Кҫadam) is used as a collective, denoting вҖңmen.вҖқ The article on вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (haКҫadam) is used in a generic sense referring to humankind as a whole; the generic article is often used with a collective singular (IBHS 244 В§13.5.1f).
n210067: tn Heb вҖңThere is no lack in respect to his appetiteвҖқ; or вҖңhis desire lacks nothing.вҖқ
n210068: tn The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ (shalat) in the Qal stem means вҖңto domineer; to dominate; to lord it over; to be master ofвҖқ and in the Hiphil stem вҖңto give power toвҖқ (BDB 1020 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ) and вҖңto grantвҖқ (HALOT 1522 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧңЧҳвҖҺ). God must grant a person the ability to enjoy the fruit of his labor, otherwise a person will not be able to enjoy his possessions and wealth. The ability to partake of the fruit of oneвҖҷs labor and to find satisfaction and joy in it is a gift from God (e.g., Eccl 2:24-26; 3:13; 5:18 [19]; 9:7).
n210069: tn Heb вҖңto eat of it.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧҗЦёЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (Кҫakhal, вҖңto eatвҖқ) functions as a metonymy of association, that is, the action of eating is associated with the enjoyment of the fruit of oneвҖҷs labor (e.g., Eccl 2:24-26; 3:12-13, 22; 5:17-19; 8:15; 9:9).
n2100610: tn The phrase вҖңthe fruit of his laborвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100611: tn Heb вҖңa stranger.вҖқ The Hebrew expression вҖҸЧҗЦҙЧҷЧ© Ч ЦёЧӣЦ°ЧЁЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҫish nokhri, вҖңstrangerвҖқ) sometimes refers not to a foreigner or someone that the person does not know, but simply to someone else other than the subject (e.g., Prov 27:2). In the light of 6:3-6, it might even refer to the manвҖҷs own heirs. The term is used as a synecdoche of species (foreigner for stranger) in the sense of someone else other than the subject: вҖңsomeone elseвҖқ (BDB 649 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӣЦ°ЧЁЦҙЧҷвҖҺ 3).
n2100613: sn Instead, someone else enjoys it. A person may be unable to enjoy the fruit of his/her labor due to an unfortunate turn of events that robs a person of his possessions (5:13-14) or a miserly, lifelong hoarding of oneвҖҷs wealth that robs him of the ability to enjoy what he has worked so hard to acquire (5:15-17). Qoheleth recommends the enjoyment of life and the fruit of oneвҖҷs labor, as God enables (5:18-20). Unfortunately, the ability to enjoy the fruits of oneвҖҷs labor is often thwarted by the obstacles described in 6:1-2 and 6:3-9.
n2100615: tn Heb вҖңthe days of his years are many.вҖқ
n2100616: tn Heb вҖңhe has no burial.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч’Ц·ЧқЦҫЧ§Ц°Ч‘Ч•ЦјЧЁЦёЧ” ЧңЦ№ЧҗЦҫЧ”ЦёЧҷЦ°ЧӘЦёЧ”вҖҺ (vegam qevurah loКҫ hayetah, вҖңhe even has no burialвҖқ) is traditionally treated as part of a description of the manвҖҷs sorry final state, that is, he is deprived of even a proper burial (KJV, NEB, RSV, NRSV, ASV, NASB, NIV, NJPS, MLB, Moffatt). However, the preceding parallel lines suggest that this a hyperbolic protasis: вҖңIf he were to live one hundred yearsвҖҰeven if he were never buried [i.e., were to live forever]вҖҰ.вҖқ A similar idea occurs elsewhere (e.g., Pss 49:9; 89:48). See D. R. Glenn, вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 990.
n2100617: tn The noun вҖҸЧ Ц¶ЧӨЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (nefel) denotes вҖңmiscarriageвҖқ and by metonymy of effect, вҖңstillborn childвҖқ (e.g., Ps 58:9; Job 3:16; Eccl 6:3); cf. HALOT 711. The noun is related to the verb вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӨЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (nafal, вҖңto fall,вҖқ but occasionally вҖңto be bornвҖқ; see Isa 26:18); cf. HALOT 710 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЧӨЧңвҖҺ 5.
n2100618: sn The point of 6:3-6 is that the futility of unenjoyed wealth is worse than the tragedy of being stillborn.
n2100619: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (вҖңthe stillborn childвҖқ) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100620: tn The phrase вҖңinto the worldвҖқ does not appear in Hebrew, but is added in the translation for clarity.
n2100621: sn The birth of the stillborn was in vainвҖ”it did it no good to be born.
n2100622: sn The name of the stillborn is forgotten.
n2100624: tn The word вҖңanythingвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100625: sn The Hebrew term translated rest here refers to freedom from toil, anxiety, and miseryвҖ”part of the miserable misfortune that the miserly man of wealth must endure.
n2100626: tn Heb вҖңDo not all go to the same place?вҖқ The rhetorical question is an example of erotesis of positive affirmation, expecting a positive answer, e.g., Ps 56:13 [14] (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 947). It affirms the fact that both the miserly rich man who lives two thousand years, as well as the stillborn who never lived one day, both go to the same placeвҖ”the grave. And if the miserly rich man never enjoyed the fruit of his labor during his life, his fate was no better than that of the stillborn who never had opportunity to enjoy any of the blessings of life. In a sense, it would have been better for the miserly rich man to have never lived than to have experienced the toil, anxiety, and misery of accumulating his wealth, but never enjoying any of the fruits of his labor.
n2100627: tn The phrase вҖңfor nothing more thanвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100628: tn Heb вҖңAll manвҖҷs work is for his mouth.вҖқ The term вҖңmouthвҖқ functions as a synecdoche of part (i.e., mouth) for the whole (i.e., person), substituting the organ of consumption for the personвҖҷs action of consumption (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 641-43), as suggested by the parallelism with вҖҸЧ Ц¶ЧӨЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (nefesh, вҖңhis appetiteвҖқ).
n2100629: tn The term вҖҸЧ Ц¶ЧӨЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (nefesh, вҖңdesire; appetiteвҖқ) is used as a metonymy of association, that is, the soul is associated with manвҖҷs desires and appetites (BDB 660 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ц¶ЧӨЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ 5.c; 6.a).
n2100630: sn So what advantage does the wise man have over a fool? The rhetorical question in Hebrew implies a negative answer: the wise man has no absolute advantage over a fool in the sense that both will share the same fate: death. Qoheleth should not be misunderstood here as denying that wisdom has no relative advantage over folly; elsewhere he affirms that wisdom does yield some relative benefits in life (7:1-22). However, wisdom cannot deliver one from death.
n2100631: sn As in the preceding parallel line, this rhetorical question implies a negative answer (see the note after the word вҖңfoolвҖқ in the preceding line).
n2100632: tn Heb вҖң What to the pauper who knows to walk before the livingвҖқ; or вҖңhow to get along in life.вҖқ
n2100633: tn The phrase вҖңto be content withвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100634: tn The expression вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧЁЦ°ЧҗЦөЧ” ЧўЦөЧҷЧ Ц·ЧҷЦҙЧқвҖҺ (marКҫeh Кҝenayim, вҖңthe seeing of the eyesвҖқ) is a metonymy of cause (i.e., seeing an object) for effect (i.e., being content with what the eyes can see); see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 552-54.
n2100635: tn Heb вҖңthe roaming of the soul.вҖқ The expression вҖҸЧһЦөЧ”ЦІЧңЦёЧҡЦ°ЦҫЧ ЦёЧӨЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (mehalakh nafesh, вҖңthe roaming of the soulвҖқ) is a metonymy for unfulfilled desires. The term вҖңsoulвҖқ (вҖҸЧ Ц¶ЧӨЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ, nefesh) is used as a metonymy of association for manвҖҷs desires and appetites (BDB 660 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ц¶ЧӨЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ 5.c; 6.a). This also involves the personification of the roving appetite as вҖңrovingвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦөЧ”ЦІЧңЦёЧҡЦ°вҖҺ); see BDB 235 s.v. вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧңЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ II.3.f; 232 I.3.
n2100636: tn The phrase вҖңcontinual longingвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100637: tn The term вҖңlikeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2100639: tn Or вҖңand what a person (Heb вҖңmanвҖқ) is was foreknown.вҖқ
n2100640: tn Heb вҖңhe cannot contend with the one who is more powerful than him.вҖқ The referent of the вҖңthe one who is more powerful than he isвҖқ (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity. The words вҖңwith God about his fateвҖқ have been added for clarity as well.
n2100641: tn Heb вҖңThe more the words, the more the futility.вҖқ
n2100642: tn Or вҖңWhat benefit does man have [in that]?вҖқ
n2100643: tn Heb вҖңFor who knows what is good for a man in life?вҖқ The rhetorical question (вҖңFor who knowsвҖҰ?вҖқ) is a negative affirmation, expecting a negative answer: вҖңFor no one knowsвҖҰ!вҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949-51). The translation renders this rhetorical device as a positive affirmation.
n2100644: tn The vav prefixed to вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦөЧқвҖҺ (veyaКҝasem, conjunction plus Qal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ, Кҝasah, вҖңto doвҖқ plus third person masculine plural suffix) functions in an explanatory or epexegetical sense (вҖңForвҖҰвҖқ).
n2100645: tn The third person masculine plural suffix on the verb вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҷЦ·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦөЧқвҖҺ (veyaКҝasem, conjunction plus Qal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”вҖҺ, Кҝasah, вҖңto doвҖқ plus third person masculine plural suffix) refers to вҖҸЧһЦҙЧЎЦ°ЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЁ ЧҷЦ°ЧһЦөЧҷЦҫЧ—Ц·ЧҷЦјЦөЧҷ Ч”Ц¶Ч‘Ц°ЧңЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (mispar yeme khayye hevlo, вҖңthe few days of his fleeting lifeвҖқ). The suffix may be taken as an objective genitive: вҖңhe spends them [i.e., the days of his life] like a shadowвҖқ (HALOT 891 s.v. I вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”вҖҺ) or as a subjective genitive: вҖңthey [i.e., the days of his life] pass like a shadowвҖқ (BDB 795 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”вҖҺ II.11).
n2100646: tn Heb вҖңWho can tell the man what shall be after him under the sun?вҖқ The rhetorical question (вҖңFor who can tell himвҖҰ?вҖқ) is a negative affirmation, expecting a negative answer: вҖңFor no one can tell himвҖҰ!вҖқ (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 949-51). The translation renders this rhetorical device as a positive affirmation.
n210071: tn Heb вҖңname.вҖқ The Hebrew term вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ (shem, вҖңnameвҖқ) is used metonymically for a personвҖҷs reputation (e.g., Prov 22:1; Deut 22:14, 19; Neh 6:13; also Gen 6:4; 12:2; 2 Sam 7:9; 8:13; 23:18, 22; 1 Chr 5:24; 12:31; 2 Chr 26:15; Neh 9:10; Isa 63:12, 14; Jer 32:20; Ezek 16:14; Dan 9:15); cf. HALOT 1549 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ D.2; BDB 1028 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ 2.b.
n210072: tn The comparative term вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңbetterвҖқ) is repeated throughout 7:1-12. It introduces a series of вҖңBetter-than sayings,вҖқ particularly in 7:1-6 in which every poetic unit is introduced by вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ.
n210073: tn Heb вҖңgood.вҖқ The repetition of вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov, вҖңgoodвҖқ) forms an inclusion (a structural device that rounds off the unit), while the two internal terms вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦјЦ¶ЧһЦ¶ЧҹвҖҺвҖҰвҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ (shem mishemen, вҖңnameвҖҰointmentвҖқ) create a paronomastic wordplay (see the note on the word вҖңperfumeвҖқ). The combination of these two sets of literary devices creates an AB:B'A' chiasm: вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦјЦ¶ЧһЦ¶Чҹ ЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ // вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ вҖҸЧҳЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (tov shem // mishemen tov, e.g., вҖңgood nameвҖқ// вҖңointment goodвҖқ).
n210074: tn Or вҖңoilвҖқ; or вҖңointment.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧһЦ¶ЧҹвҖҺ (shemen) refers to fragrant вҖңperfume; cologne; ointmentвҖқ (Amos 6:6; Eccl 10:1; Song 1:2 [1:3 HT]; 4:10); see HALOT 1568 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧһЦ¶ЧҹвҖҺ A.2.c. Bodily oils were expensive (1 Kgs 17:12; 2 Kgs 2:4). Possession of oils and perfumes was a sign of prosperity (Deut 32:8; 33:24; Job 29:6; Prov 21:17; Ezek 16:13, 20). Wearing colognes and oils was associated with joy (Ps 45:8; Eccl 9:8; Isa 61:3) because they were worn on festive occasions (Prov 27:9). The similar sounding terms вҖңnameвҖқ (вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦөЧқвҖҺ, shem) and вҖңperfumeвҖқ (вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧһЦ¶ЧҹвҖҺ) create a wordplay (paronomasia). See W. G. E. Watson, Classical Hebrew Poetry (JSOTSup), 242-43; J. J. GlГјck, вҖңParonomasia in Biblical Literature,вҖқ Semitics 1 (1970): 50-78; A. Guillaume, вҖңParonomasia in the Old Testament.вҖқ JSS 9 (1964): 282-90; J. M. Sasson, вҖңWordplay in the OT,вҖқ IDBSup 968-70.
n210075: tn The vav prefixed to the form вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧқвҖҺ (veyom) functions in a comparative sense, e.g., Job 5:7; 12:11; 16:21; Prov 25:25 (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 71, В§437).
n210076: tn The word вҖңoneвҖҷsвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n210077: tn The article prefixed to вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧһЦјЦёЧ•Ц¶ЧӘвҖҺ (hammavet, вҖңdeathвҖқ) probably functions in an indefinite possessive sense or in a generic sense: вҖңoneвҖҷs death,вҖқ e.g., Gen 44:2 (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 19, В§86, В§92).
n210078: sn There are two ways to understand this proverb: (1) Happy times (characterized by celebration and вҖңfragrant perfumeвҖқ) teach us less than hard times (вҖңthe day of oneвҖҷs deathвҖқ) which can bring about moral improvement (вҖңa good reputationвҖқ). (2) It is better to come to the end of oneвҖҷs life (вҖңday of oneвҖҷs deathвҖқ) with a good reputation (вҖңa good nameвҖқ) than to merely be starting life (вҖңday of oneвҖҷs birthвҖқ) in an auspicious manner in joy and wealth (вҖңfine perfumeвҖқ). Folly and wickedness could foil a good beginning so that a person ends life as a fool. For example, Solomon began as the wisest man who ever lived, only to end life as one of historyвҖҷs greatest fools.
n210079: tn Heb вҖңhouse of mourning.вҖқ The phrase refers to a funeral where the deceased is mourned.
n2100710:
tn Heb вҖңhouse of drinkingвҖқ; or вҖңhouse of feasting.вҖқ The Hebrew noun вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧӘЦјЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ (mishteh) can denote (1) вҖңfeast; banquet,вҖқ occasion for drinking-bouts (1 Sam 25:36; Isa 5:12; Jer 51:39; Job 1:5; Esth 2:18; 5:14; 8:17; 9:19) or (2) вҖңdrinkвҖқ (exilic/postexilicвҖ”Ezra 3:7; Dan 1:5, 8, 16); see HALOT 653 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧӘЦјЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ 4; BDB 1059 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӘЦ·Ч”вҖҺ.
sn Qoheleth recommended that people soberly reflect on the brevity of life and the reality of death (It is better to go to a house of mourning) than to waste oneвҖҷs life in the foolish pursuit of pleasure (than to go to a house of banqueting). Sober reflection on the brevity of life and reality of death has more moral benefit than frivolous levity.
n2100711: tn Heb вҖңitвҖқ; the referent (вҖңdeathвҖқ) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100712: tn Heb вҖңthe end.вҖқ The noun вҖҸЧЎЧ•Ц№ЧЈвҖҺ (sof) literally means вҖңend; conclusionвҖқ (HALOT 747 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЧ•Ц№ЧЈвҖҺ 1; BDB 693 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЧ•Ц№ЧЈвҖҺ). It is used in this context in reference to death, as the preceding phrase вҖңhouse of mourningвҖқ (i.e., funeral) suggests.
n2100714: tn The imperfect tense verb вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ, yitten (from вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӘЦёЧҹвҖҺ, natan, вҖңto giveвҖқ) functions in a modal sense, denoting obligation, that is, the subjectвҖҷs obligatory or necessary conduct: вҖңshouldвҖқ or вҖңought toвҖқ (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 31-32, В§172; IBHS 508-9 В§31.4g).
n2100715: tn The word вҖңthisвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100717: tn Heb вҖңin sadness of face there is good for the heart.вҖқ
n2100718: tn Or possibly вҖңThough the face is sad, the heart may be glad.вҖқ
n2100719: sn The expression the house of merrymaking refers to a banquet where those who attend engage in self-indulgent feasting and riotous drinking.
n2100721: tn Heb вҖңrebuke of the wise,вҖқ a subjective genitive (вҖңthe wiseвҖқ administer the rebuke).
n2100722: tn Or вҖңpraise.вҖқ The antithetical parallelism between вҖңrebukeвҖқ (вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧўЦІЧЁЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ, gaКҝarat) and вҖңsongвҖқ (вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧЁвҖҺ, shir) suggests that the latter is figurative (metonymy of association) for praise/flattery which is вҖңmusicвҖқ to the ears: вҖңpraise of foolsвҖқ (NEB, NJPS) and вҖңflattery of foolsвҖқ (Douay). However, the collocation of вҖңsongвҖқ (вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧЁвҖҺ) in 7:5 with вҖңlaughterвҖқ (вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦ°Ч—Ц№Ч§вҖҺ, sekhoq) in 7:6 suggests simply frivolous merrymaking: вҖңsong of foolsвҖқ (KJV, NASB, NIV, ASV, RSV, NRSV).
n2100723: tn The term вҖңthornsвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧЎЦјЦҙЧҷЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, hassirim) refers to twigs from wild thorn bushes which were used as fuel for quick heat, but burn out quickly before a cooking pot can be properly heated (e.g., Pss 58:9; 118:12).
n2100724: tn The word вҖңkind of follyвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100725: tn It is difficult to determine whether the Hebrew term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) means вҖңfleetingвҖқ or вҖңuselessвҖқ in this context. The imagery of quick-burning thorns under a cooking pot is ambiguous and can be understood in more than one way: (1) It is useless to try to heat a cooking pot by burning thorns because they burn out before the pot can be properly heated; (2) the heat produced by quick-burning thorns is fleetingвҖ”it produces quick heat, but lasts only for a moment. Likewise, the вҖңlaughter of a foolвҖқ can be taken in both ways: (1) In comparison to the sober reflection of the wise, the laughter of fools is morally useless: the burning of thorns, like the laughter of fools, makes a lot of noise but accomplishes nothing; (2) the laughter of fools is fleeting due to the brevity of life and certainty of death. Perhaps this is an example of intentional ambiguity.
n2100726: tn Or вҖңextortion.вҖқ Scholars debate whether the noun вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ (Кҝosheq, вҖңoppression; extortionвҖқ) in this context denotes вҖңoppressionвҖқ (HALOT 897 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ 1) or вҖңgain of extortionвҖқ (BDB 799 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ 3). The parallelism between вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ and вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧӘЦјЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (mattanah, вҖңbribeвҖқ) seems to suggest the latter, but the prominence of the theme of oppression in 7:8-10 argues for the former. Elsewhere in Ecclesiastes, the noun вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶Ч§вҖҺ denotes вҖңoppressionвҖқ (Eccl 4:1) and вҖңextortionвҖқ (Eccl 5:8 [Heb 5:7]). The LXX rendered it as ПғП…ОәОҝПҶОұОҪП„ОҜОұ (sukophantia, вҖңoppressionвҖқ). English translations are split between these two options: вҖңextortionвҖқ (ASV, MLB, NIV), вҖңoppressionвҖқ (KJV, NAB, NASB, RSV, NRSV, YLT, Douay, Moffatt), as well as вҖңcheatingвҖқ (NJPS) and вҖңslanderвҖқ (NEB).
n2100727: tn Or вҖңOppression drives a wise person crazyвҖқ; or вҖңExtortion drives a wise person crazy.вҖқ The verb III вҖҸЧ”ЧңЧңвҖҺ (вҖңto be foolishвҖқ) denotes вҖңto make foolish; to make a fool out of someone; to make into a madmanвҖқ (Job 12:17; Isa 44:25); cf. HALOT 249 s.v. III вҖҸЧ”ЧңЧңвҖҺ; BDB 239 s.v. II вҖҸЧ”ЧңЧңвҖҺ. It has been handled variously: вҖңmakes a wise man madвҖқ (KJV, NASB); вҖңdrives a wise man crazyвҖқ (NEB); вҖңcan make a fool of a wise manвҖқ (NAB); вҖңmakes the wise man foolishвҖқ (RSV, NRSV); and вҖңturns a wise man into a foolвҖқ (NIV).
n2100728: tn The vav prefixed to вҖҸЧ•ЦҙЧҷЧҗЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦөЧ“вҖҺ (viКҫabbed, вҖңcorruptsвҖқ) may function in a comparative sense, e.g., Job 5:7; 12:11; 16:21; Prov 25:25 (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 71, В§437).
n2100729: tc The text has вҖҸЧ•ЦҙЧҷЧҗЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦөЧ“вҖҺ (viКҫabbed, conjunction plus Piel imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ, Кҫavad, вҖңto destroyвҖқ), but the Dead Sea Scrolls text 4Q109 (Qoha), which reads вҖҸ,Ч•ЧҷЧўЧ•Ч”вҖҺ assumes вҖҸЧ•ЧҷЧўЧ•Ч”вҖҺ вҖңtwistsвҖқ or вҖңpervertsвҖқ (conjunction plus Piel imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧўЦёЧ•ЦёЧ”вҖҺ I, Кҝavah, вҖңto bend; to twist.вҖқ See J. Muilenburg, вҖңA Qoheleth Scroll from Qumran,вҖқ BASOR 135 [1954]: 27). The verb I вҖҸЧўЦёЧ•ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (вҖңto bend; to twistвҖқ) is used in reference to moral perversion (e.g., 2 Sam 7:14; 19:20; 24:17; 1 Kgs 8:47; Job 33:27; Prov 12:8; Jer 9:4); cf. HALOT 796-97 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•Ч”вҖҺ; BDB 730 s.v. I вҖҸЧўЦёЧ•ЦёЧ”вҖҺ. The verb вҖҸЧ•ЧҷЧҗЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ is used similarly in reference to moral corruption, e.g., Eccl 3:6; 9:18; Jer 23:1 (HALOT 3 s.v. I вҖҸЧҗЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ; BDB 2 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч“вҖҺ 2).
n2100730: tn Or вҖңand a bribe drives a person mad.вҖқ The noun вҖҸЧңЦөЧ‘вҖҺ (lev, вҖңheartвҖқ) may be taken as a synecdoche of part (i.e., heart) for the whole (i.e., a person). HALOT 3 s.v. I вҖҸЧҗЧ‘Ч“вҖҺ suggests that вҖҸЧ•ЦҙЧҷЧҗЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦөЧ“ ЧңЦөЧ‘вҖҺ (viКҫabbed lev, вҖңdestroys the heartвҖқ) is an idiom meaning, вҖңdrives a person mad.вҖқ The B-line is taken as a comparison with the preceding A-line. On the other hand, the A-line and B-line might be in synonymous parallelism in which case the two lines could be rendered: вҖңSurely [the gain of] extortion turns a wise man into a fool, and a bribe corrupts the heart.вҖқ On the other hand, the lines could be rendered, вҖңSurely oppression drives a wise man crazy, and a bribe drives a person mad.вҖқ
n2100731: tn The term вҖҸЧ“ЦёЧ‘ЦёЧЁвҖҺ (davar) denotes вҖңmatter; thingвҖқ here rather than вҖңspeech; word,вҖқ as the parallelism with вҖңpatienceвҖқ suggests. The term was misunderstood as вҖңspeech; wordвҖқ by the Vulgate (so also Douay).
n2100734: tn Heb вҖңDo not be hasty in your spirit to become angry.вҖқ
n2100736: tn Heb вҖңthese.вҖқ вҖңDaysвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text as second time, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100737: tn Heb вҖңIt is not from wisdom that you ask about this.вҖқ
n2100738: tn Or вҖңWisdom with an inheritance, is goodвҖқ; or вҖңWisdom is as good as an inheritance.вҖқ This use of the preposition вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ (Кҝim) may denote: (1) accompaniment: вҖңtogether with,вҖқ or (2) comparison: вҖңas good as; like; in comparison toвҖқ (HALOT 839-40 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ; BDB 767-69 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦҙЧқвҖҺ). BDB 767 s.v. 1 suggests the accompaniment nuance вҖңtogether with,вҖқ while HALOT 840 s.v. 2.c suggests the comparative sense вҖңin comparison to.вҖқ The translations are also divided: вҖңwisdom with an inheritance is goodвҖқ (KJV, ASV margin, RSV, NASB, YLT); вҖңwisdom, like an inheritance, is a good thingвҖқ (NIV); вҖңwisdom is as good as an inheritanceвҖқ (ASV, NRSV, MLB, NJPS, Moffatt); вҖңwisdom is better than an inheritanceвҖқ (NEB). Because v. 12 compares wisdom with money (i.e., an inheritance), v. 11 is probably making a comparison as well: вҖңWisdom, like an inheritance, is goodвҖқ (7:11a) = вҖңWisdom provides protection, just as money provides protectionвҖқ (7:12a). The вҖңgood thingвҖқ that wisdomвҖ”like an inheritance or moneyвҖ”provides is protection.
n2100740: tn Heb вҖңwisdom is a shade.вҖқ When used with a predicate nominative in a verbless clause, the preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) which appears twice in the line вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧҰЦөЧң Ч”Ц·Ч—ЦёЧӣЦ°ЧһЦёЧ” Ч‘ЦјЦ°ЧҰЦөЧң Ч”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦёЧЎЦ¶ЧЈвҖҺ (betsel hakhokhmah betsel hakkasef) denotes identity, the so-called bet of essence (HALOT 104 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°вҖҺ 3; BDB 88 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°вҖҺ 1.7; see also R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 45, В§249).
n2100741: tn The term вҖҸЧҰЦөЧңвҖҺ (tsel, вҖңshade, shadowвҖқ) refers to that which provides protection or a shelter from the sun (Gen 19:8; Judg 9:36; Isa 25:5; 32:2; Jer 48:45; Jonah 4:5). It is used often in a figurative sense (hypocatastasis) to connote вҖңprotectionвҖқ from calamity (Num 14:9; Isa 49:2; Hos 14:8; Pss 17:8; 36:8; 57:2; 63:8; 91:1; 121:5; Lam 4:20).
n2100742: tn The phrase вҖңjust asвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness and clarity.
n2100743: tn Heb вҖңWisdom is a shade, money is a shade.вҖқ The repetition of вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧҰЦөЧңвҖҺ (betsel, вҖңshade; protectionвҖқ) suggests that the A-line and B-line function as comparisons. Thus the Hebrew phrases вҖңWisdom is a shade, money is a shadeвҖқ may be nuanced, вҖңWisdom [provides] protection [just as] money [provides] protection.вҖқ This approach is adopted by several translations: вҖңwisdom is a defense, as money is a defenseвҖқ (ASV), вҖңwisdom is protection just as money is protectionвҖқ (NASB), вҖңwisdom like wealth is a defenseвҖқ (Moffatt), вҖңthe protection of wisdom is as the protection of moneyвҖқ (NAB), вҖңthe protection of wisdom is like the protection of moneyвҖқ (RSV, NRSV), вҖңwisdom protects as wealth protectsвҖқ (MLB), and вҖңwisdom is a shelter, as money is a shelterвҖқ (NIV). The comparison is missed by KJV вҖңwisdom is a defense, and money is a defense.вҖқ Less likely is taking вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) in a locative sense: вҖңto be in the shelter of wisdom is to be in the shelter of moneyвҖқ (NJPS).
n2100744: tn The verb вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧҷЦёЧ”вҖҺ (khayah, вҖңto liveвҖқ) in the Piel denotes (1) вҖңto let live; to keep alive; to preserve alive; to allow to live happilyвҖқ (Gen 12:12; Exod 1:17; Num 31:15; Deut 6:24; Josh 9:15; Isa 7:21; Jer 49:11) and (2) вҖңto bring back to lifeвҖқ persons who are ill (Ps 30:4) or deceased (Hos 6:2); HALOT 309 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧҷЦёЧ”вҖҺ. Its parallelism with вҖҸЧҰЦөЧңвҖҺ (tsel, вҖңprotectionвҖқ) indicates that it means вҖңto preserve someoneвҖҷs lifeвҖқ from premature death or calamity. Therefore, вҖңpreserves the lifeвҖқ (RSV, NAB, ASV, NASB, NIV, NJPS) is preferable to вҖңgives life toвҖқ (KJV, Douay, NRSV, YLT).
n2100747: tn Less probable renderings of this line are вҖңGod hath made the one side by side with the otherвҖқ (ASV) and вҖңGod has set the one alongside the otherвҖқ (NEB).
n2100748: tn Heb вҖңanything after him.вҖқ This line is misinterpreted by several versions: вҖңthat man may not find against him any just complaintвҖқ (Douay); вҖңconsequently, man may find no fault with HimвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңso that man cannot find fault with him in anythingвҖқ (NAB).
n2100749: tn The word вҖңlifeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness and clarity.
n2100750: tn As is the case throughout Ecclesiastes, the term вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (hakkol) should be nuanced вҖңbothвҖқ rather than вҖңall.вҖқ
n2100751: tn Heb вҖңThere is.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (yesh, вҖңthere isвҖқ) is often used in aphorisms to assert the existence of a particular situation that occurs sometimes. It may indicate that the situation is not the rule but that it does occur on occasion, and may be nuanced вҖңsometimesвҖқ (Prov 11:24; 13:7, 23; 14:12; 16:25; 18:24; 20:15; Eccl 2:21; 4:8; 5:12; 6:1; 7:15 [2x]; 8:14 [3x]).
n2100753: tn Or вҖңin his righteousness.вҖқ The preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) on the terms вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧҰЦҙЧЁЦ°Ч§Ч•Ц№вҖҺ (betsirqo, вҖңhis righteousnessвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (beraКҝato, вҖңhis evil-doingвҖқ) in the following line are traditionally taken in a locative sense: вҖңin his righteousnessвҖқ and вҖңin his wickednessвҖқ (KJV, NASB, NIV). However, it is better to take the вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) in the adversative sense вҖңin spite ofвҖқ (e.g., Lev 26:27; Num 14:11; Deut 1:32; Isa 5:25; 9:11, 16, 20; 10:4; 16:14; 47:9; Pss 27:3; 78:32; Ezra 3:3); cf. HALOT 104 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°вҖҺ 7; BDB 90 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°вҖҺ 3.7. NJPS renders it well: вҖңSometimes a good man perishes in spite of his goodness, and sometimes a wicked one endures in spite of his wickedness.вҖқ In a similar vein, D. R. Glenn (вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 993-94) writes: вҖңThe word вҖҳinвҖҷ in the phrases вҖҳin his righteousnessвҖҷ and вҖҳin his wickednessвҖҷ can here mean вҖҳin spite of.вҖҷ These phrasesвҖҰargue against the common view that in 7:16 Solomon was warning against legalistic or Pharisaic self-righteousness. Such would have been a sin and would have been so acknowledged by Solomon who was concerned about true exceptions to the doctrine of retribution, not supposed ones (cf. 8:10-14 where this doctrine is discussed again).вҖқ
n2100754: tn Heb вҖңThere is.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧҷЦөЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (yesh, вҖңthere isвҖқ) is often used in aphorisms to assert the existence of a particular situation that occurs sometimes. It may indicate that the situation is not the rule but that it does occur on occasion, and may be nuanced вҖңsometimesвҖқ (Prov 11:24; 13:7, 23; 14:12; 16:25; 18:24; 20:15; Eccl 2:21; 4:8; 5:12; 6:1; 7:15 [2x]; 8:14 [3x]).
n2100756: tn The adjective вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧӘЦөЧЁвҖҺ (yoter) means вҖңtoo much; excessive,вҖқ e.g., 2:15 вҖңexcessively wiseвҖқ (HALOT 404 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧӘЦөЧЁвҖҺ 2; BDB 452 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧӘЦөЧЁвҖҺ). It is derived from the root вҖҸЧҷЦ¶ЧӘЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (yeter, вҖңwhat is left overвҖқ; cf. HALOT 452 s.v. I вҖҸЧҷЦ¶ЧӘЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ) and related to the verb вҖҸЧҷЦёЧӘЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (yatar, Niphal вҖңto be left overвҖқ and Hiphil вҖңto have left overвҖқ; cf. HALOT 451-52). In 2:15 the adjective вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ц№ЧӘЦөЧЁвҖҺ is used with the noun вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (yitron, вҖңadvantage; profitвҖқ) in a wordplay or pun: The wise man has a relative вҖңadvantageвҖқ (вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧӘЦ°ЧЁЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ) over the fool (2:13-14a); however, there is no ultimate advantage because both share the same fateвҖ”death (2:14b-15a). Thus, QohelethвҖҷs acquisition of tremendous wisdom (1:16; 2:9) was вҖңexcessiveвҖқ because it exceeded its relative advantage over folly: it could not deliver him from the same fate as the fool. He strove to obtain wisdom, yet it held no ultimate advantage. Likewise, in 7:16, Qoheleth warns that wisdom and righteous behavior do not guarantee an advantage over wickedness and folly, because the law of retribution is sometimes violated.
n2100757: tn Heb вҖңSo do not be overly righteous and do not be overly wise.вҖқ The Hitpael verb вҖҸЧӘЦјЦҙЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ·ЧқвҖҺ (titkhakkam, from вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧқвҖҺ, khakham, вҖңto be wiseвҖқ) means вҖңto make or show yourself wiseвҖқ (HALOT 314 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЧӣЧқвҖҺ; BDB 314 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧқвҖҺ). The Hitpael may be understood as: (1) benefactive reflexive use which refers to an action done for oneвҖҷs own behalf (e.g., Gen 20:7; Josh 9:12; 1 Kgs 8:33; Job 13:27): because the law of retribution is sometimes violated, it is not wise for a person to be overly dependent upon wisdom or righteousness for his own benefit; (2) estimative-declarative reflexive which denotes esteeming or presenting oneself in a certain state, without regard to the question of truthfulness (e.g., 2 Sam 13:5; Prov 13:6; Esth 8:17): it is useless to overly esteem oneself as wise or to falsely present oneself as wiser than he really is because the law of retribution sometimes fails to reward the wise. The enigma of this lineвҖ”вҖңoverly righteous and overly wiseвҖқвҖ”may be resolved by proper classification of the Hitpael stem of this verb.
n2100759: tn The imperfect of вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦөЧқвҖҺ (shamem) functions in a modal sense, denoting possibility: вҖңyou might beвҖҰвҖқ (see IBHS 508 В§31.4e).
n2100760: tn Or вҖңWhy should you ruin yourself?вҖқ; or вҖңWhy should you destroy yourself?вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦөЧқвҖҺ (shamem) is traditionally taken as вҖңto destroy; to ruin oneself.вҖқ For its use here HALOT 1566 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧһЧқвҖҺ 2 has вҖңto cause oneself ruinвҖқ; BDB 1031 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦөЧқвҖҺ 2 has вҖңcause oneself desolation, ruin.вҖқ Most English versions take a similar approach: вҖңWhy destroy yourself?вҖқ (KJV, ASV, NEB, NRSV, MLB, NIV); вҖңWhy ruin yourself?вҖқ (NAB, NASB). However, in the Hitpolel stem the root вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧһЧқвҖҺ never means this elsewhere, but is always nuanced elsewhere as вҖңto be appalled; to be astonished; to be dumbfounded; to be confounded; to be horrifiedвҖқ (e.g., Ps 143:4; Isa 59:16; 63:5; Dan 8:27); cf. BDB 1031 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦөЧқвҖҺ 1; HALOT 1566 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧһЧқвҖҺ 1. It is taken this way in the English version of the Tanakh: вҖңor you may be dumbfoundedвҖқ (NJPS). Likewise, Cohen renders, вҖңWhy should you be overcome with amazement?вҖқ (A. Cohen, The Five Megilloth [SoBB], 154). If a person was trusting in his own righteousness or wisdom to guarantee prosperity, he might be scandalized by the exceptions to the doctrine of retribution that Qoheleth had observed in 7:15. D. R. Glenn (вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 994) notes: вҖңThis fits in nicely with SolomonвҖҷs argument here. He urged his readers not to be over-righteous or over-wise вҖҳlest they be confounded or astonished.вҖҷ He meant that they should not depend on their righteousness or wisdom to guarantee GodвҖҷs blessing because they might be confounded, dismayed, or disappointed like the righteous people whom Solomon had seen perishing in spite of their righteousness [in 7:15].вҖқ See GKC 149 В§54.c.
n2100762: tn The word вҖңwarningвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation two times in this line for clarity.
n2100763: sn The other warning. Qoheleth is referring to the two words of advice in 7:16-17. He is not, as some suggest, urging his readers to grasp righteousness without letting go of wickedness. His point is not that people should live their lives with a balance of modest righteousness and modest wickedness. Because he urges the fear of God in 7:18b, he cannot be inconsistent in suggesting that his readers offend the fear of God by indulging in some degree of sin in order to counterbalance an overly righteous life. Rather, the proper fear of God will prevent a person from trusting in righteousness and wisdom alone for his security, and it will also prevent indulgence in wickedness and folly.
n2100764: tn Or вҖңwill escape bothвҖқ; or вҖңwill go forth in both.вҖқ The Hebrew phrase вҖҸЧҷЦөЧҰЦөЧҗ ЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧӣЦјЦ»ЧңЦјЦёЧқвҖҺ (yetseКҫ Кҫet kullam, вҖңhe will follow both of themвҖқ) has been interpreted in several ways: (1) To adopt a balanced lifestyle that is moderately righteous while allowing for self-indulgence in moderate wickedness (вҖңto follow both of them,вҖқ that is, to follow both righteousness and wickedness). However, this seems to unnecessarily encourage an antinomian rationalization of sin and moral compromise. (2) To avoid the two extremes of being over-righteous and over-wicked. This takes вҖҸЧҷЦөЧҰЦөЧҗвҖҺ in the sense of вҖңto escape,вҖқ e.g., Gen 39:12, 15; 1 Sam 14:14; Jer 11:11; 48:9; cf. HALOT 426 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЧҰЧҗвҖҺ 6.c; BDB 423 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧҰЦёЧҗвҖҺ 1.d. (3) To follow both of the warnings given in 7:16-17. This approach finds parallels in postbiblical rabbinic literature denoting the action of discharging oneвҖҷs duty of obedience and complying with instruction. In postbiblical rabbinic literature the phrase вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч“ЦөЧҷвҖҺ вҖҸЧҷЦөЧҰЦөЧҗвҖҺ (yetseКҫ yede, вҖңto go out of the hands ofвҖқ) is an idiom meaning вҖңto comply with the requirements of the lawвҖқ (Jastrow 587 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧҰЦёЧҗвҖҺ Hif.5.a). This fits nicely with the context of 7:16-17 in which Qoheleth issued two warnings. In 7:18a Qoheleth exhorted his readers to follow both of his warnings: вҖңIt is best to grasp the first warning without letting go of the second warning.вҖқ The person who fears God will heed both warnings. He will not depend upon his own righteousness and wisdom, but upon GodвҖҷs sovereign bestowal of blessings. Likewise, he will not exploit the exceptions to the doctrine of retribution to indulge in sin, rationalizing sin away just because the wicked sometimes do not get what they deserve.
n2100765: tn Heb вҖңboth.вҖқ The term вҖңwarningsвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity. Alternately, вҖңboth [extremes]вҖқ or вҖңboth [fates].вҖқ The point of this expression is either (1) вҖң he achieves both things,вҖқ (2) вҖңhe escapes all these misfortunes,вҖқ (3) вҖңhe does his duty by both,вҖқ or (4) вҖңhe avoids both extremes.вҖқ See D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:580-81.
n2100767: tn The introductory particle вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (ki) is rendered variously: вҖңforвҖқ (KJV); вҖңindeedвҖқ (NASB); not translated (NIV); вҖңforвҖқ (NJPS). The particle functions in an explanatory sense, explaining the need for wisdom in v. 19. Righteousness alone cannot always protect a person from calamity (7:15-16); therefore, something additional, such as wisdom, is needed. The need for wisdom as protection from calamity is particularly evident in the light of the fact that no one is truly righteous (7:19-20).
n2100768: tn The term вҖңtrulyвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity. Qoheleth does not deny the existence of some people who are relatively righteous.
n2100769: tn Heb вҖңtheyвҖқ; the referent (people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100770: tn Heb вҖңso that you do not hearвҖҰвҖқ; or вҖңlest you hearвҖҰ.вҖқ
n2100771: tn The imperfect tense verb вҖҸЧӘЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧһЦ·ЧўвҖҺ (tishmaКҝ; from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦ·ЧўвҖҺ [shamaКҝ, вҖңto hearвҖқ]) functions in a modal sense, denoting possibility: вҖңyou might hearвҖқ (see IBHS 508 В§31.4e).
n2100773: tn The cohortative вҖҸЧҗЦ¶Ч—Ц°ЧӣЦјЦёЧһЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҫekhkamah, from вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧқвҖҺ, khakham, вҖңto be wiseвҖқ) emphasizes the resolve (determination) of Qoheleth to become wise enough to understand the perplexities of life.
n2100774: tn Or вҖңI am determined to become wiseвҖқ
n2100775: tn Or вҖңbut it eluded meвҖқ; Heb вҖңbut it was far from me.вҖқ
n2100776: tn The word вҖңhumanвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2100778: tn Heb вҖңIt is deep, deepвҖ”who can find it?вҖқ The repetition of the word вҖңdeepвҖқ emphasizes the degree of incomprehensibility. See IBHS 233-34 В§12.5a.
n2100779: tn Heb вҖңI turned, I, even my heart.вҖқ
n2100781: tn The phrase вҖңthe role ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2100782: tn The phrase вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧӣЦ°ЧһЦёЧ” Ч•Ц°Ч—Ц¶Ч©ЧҒЦ°Ч‘ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (khokhmah vekheshbon, вҖңwisdom and the scheme of thingsвҖқ) is a hendiadys (a figure of speech in which two nouns connote one idea): вҖңwisdom in the scheme of things.вҖқ This is similar to the hendiadys вҖҸЧўЦҙЧҰЦјЦ°Ч‘Ч•Ц№Ч ЦөЧҡЦ° Ч•Ц°Ч”ЦөЧЁЦ№Ч ЦөЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (Кҝitsevonekh veheronekh, вҖңpain and childbearingвҖқ) which connotes вҖңpain in childbearingвҖқ (Gen 3:16).
n2100783: tn Or вҖңthe evil of follyвҖқ The genitive construct phrase вҖҸЧЁЦ¶Ч©ЧҒЦ·Чў ЧӣЦјЦ¶ЧЎЦ¶ЧңвҖҺ (reshaКҝ kesel) may be taken as a genitive of attribution (вҖңthe wickedness of follyвҖқ) or as a genitive of attribute (вҖңthe folly of wickednessвҖқ). The English versions treat it in various ways: вҖңwickedness of follyвҖқ (KJV); вҖңwrong of follyвҖқ (YLT); вҖңevil of follyвҖқ (NASB); вҖңstupidity of wickednessвҖқ (NIV); вҖңwickedness, stupidityвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңwickedness is folly [or foolish]вҖқ (ASV, NAB, NRSV, MLB, Moffatt), and вҖңit is folly to be wickedвҖқ (NEB).
n2100784: tn Or вҖңthe folly of madnessвҖқ The genitive construct phrase вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч”Ц·ЧЎЦјЦҙЧӣЦ°ЧңЧ•ЦјЧӘ Ч”Ч•Ц№ЧңЦөЧңЧ•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (vehassikhelut holelot) may be taken as a genitive of attribution (вҖңthe stupidity of wickednessвҖқ) or a genitive of attribute (вҖңthe evil of follyвҖқ). The phrase is rendered variously: вҖңfoolishness and madnessвҖқ (KJV); вҖңfoolishness of madnessвҖқ (NASB); вҖңmadness of follyвҖқ (NIV); вҖңmadness and follyвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңthe foolishness which is madnessвҖқ (NEB); and вҖңfoolishness [or folly] is madnessвҖқ (ASV, NAB, NRSV, MLB, Moffatt).
n2100785: tn The word вҖңthisвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100786: tn The phrase вҖңkind ofвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity (see the following note on the word вҖңwomanвҖқ).
n2100787: tn The article on вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ (haКҫishah) functions in a particularizing sense (вҖңthe kind of womanвҖқ) rather than in a generic sense (i.e., вҖңwomenвҖқ).
n2100788: tn Heb вҖңis snares.вҖқ The plural form вҖҸЧһЦ°ЧҰЧ•Ц№Ч“ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (metsodim, from the noun I вҖҸЧһЦёЧҰЧ•Ц№Ч“вҖҺ, matsod, вҖңsnareвҖқ) is used to connote either intensity, repeated or habitual action, or moral characteristic. For the function of the Hebrew plural, see IBHS 120-21 В§7.4.2. The term II вҖҸЧһЦёЧҰЧ•Ц№Ч“вҖҺ вҖңsnareвҖқ is used in a concrete sense in reference to the hunterвҖҷs snare or net, but in a figurative sense of being ensnared by someone (Job 19:6; Prov 12:12; Eccl 7:26).
n2100789: tn The word вҖңonlyвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100790: tn The word вҖңuprightвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation twice, here and in the following line, for clarity.
n210081: tn The preposition вҖҸЧӣвҖҺ (kaf) prefixed to вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°Ч”Ц¶Ч—ЦёЧӣЦёЧқвҖҺ (kehekhakham, вҖңwise manвҖқ) is traditionally taken in a comparative sense: вҖңWho is like [or as] the wise man?вҖқ On the other hand, it may denote identity, e.g., Gen 1:26; Num 11:1; 1 Sam 20:3; 2 Sam 9:8; Neh 7:2; Job 10:9; Nah 3:6 (see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 47, В§261; IBHS 202-4 В§11.2.9b).
n210082: tn The definite article on вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч—ЦёЧӣЦёЧқвҖҺ (hekhakham, вҖңwise manвҖқ) may be taken in an individualizing (вҖңthe wise manвҖқ) or generic sense (вҖңa wise manвҖқ).
n210083: tn Or вҖңthe explanation.вҖқ The noun вҖҸЧӨЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (pesher) denotes вҖңsolution; explanation; interpretation; meaningвҖқ (HALOT 982-83 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 833 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦөЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ). The Hebrew term is an Aramaic loanword from вҖҸЧӨЦјЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧЁЦёЧҗвҖҺ (pishraКҫ, вҖңdiagnosis; meaning; solutionвҖқ). The Aramaic noun вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (peshar, вҖңinterpretation of a dream or prophecyвҖқ) and verb вҖҸЧӨЦјЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (peshar, вҖңto interpret a dream or prophecyвҖқ) reflect a later meaning not present in Ecclesiastes, but current at the time of Daniel (Dan 2:5-7; 4:3, 15, 16; 5:12, 15, 16; 7:16) and Qumran (e.g., 1QpHab).
n210086:
tc The MT vocalizes the consonantal form вҖҸЧҷЧ©Ч ЧҗвҖҺ as вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦ»Ч ЦјЦ¶ЧҗвҖҺ (yeshunneКҫ, Pual imperfect third person masculine singular from I вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ, shanah, вҖңto changeвҖқ). However, the LXX ОјО№ПғОёбҪөПғОөП„ОұО№ (misthД“setai) reflects an alternate vocalization tradition of вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧ©ЧӮЦјЦёЧ ЦөЧҗвҖҺ (yissaneКҫ, Niphal imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ ЦөЧҗвҖҺ, saneКҫ, вҖңto hateвҖқ), while the VulgateвҖҷs commutabit reflects вҖҸЧҷЦ°Ч©ЧҒЦ·Ч ЦјЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ (yeshanneh, Piel imperfect third person masculine singular from II вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ, shanah, вҖңto repeatвҖқ).
tn Heb вҖңthe strength of his face is changed.вҖқ
n210087: tn Heb вҖңthe strength of his face is changed.вҖқ The expression вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч– ЧӨЦјЦёЧ ЦёЧҷЧ•вҖҺ (Кҝoz panayv, вҖңstrength of his faceвҖқ) is an idiom for вҖңboldness; impudenceвҖқ (BDB 739 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч–вҖҺ 4) or вҖңhard faceвҖқ = harsh countenance (HALOT 805 s.v. I вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч–вҖҺ 1.c).
n210088: tc The Leningrad Codex (the basis of BHS) reads вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ ЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (Кҫani, first person common singular independent personal pronoun): вҖңI obey the kingвҖҷs command.вҖқ Other medieval Hebrew mss and all the versions (LXX, Vulgate, Targum, Syriac Peshitta) preserve an alternate textual tradition of the definite accusative marker вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (Кҫet) introducing the direct object: вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧӨЦјЦҙЧҷЦҫЧһЦ¶ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦ° Ч©ЧҒЦ°ЧһЧ•Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ (Кҫet pi melekh shemor, вҖңObey the command of the kingвҖқ). External evidence supports the alternate textual tradition. The MT is guilty of simple orthographic confusion between similar looking letters. The BHS editors and the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project adopt вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ as the original reading. See D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:582-83.
n210089: tn The phrase вҖңyou tookвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100810: tn The genitive-construct вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ°Ч‘Ч•ЦјЧўЦ·ЧӘ ЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (shevuКҝat Кҫelohim, вҖңan oath of GodвҖқ) functions as a genitive of location (вҖңan oath before GodвҖқ) or an adjectival genitive of attribute (вҖңa supreme oathвҖқ).
n2100811: tn The words вҖңto be loyal to himвҖқ do not appear in the Hebrew text, but are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n2100812: tn Or вҖңdo not stand up for a bad cause.вҖқ
n2100816: tn The word вҖңhisвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness and clarity.
n2100817: tn Heb вҖңthe heart of a wise man.вҖқ
n2100818: tn The term вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ (Кҝet, вҖңtimeвҖқ) connotes вҖңa proper, suitable time for an event; the right momentвҖқ (HALOT 900 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 6; BDB 773 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 2.b); e.g., вҖңit was the time for rainвҖқ (Ezra 10:13); вҖңa time of judgment for the nationsвҖқ (Ezek 30:3); вҖңthere is an appropriate time for every occasionвҖқ (Eccl 3:1); вҖңthe time when mountain goats are bornвҖқ (Job 39:1); вҖңthe rain in its seasonвҖқ (Deut 11:14; Jer 5:24); вҖңthe time for the harvestвҖқ (Hos 2:11; Ps 1:3); вҖңfood in its seasonвҖқ (Ps 104:27).
n2100823: tn Heb вҖңWho can tell him what will be?вҖқ
n2100824: tn Heb вҖңThere is not a man who has mastery over the wind to restrain the wind.вҖқ
n2100825: tn The word вҖңhisвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100826: tn Heb вҖңThere is no discharge in war.вҖқ
n2100828: tn The term вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№ЧҹвҖҺ (naton, Qal infinitive absolute from вҖҸЧ ЦёЧӘЦ·ЧҹвҖҺ, natan, вҖңto giveвҖқ) is a verbal use of the infinitive absolute, used with vav to indicate an action that took place simultaneous to the main verb (see IBHS 596-97 В§35.5.2d). Thus, the clause вҖҸЧ•Ц°Ч ЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№Чҹ ЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧңЦҙЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (venaton Кҫet libbi, вҖңwhile applying my mindвҖҰвҖқ) indicates contemporaneous action to the clause, вҖңAll this I have seenвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЦ¶ЧӘЦҫЧӣЦјЦёЧңЦҫЧ–Ц¶Ч” ЧЁЦёЧҗЦҙЧҷЧӘЦҙЧҷвҖҺ, Кҫet kol zeh raКҫiti). This is view is taken by several translations: вҖңAll this I have seen, having applied my mind toвҖқ (NEB); вҖңAll this I observed while applying my mind toвҖқ (RSV); вҖңAll this I saw, as I applied my mind toвҖқ (NIV); вҖңAll this I saw, as thoughtfully I ponderedвҖқ (Moffatt). On the other hand, the LXX took the vav in a coordinating sense (вҖңandвҖқ) and the infinitive absolute as an independent verb: вҖңI saw all this, and I applied my heartвҖқ. This reading is adopted by other English versions (KJV, NAB, ASV, NASB, NJPS).
n2100831: tn Heb вҖңthat is done under the sun.вҖқ The phrase вҖңthat is done under the sunвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁ Ч Ц·ЧўЦІЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ” ЧӘЦјЦ·Ч—Ц·ЧӘ Ч”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦёЧһЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ, Кҫasher naКҝasah takhat hashamesh) is an idiom for вҖңwhat happens in this worldвҖқ or вҖңon the earthвҖқ (BDB 1039 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧһЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ 4.c). Moffatt renders this idiom, вҖңwhat goes on within this world.вҖқ
n2100832: tn Heb вҖңthe man.вҖқ The article on вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (haКҫadam, вҖңthe manвҖқ) can be taken in a particularizing sense (вҖңone personвҖқ) or in a collective sense as humankind as a whole (вҖңhumankindвҖқ); see HALOT 14 s.v. I вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ 1; BDB 9 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ 2. So LXX: вҖңAll the things in which man has power over [his fellow] man to afflict him.вҖқ This is adopted by the RSV (вҖңman lords it over man to his hurtвҖқ); NJPS (вҖңmen still had authority over men to treat them unjustlyвҖқ); Moffatt (вҖңmen have power over their fellows, power to injure themвҖқ); MLB (вҖңman has mastery over another to harm himвҖқ); and YLT (вҖңman hath ruled over man to his own evilвҖқ). On the other hand, 8:1-9 focuses on the absolute power of the king, so the referent of вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ is probably the king. The article functions in an individualizing, particularizing sense. The particularization of вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ is reflected in many English versions: вҖңone manвҖқ (KJV, ASV, NEB, NAB, Douay), вҖңa manвҖқ (NASB, NIV), and вҖңone personвҖқ (NRSV).
n2100833: tn The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ (shalat) denotes вҖңto domineer; to dominate; to lord it overвҖқ (HALOT 1522 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧңЧҳвҖҺ; BDB 1020 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ). The English versions have: вҖңrule overвҖқ (KJV, YLT, Douay), вҖңhave power overвҖқ (NEB, ASV), вҖңlord it overвҖқ (RSV, NIV), вҖңhave authority overвҖқ (NJPS), вҖңexercise authority overвҖқ (NASB, NRSV); вҖңhave mastery overвҖқ (MLB); вҖңtyrannizeвҖқ (NAB).
n2100834: tn Heb вҖңman.вҖқ The word вҖңotherвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity. The singular noun вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (Кҫadam, вҖңmanвҖқ) functions as a collective singular, connoting вҖңmen, peopleвҖқ (cf. HALOT 14 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ 1; BDB 9 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ 2). The absence of the article might suggest an indefinite rather than an individual, particular sense.
n2100835: tn Heb вҖңa man exercises power over [another] man to his harmвҖқ [or вҖңto his own harmвҖқ]. The third person masculine singular singular pronominal suffix вҖҸЧңЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (lo, вҖңto hisвҖқ) may refer to the antecedent вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (Кҫadam, вҖңmanвҖқ or вҖңmenвҖқ), being understood either in a singular sense (so NEB, RSV, NRSV, NAB, ASV, NASB) or in a collective sense (Moffatt, NJPS, NIV margin). However, the antecedent might be вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЧқвҖҺ (haКҫadam, вҖң[one] manвҖқ = the king) with the suffix functioning reflexively: вҖңto his own harmвҖқ (KJV, ASV margin, YLT, Douay, NIV).
n2100836: tn Heb вҖңThenвҖҰвҖқ The construction вҖҸЧ‘Ц°ЧӣЦөЧҹвҖҺ (vekhen) means вҖңthen; thereupon; on this conditionвҖқ (cf. Eccl 8:10; Esth 4:16; Sir 13:7; see GKC 384 В§119.ii; BDB 486 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ 3.b; HALOT 483 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ 8.c). The line could be rendered, вҖңIt is was then that I saw.вҖқ
n2100837: tc There are three textual options: (1) The MT reads вҖҸЧ§Ц°Ч‘Ц»ЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқ Ч•ЦёЧ‘ЦёЧҗЧ•Цј Ч•ЦјЧһЦҙЧһЦјЦ°Ч§Ч•Ц№ЧқвҖҺ (qevurim vavaКҫu umimmeqom, вҖңthey were buried, and they came, and from the placeвҖқ). вҖҸЧ§Ц°Ч‘Ц»ЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ is a Qal passive participle masculine plural from вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ‘Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ, qavar, вҖңto bury.вҖқ The MT reading is retained by most translations: вҖң[And so I saw the wicked] buried, who had come and gone from the place [of the holy]вҖқ (KJV); вҖң[Then I saw the wicked] buried; they used to go in and out of the [holy] placeвҖқ (RSV, NRSV); вҖң[I saw how the wicked] were buried, who had gone in and out from the [holy] placeвҖқ (MLB); вҖң[I have seen the wicked] buried, those who used to go in and out from the [holy] placeвҖқ (NASB); вҖң[Then too, I saw the wicked] buriedвҖ”those who used to come and go from the [holy] placeвҖқ (NIV); and вҖң[And then I saw] scoundrels coming from the [Holy] Site and being brought to burialвҖқ (NJPS). (2) The LXX reflects the reading вҖҸЧ§Ц°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқ ЧһЧ•ЦјЧ‘ЦёЧҗЦҙЧҷЧқ Ч•ЦјЧһЦҙЧһЦјЦ°Ч§Ч•Ц№ЧқвҖҺ (qevarim muvaКҫim umimmeqom, вҖңto the tombs they are brought, and from the placeвҖқ). The LXX reflects the consonantal text of вҖҸЧ§Ч‘ЧЁЧҷЧқвҖҺ but П„О¬ПҶОҝП…ПӮ (taphous, вҖңtombsвҖқ) reflects a vocalization tradition of вҖҸЧ§Ц°Ч‘ЦёЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (вҖңtombsвҖқ). (3) Several scholars suggest emending the text to вҖҸЧ§ЧЁЧ‘ЧҷЧқ Ч•Ч‘ЧҗЧҷЧқ Ч•ЧһЧһЧ§Ч•ЧқвҖҺ (вҖңapproaching and coming to the placeвҖқ). The emendation involves вҖҸЧ§ЧЁЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (Qal active participle mpl from вҖҸЧ§ЧЁЧ‘вҖҺ вҖңto approach; to draw nearвҖқ). The emendation is adopted by several English versions: вҖңI saw wicked men approach and enterвҖҰthe sacred placeвҖқ (NAB); вҖңI saw wicked men approaching and even entering the holy placeвҖқ (NEB). The emendation makes good sense because вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧЁЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (qarav, вҖңto approach; to draw nearвҖқ) is a synonym to вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•Ц№ЧҗвҖҺ (boКҫ, вҖңto enterвҖқ), and is often used in reference to a person approaching the Lord at the tabernacle or temple. The textual defect would be due to transposition of вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet) and вҖҸЧЁвҖҺ (resh) in вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧЁЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (qarav, вҖңto approachвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧ‘Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ (qavar, вҖңto buryвҖқ). See D. BarthГ©lemy, Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:584.
n2100838: tn The phrase вҖңthe templeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness. Note the reference to the sanctuary in the next line.
n2100840: tc The MT reads вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҷЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧӘЦјЦ·ЧӣЦјЦ°Ч—Ч•ЦјвҖҺ (veyishtakkekhu, вҖңand they were forgottenвҖқ; Hitpael imperfect third person masculine plural from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч—вҖҺ, shakhakh, вҖңto forgetвҖқ). Apart from the MT reading here, the verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч—вҖҺ вҖңto forgetвҖқ never occurs elsewhere in the Hitpael (HALOT 1490 s.v. I вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧӣЧ—вҖҺ; BDB 1013 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч—вҖҺ). Many medieval Hebrew mss read вҖҸЧ•ЧҷЧ©ЧҒЧӘЧ‘ЦјЧ—Ч•вҖҺ вҖңand they boastedвҖқ (Hitpael imperfect third person masculine singular from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ, shavakh, вҖңpraise, boastвҖқ). This alternate textual tradition is reflected in the Greek versions, e.g., Old Greek: ОәОұО№ бјҗПҖбҝғОҪОӯОёО·ПғОұОҪ (kai epД“nethД“san, вҖңand they were praisedвҖқ), Aquila and Theodotion: ОәОұО№ бјҗОәОұП…ПҮбҪөПғОұОҪП„Оҝ (kai ekauchД“santo, вҖңand they boastedвҖқ), and Symmachus: ОәОұО№ бјҗПҖОұО№ОҪОҝПҚОјОөОҪОҝО№ (kai epainoumenoi, вҖңand they were praisedвҖқ). This is also reflected in the Vulgate. The English versions are divided; several follow the MT and translate вҖңthey were forgottenвҖқ (KJV, ASV, NASB, MLB, NJPS), but a good number adopt the alternate textual tradition and translate either вҖңthey were praisedвҖқ or вҖңthey boastedвҖқ (NEB, RSV, NAB, NIV, NRSV). The context of 8:10-17, which focuses on the enigmatic contradictions in divine retribution (sometimes the wicked are not punished), favors the alternate tradition. The wicked boast that they can come and go as they please in the temple, flaunting their irreligion without fearing divine retribution (8:10). This thought is continued in v. 11: failure to execute a sentence against a criminal emboldens the wicked to commit more crimes, confident they will not suffer retribution. It is likely that the original reading of вҖҸЧ•ЧҷЧ©ЧҒЧӘЧ‘Ч—Ч•вҖҺ was confused for вҖҸЧ•ЧҷЧ©ЧҒЧӘЧӣЧ—Ч•вҖҺ because the root вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ (вҖңto praise; to boastвҖқ) is much rarer than the common root вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧӣЦ·Ч—вҖҺ (вҖңto forgetвҖқ). The phrase is best rendered вҖңthey boastedвҖқ (NEB вҖңpriding themselvesвҖқ) rather than вҖңthey were praisedвҖқ (NAB, RSV, NRSV, NIV)вҖ”the verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ means вҖңto praiseвҖқ in Piel, but вҖңto boastвҖқ in Hitpael (Ps 106:47; 1 Chr 16:35; HALOT 1387 s.v. I вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ‘Ч—вҖҺ; BDB 986 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч—вҖҺ). This approach is adopted by the committee for the Jerusalem Hebrew Bible Project: see D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:584-85.
n2100841: tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) here means вҖңenigmatic,вҖқ that is, difficult to grasp mentally. This sense is derived from the literal concept of breath, vapor or wind that cannot be seen; thus, the idea of вҖңobscure, dark, difficult to understand, enigmaticвҖқ (HALOT 236-37 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ; BDB 210-11 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). It is used in this sense in reference to enigmas in life (6:2; 8:10, 14) and the future which is obscure (11:8, 10).
n2100842: tn The particle вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (Кҫasher) is used as a conjunction in a conditional/temporal clause to introduce the protasis (вҖңwhenвҖқ or вҖңifвҖқ), and вҖҸЧўЦ·ЧңЦҫЧӣЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ (Кҝal ken) introduces the apodosis (вҖңthenвҖқ); cf. BDB 83 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ 8.d.
n2100843: tn The noun вҖҸЧӨЦҙЧӘЦ°Ч’ЦёЧқвҖҺ (fitgam, вҖңdecision; announcement; edict; decreeвҖқ) is a loanword from Persian patigama (HALOT 984 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦҙЧӘЦ°Ч’ЦёЧқвҖҺ; BDB 834 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦҙЧӘЦ°Ч’ЦёЧқвҖҺ). The Hebrew noun occurs twice in the OT (Eccl 8:11; Esth 1:20), twice in the Apocrypha (Sir 5:11; 8:9), and five times in Qumran (11QtgJob 9:2; 29:4; 30:1; 34:3; 1QapGen 22:27). The English versions consistently nuance this as a judicial sentence against a crime: вҖңsentenceвҖқ (KJV, NEB, NAB, ASV, NASB, RSV, NRSV, MLB, YLT), вҖңsentence for a crimeвҖқ (NIV), вҖңsentence imposedвҖқ (NJPS), вҖңsentence on a crimeвҖқ (Moffatt).
n2100844: tn Heb вҖңis not done.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦ·Ч”вҖҺ (Кҝasah, вҖңto doвҖқ) refers to a judicial sentence being carried out (HALOT 892 s.v. 2). The Niphal can denote вҖңbe executed; be carried outвҖқ of a sentence (Eccl 8:11) or royal decree (Esth 9:1; BDB 795 s.v. 1.a). Similarly, the Qal can denote вҖңto executeвҖқ vengeance (Judg 11:36) or judgment (1 Sam 28:18; Isa 48:14; Ezek 25:11; 28:26; Ps 149:7, 9; BDB 794 s.v.).
n2100846: tn Heb вҖңthe heart of the sons of man.вҖқ The singular noun вҖҸЧңЦөЧ‘вҖҺ (lev, вҖңheartвҖқ) is used collectively. The term вҖҸЧңЦөЧ‘вҖҺ is often used figuratively (metonymy) in reference to inclinations and determinations of the will (BDB 525 s.v. 4), moral character (BDB 525 s.v. 6), and as a synecdoche for the man himself (BDB 525 s.v. 7).
n2100847: tn Heb вҖңis full to do evil.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ (maleКҫ, вҖңto fillвҖқ) is used figuratively (metonymy): the lack of swift judicial punishment only emboldens the wicked to commit more crimes without fear of retribution. Most English versions translate the term literally: вҖңare filledвҖқ (NIV, MLB, YLT), вҖңis fully setвҖқ (KJV, ASV, RSV, NRSV). However, several versions nuance it figuratively: вҖңemboldenedвҖқ (ASV, NJPS) and вҖңboldlyвҖқ (NEB). Moffatt renders the line, вҖңBecause sentence on a crime is not executed at once, the mind of man is prone to evil practices.вҖқ
n2100853: tn The word вҖңtheirвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100854: tn The phrase вҖңlike a shadowвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ·ЧҰЦјЦөЧңвҖҺ, katsel) modifies the verb (вҖңprolongвҖқ) rather than the noun (вҖңdaysвҖқ). Several English versions misconstrue the line: вҖңhe will not prolong his days, [which are] like a shadowвҖқ (KJV, ASV); вҖңthe man who does not fear God is like a shadowвҖқ (NEB); and вҖңhe will not prolong his shadowy daysвҖқ (NAB). It should be rendered вҖңhe will not prolong his days like a shadowвҖқ (RSV, NRSV, NASB, MLB, NIV). Unlike a shadow that lengthens at sunset, the wicked do not normally live long.
n2100857: tn Heb вҖңthere is.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧҷЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (yesh, вҖңthere isвҖқ) is often used in aphorisms to assert the existence of a particular situation that occurs sometimes. It may indicate that the situation is not the rule but that it does occur on occasion, and may be nuanced вҖңsometimesвҖқ (Prov 11:24; 13:7, 23; 14:12; 16:25; 18:24; 20:15; Eccl 2:21; 4:8; 5:12; 6:1; 7:15 [2x]; 8:14 [3x]).
n2100858: tn The word вҖңanotherвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2100859: tn Or вҖңvanityвҖқ (again at the end of this verse). The Hebrew term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) here denotes вҖңenigma,вҖқ that is, something that is difficult to understand. This sense is derived from the literal referent of breath, vapor or wind that cannot be seen; thus, вҖңobscure; dark; difficult to understand; enigmaticвҖқ (see HALOT 236-37 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ; BDB 210-11 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). It is used in this sense in reference to enigmas in life (6:2; 8:10, 14) and the future which is obscure (11:8, 10).
n2100860: tn Heb вҖңto whom it happens according to the deeds of the wickedвҖқ; or вҖңwho are punished for the deeds of the wicked.вҖқ
n2100861: tn Heb вҖңto whom it happens according to the deeds of the righteousвҖқ; or вҖңwho are rewarded for the deeds of the righteous.вҖқ
n2100862: tn Heb вҖңthe enjoyment.вҖқ The phrase вҖңof lifeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100864: tn The phrase вҖңto doвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100865: tn The construction вҖҸЧҗЦҙЧқвҖҺвҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (ki Кҫim) is used as a particle of exception to limit the preceding clause (вҖңexcept; nothing butвҖқ). See, e.g., Gen 28:17; 39:9; Lev 21:2; Num 14:30; Deut 10:12; 1 Sam 30:22; 2 Kgs 4:2; 5:15; 2 Chr 21:17; Esth 2:15; 5:12; Eccl 3:12; Isa 42:19; Dan 10:21; Mic 6:8 (cf. HALOT 471 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦҙЧқвҖҺ вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ B.2; BDB 474 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦҙЧқвҖҺ вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ 2.a).
n2100866: sn Except to eat, drink, and enjoy life. Qoheleth is not commending a self-indulgent lifestyle of Epicurean hedonism. Nor is he lamenting the absolute futility of life and the lack of eternal retribution. He is submitting to the reality that in a sin-cursed world there is much of human existence marked by relative futility. Since the righteous man cannot assume that he will automatically experience temporal prosperity and blessings on this earth, he shouldвҖ”at the very leastвҖ”enjoy each day to its fullest as a gift from God. D. R. Glenn (вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 997) notes, вҖңEach dayвҖҷs joys should be received as gifts from GodвҖҷs hands and be savored as God permits (3:13; 5:19).вҖқ
n2100867: tn The term вҖңlifeвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2100869: tn Heb вҖңitвҖқ; the referent (enjoyment of life) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100873: tn Heb вҖңand to see the business which is done.вҖқ
n2100874: tn Heb вҖңfor no one sees sleep with their eyes either day or night.вҖқ The construction вҖҸЧ’Ц·ЧқвҖҺвҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷ вҖҺ (ki gam) expresses a concessive sense: вҖңeven thoughвҖқ (e.g., Ps 23:4; Prov 22:6; Eccl 4:14; Isa 1:15; Lam 3:8; Hos 8:10; 9:16); cf. HALOT 196 s.v. вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧқвҖҺ 9; BDB 169 s.v. вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧқвҖҺ 6; 473 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦҙЧҷвҖҺ 2.c.
n2100878: tn Heb вҖңhisвҖқ; the referent (man, in a generic sense) has been specified in the translation as the adjective вҖңhumanвҖқ for clarity.
n2100880: tn The term вҖңitвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2100881: tn The particle вҖҸЧҗЦҙЧқвҖҺ (Кҫim, вҖңeven ifвҖқ) introduces the protasis in a real conditional clause (вҖңIf a wise manвҖҰвҖқ); see IBHS 636-37 В§38.2d; R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 74, В§453.
n2100882: tn The imperfect tense verb вҖҸЧҷЦ№ЧҗЧһЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (yoКҫmar, вҖңto sayвҖқ) functions in a modal sense, denoting possibility (see IBHS 508 В§31.4e; R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 31, В§169).
n2100883: tn Heb вҖңhe cannot findвҖқ; or вҖңhe does not find.вҖқ
n2100884: tn The term вҖңitвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is an implied direct object and has been supplied in the translation for smoothness and stylistic reasons.
n210091: tn Heb вҖңI laid all this to my heart.вҖқ
n210092: tn The term вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦёЧ‘Ч•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ (velavur, conjunction plus Qal infinitive construct from вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ, bur, вҖңto make clearвҖқ) denotes вҖңto examine; to make clear; to clear up; to explainвҖқ (HALOT 116 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘Ч•ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 101 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ). The term is related to Arabic baraw вҖңto examineвҖқ (G. R. Driver, вҖңSupposed Arabisms in the Old Testament,вҖқ JBL 55 [1936]: 108). This verb is related to the Hebrew noun вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ№ЧЁвҖҺ (bor, вҖңcleannessвҖқ) and adjective вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (bar, вҖңcleanвҖқ). The term is used in the OT only in Ecclesiastes (1:13; 2:3; 7:25; 9:1). This use of the infinitive has a connotative sense (вҖңattempting toвҖқ), and functions in a complementary sense, relative to the main verb.
n210093: tn The words вҖңI concluded thatвҖқ do not appear in the Hebrew text, but are supplied in the translation for clarity.
n210095: tn Heb вҖңman does not know anything before them.вҖқ
n210096: tn Heb вҖңall things just as to everyone, one fate.вҖқ
n210097: tc The MT reads simply вҖңthe good,вҖқ but the Greek versions read вҖңthe good and the bad.вҖқ In contrast to the other four pairs in v. 2 (вҖңthe righteous and the wicked,вҖқ вҖңthose who sacrifice, and those who do not sacrifice,вҖқ вҖңthe good manвҖҰthe sinner,вҖқ and вҖңthose who make vowsвҖҰthose who are afraid to make vowsвҖқ), the MT has a triad in the second line: вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЧ•Ц№Ч‘ Ч•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЦёЧ”Ч•Ц№ЧЁ Ч•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЦёЧһЦөЧҗвҖҺ (lattov velattahor velattameКҫ, вҖңthe good, and the clean, and the uncleanвҖқ). This reading in the Leningrad Codex (ca. a.d. 1008)вҖ”the basis of the BHS and BHK publications of the MTвҖ”is also supported by the Ben Asher text of the First Rabbinic Bible (вҖңthe Soncino BibleвҖқ) published in a.d. 1488-94. On the other hand, the Greek version in B (Aquila) has two pairs: вҖңthe good and the bad, and the clean and the unclean.вҖқ Either Aquila inserted ОәОұбҪ¶ П„бҝ· ОәОұОәбҝ· (kai tЕҚ kakЕҚ, вҖңand the badвҖқ) to fill out a pair and to create six parallel pairs in v. 2, or Aquila reflects an early Hebrew textual tradition tradition of вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЧ•Ц№Ч‘ Ч•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (lattov velaraКҝ, вҖңthe good and the badвҖқ). Since Aquila is well known for his commitment to a literalвҖ”at times even a mechanically woodenвҖ”translation of the Hebrew, with no room for improvisation, it is more than likely that Aquila is reflecting an authentic Hebrew textual tradition. Aquila dates to a.d. 130, while the Leningrad Codex dates to a.d. 1008; therefore, the Vorlage of Aquila might have been the original Hebrew textual tradition, being much earlier than the MT of the Leningrad Codex. The alternate textual tradition of Aquila is also seen in the Syriac and Latin versions (but these are dependent upon the Greek = Aquila). On the other hand, the editors of BHK and BHS suggest that the presence of the anomalous вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ was an addition to the Hebrew text, and should be deleted. They also suggest that the Greek pair П„бҝ· бјҖОіОұОёбҝ· ОәОұбҪ¶ П„бҝ· ОәОұОәбҝ· (tЕҚ agathЕҚ kai tЕҚ kakЕҚ, вҖңthe good and the badвҖқ) does not reflect an alternate textual tradition, but that their Vorlage contained only вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ: the Greek version intentionally added ОәОұбҪ¶ П„бҝ· ОәОұОәбҝ· (kai tЕҚ kakЕҚ, вҖңand the badвҖқ) to create a pair. The English versions are divided. Several follow the Greek: вҖңthe good and the bad, the clean and the uncleanвҖқ (NEB, NAB, RSV, NRSV, NIV, Moffatt, NLT). Others follow the Hebrew: вҖңthe good and the clean and the uncleanвҖқ (KJV, ASV, MLB, NJPS). None, however, delete вҖңthe goodвҖқ (вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ) as suggested by the BHK and BHS editors. If the shorter text were original, the addition of ОәОұбҪ¶ П„бҝ· ОәОұОәбҝ· would be intentional. If the longer text were original, the omission of вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (вҖңand the badвҖқ) could have caused by unintentional homoioarcton (вҖңsimilar beginningвҖқ) in the three-fold repetition of вҖҸЧңЧҳвҖҺ in вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧЁЦёЧў Ч•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЦёЧ”Ч•Ц№ЧЁ Ч•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЦёЧһЦөЧҗвҖҺ вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (lattov velaraКҝ velattahor velattameКҫ, вҖңthe good and the bad, the clean and the uncleanвҖқ). The term вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧЁЦёЧўвҖҺ (вҖңand the badвҖқ) was accidentally omitted when a scribe skipped from the first occurrence of вҖҸЧңЧҳвҖҺ in вҖҸЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ to its second occurrence in the word вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧңЦ·ЧҳЦјЦёЧ”Ч•Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ (вҖңthe cleanвҖқ).
n210098: tn Heb вҖңAs is the good (man), so is the sinner.вҖқ
n2100911: tn The term вҖңawaitsвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness and stylistic reasons.
n2100912: tn Heb вҖңalso the heart of the sons of man.вҖқ Here вҖңheartвҖқ is a collective singular.
n2100913: tn Heb вҖңand after that [they go] to [the place of] the dead.вҖқ
n2100914: tn The consonantal text (Kethib) has вҖңis chosen, selected.вҖқ The translation follows the marginal reading (Qere), вҖңis joined.вҖқ See BDB 288 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧ‘Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ Pu.
n2100916: tn Heb вҖңfor their memory is forgotten.вҖқ The pronominal suffix is an objective genitive, вҖңmemory of them.вҖқ
n2100924: tn As discussed in the note on the word вҖңfutileвҖқ in 1:2, the term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) has a wide range of meanings, and should not be translated the same in every place (see HALOT 236-37 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ; BDB 210-11 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). The term is used in two basic ways in OT, literally and figuratively. The literal, concrete sense is used in reference to the wind, manвҖҷs transitory breath, evanescent vapor (Isa 57:13; Pss 62:10; 144:4; Prov 21:6; Job 7:16). In this sense, it is often a synonym for вҖңbreath; windвҖқ (Eccl 1:14; Isa 57:13; Jer 10:14). The literal sense lent itself to the metaphorical sense. Because breath/vapor/wind is transitory and fleeting, the figurative connotation вҖңfleeting; transitoryвҖқ arose (e.g., Prov 31:30; Eccl 6:12; 7:15; 9:9; 11:10; Job 7:16). In this sense, it is parallel to вҖңfew daysвҖқ and вҖң[days] which he passes like a shadowвҖқ (Eccl 6:12). It is used in reference to youth and vigor (11:10) or life (6:12; 7:15; 9:9) which are вҖңtransitoryвҖқ or вҖңfleeting.вҖқ In this context, the most appropriate meaning is вҖңfleeting.вҖқ
n2100925: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100927: tc The phrase вҖҸЧӣЦјЦёЧң ЧҷЦ°ЧһЦөЧҷ Ч”Ц¶Ч‘Ц°ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦёвҖҺ (kol yeme hevlekha, вҖңall your fleeting daysвҖқ) is present in the MT, but absent in the Greek versions, other medieval Hebrew mss, and the Targum. Its appearance in the MT may be due to dittography (repetition: the scribe wrote twice what should have been written once) from вҖҸЧӣЦјЦёЧң ЧҷЦ°ЧһЦөЧҷ Ч—Ц·ЧҷЦјЦөЧҷ Ч”Ц¶Ч‘Ц°ЧңЦ¶ЧҡЦёвҖҺ (kol yeme khayye hevlekha, вҖңall the days of your fleeting lifeвҖқ) which appears in the preceding line. On the other hand, its omission in the alternate textual tradition may be due to haplography (accidental omission of repeated words) with the earlier line.
n2100928: tn Heb вҖңin your toil in which you toil.вҖқ
n2100930: tn Heb вҖңWhatever your hand finds to do.вҖқ
n2100932: tn Or вҖңwhere you are about to go.вҖқ
n2100933: tn Heb вҖңI returned and.вҖқ In the Hebrew idiom, вҖңto return and doвҖқ means вҖңto do again.вҖқ
n2100935: tn The term вҖңalwaysвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation (five times in this verse) for clarity.
n2100939: tn Heb вҖңman.вҖқ The term is used here in a generic sense and translated вҖңno one.вҖқ
n2100940: tn Heb вҖңtime.вҖқ BDB 773 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 2.d suggests that вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ (Кҝet, вҖңtimeвҖқ) refers to an вҖңuncertain time.вҖқ On the other hand, HALOT 901 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 6 nuances it as вҖңdestined time,вҖқ that is, вҖңno one knows his destined time [i.e., hour of destiny].вҖқ It is used in parallelism with вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ (zeman, вҖңappointed time; appointed hourвҖқ) in 3:1 (HALOT 273 s.v. вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ; BDB 273 s.v. вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧһЦёЧҹвҖҺ). Eccl 3:9-15 teaches GodвҖҷs sovereignty over the appointed time-table of human events. Similarly, Qoheleth here notes that no one knows what God has appointed in any situation or time. This highlights the limitations of human wisdom and human ability, as 9:11 stresses.
n2100941: tn Heb вҖңbad, evil.вҖқ The moral connotation hardly fits here. The adjective would seem to indicate that the net is the instrument whereby the fish come to ruin.
n2100943: tn The Masoretes pointed the consonantal form вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ч§Ч©ЧҒЧҷЧқвҖҺ (вҖңare ensnaredвҖқ) as вҖҸЧҷЧ•ЦјЧ§ЦёЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (yuqashim, Pual participle mpl from вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ§Ц№Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ , yaqosh, вҖңto be ensnaredвҖқ). This is an unusual form for a Pual participle: (1) The characteristic doubling of the middle consonant was omitted due to the lengthening of the preceding short vowel from вҖҸЧҷЦ»Ч§ЦјЦёЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ to вҖҸЧҷЧ•ЦјЧ§ЦёЧ©ЧҒЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (GKC 74 В§20.n and 143 В§52.s), and (2) The characteristic prefix вҖҸЧһвҖҺ (mem) is absent, as in a few other Pual participles, e.g., Exod 3:2; Judg 13:8; 2 Kgs 2:10; Isa 30:24; 54:11 (GKC 143 В§52.s). On the other hand, the consonant form вҖҸЧҷЧ•Ч§Ч©ЧҷЧқвҖҺ might actually be an example of the old Qal passive participle which dropped out of Hebrew at an early stage, and was frequently mistaken by the Masoretes as a Pual form (e.g., Jer 13:10; 23:32) (GKC 143 В§52.s). Similarly, the Masoretes pointed вҖҸЧҗЧӣЧңвҖҺ as вҖҸЧҗЦ»ЧӣЦјЦёЧңвҖҺ (Кҫukkal, Pual perfect third person masculine singular вҖңhe was eatenвҖқ); however, it probably should be pointed вҖҸЧҗЦ»ЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (Кҫukhal, old Qal passive perfect third person masculine singular вҖңhe was eatenвҖқ) because вҖҸЧҗЦёЧӣЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (Кҫakhal) only occurs in the Qal (see IBHS 373-74 В§22.6a).
n2100944: tn Heb вҖңevil.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah, вҖңevil; unfortunateвҖқ) is repeated in v. 12 in the two parts of the comparison: вҖңfish are caught in an evil (вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ) netвҖқ and вҖңmen are ensnared at an unfortunate (вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ) time.вҖқ
n2100946: tn The term вҖңburdenвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2100947: tn The verbs in this section function either as past definite actions (describing a past situation) or as hypothetical past actions (describing an imaginary hypothetical situation for the sake of illustration). The LXX uses subjunctives throughout vv. 14-15 to depict the scenario as a hypothetical situation: вҖңSuppose there was a little city, and a few men [lived] in it; and there should come against it a great king, and surround it, and build great siege-works against it; and should find in it a poor wise man, and he should save the city through his wisdom; yet no man would remember that poor man.вҖқ
n2100948: tn The two perfect tense verbs вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧЎЦёЧ‘Ц·Ч‘вҖҺ (vesavav, вҖңhe besiegedвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧ‘ЦёЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (uvanah, вҖңhe builtвҖқ) may be taken in a complementary sense, qualifying the action of the main perfect tense verb вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧ‘ЦёЧҗвҖҺ (uvaКҫ, вҖңhe attacked itвҖқ).
n2100949: tn The root вҖҸЧ’Ч“ЧңвҖҺ (вҖңmighty; strong; largeвҖқ) is repeated in 9:13b for emphasis: вҖңa mighty (вҖҸЧ’ЦёЧ“Ч•Ц№ЧңвҖҺ, gadol) kingвҖҰbuilding strong (вҖҸЧ’Ц°Ч“Ц№ЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, gedolim) siege works.вҖқ This repetition highlights the contrast between the vast power and resources of the attacking king, and the meager resources of the вҖңlittleвҖқ (вҖҸЧ§Ц°ЧҳЦ·Ч ЦјЦёЧ”вҖҺ, qetannah) city with вҖңfewвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦ°ЧўЦёЧҳвҖҺ, meКҝat) men in it to defend it.
n2100950: tn Heb вҖңwas found in itвҖқ; the referent (the city) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2100951: tn Or вҖңhe delivered.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧһЦҙЧңЦјЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ (umillat, from вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦ·ЧҳвҖҺ, malat, вҖңto deliverвҖқ) is functioning either in an indicative sense (past definite action: вҖңhe deliveredвҖқ) or in a modal sense (past potential: вҖңhe could have deliveredвҖқ). The literal meaning of вҖҸЧ–ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (zakhar, вҖңto rememberвҖқ) in the following line harmonizes with the indicative: вҖңbut no one remembered that poor man [afterward].вҖқ However, the modal is supported by v. 16: вҖңA poor manвҖҷs wisdom is despised; no one ever listens to his advice.вҖқ This approach must nuance вҖҸЧ–ЦёЧӣЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (вҖңto rememberвҖқ) as вҖң[no one] listened to [that poor man].вҖқ Most translations favor the indicative approach: вҖңhe deliveredвҖқ or вҖңhe savedвҖқ (KJV, RSV, NRSV, NAB, ASV, NASB, MLB, NIV); however, some adopt the modal nuance: вҖңhe might have savedвҖқ (NEB, NJPS, NASB margin).
n2100954: tn The participle form вҖҸЧ ЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦ°ЧһЦёЧўЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (nishmaКҝim, Niphal participle masculine plural from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧһЦ·ЧўвҖҺ, [shamaКҝ] вҖңto listenвҖқ) is used verbally to emphasize a continual, durative, gnomic action.
n2100955: tn Heb вҖңhis words are never listened to.вҖқ
