n2100956: tn The phrase вҖңis heardвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness. Note its appearance in the previous line.
n210101: tn Heb вҖңflies of death.вҖқ The plural form of вҖңfliesвҖқ (вҖҸЧ–Ц°Ч‘Ч•ЦјЧ‘ЦөЧҷвҖҺ, zevuve) may be taken as a plural of number (вҖңdead fliesвҖқ) or a distributive plural referring to one little fly (вҖңone dead flyвҖқ). The singular form of the following verb and the parallelism support the latter: вҖңone little flyвҖҰso a little folly.вҖқ
n210102: tn The verb вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗЦ·Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ (baКҫash) means вҖңto cause to stink; to turn rancid; to emit a stinking odorвҖқ (e.g., Exod 16:24; Ps 38:6; Eccl 10:1); see HALOT 107 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЧҗЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ 1; BDB 93 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧҗЦ·Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ. It is related to the noun вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧҗЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ (beКҫosh, вҖңstenchвҖқ; Isa 34:3; Joel 2:20; Amos 4:10); cf. HALOT 107 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЧҗЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ; BDB 93 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°ЧҗЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ. The verbal root вҖҸЧ Ч‘ЧўвҖҺ means вҖңto fermentвҖқ or вҖңto emit; to pour out; to bubble; to belch forth; to cause to gush forthвҖқ (HALOT 665 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ч‘ЧўвҖҺ; BDB 615 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЦёЧ‘Ц·ЧўвҖҺ). The two terms вҖҸЧҷЦ·Ч‘Ц°ЧҗЦҙЧҷЧ©ЧҒ ЧҷЦ·Ч‘ЦјЦҙЧҷЧўЦ·вҖҺ (yavКҫish yabbiaКҝ, вҖңto stinkвҖқ and вҖңto fermentвҖқ) create a hendiadys: a figurative expression in which two terms are used to connote one idea: вҖңmakes a rancid stench.вҖқ Several versions treat this as a hendiadys (Old Greek, Symmachus, Targum, Vulgate); however, the Syriac treats them as separate verbs. Most translations treat these as a hendiadys: вҖңDead flies cause the ointment of the apothecary to send forth a stinking savorвҖқ (KJV); вҖңDead flies make a perfumerвҖҷs oil stinkвҖқ (NASB); вҖңdead flies give perfume a bad smellвҖқ (NIV); вҖңDead flies make the perfumerвҖҷs ointment give off an evil odorвҖқ (RSV); Dead flies make the perfumerвҖҷs ointment give off a foul odorвҖқ (NRSV); вҖңDead flies cause a perfumerвҖҷs perfume to send forth a stinkвҖқ (YLT); вҖңDead flies make the perfumerвҖҷs ointment give off a foul odorвҖқ (NRSV). Others render both separately: вҖңDead flies make the perfumerвҖҷs sweet ointment rancid and fermentвҖқ (NEB); вҖңDead flies turn the perfumerвҖҷs ointment fetid and putridвҖқ (NJPS).
n210103:
tn Heb вҖңcarries more weight thanвҖқ; or вҖңis more precious than.вҖқ The adjective вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ§ЦёЧЁвҖҺ (yaqar) denotes вҖңprecious; valuable; costlyвҖқ (HALOT 432 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ§ЦёЧЁвҖҺ 2) or вҖңweighty; influentialвҖқ (BDB 430 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ§ЦёЧЁвҖҺ 4). The related verb denotes вҖңto carry weight,вҖқ that is, to be influential (HALOT 432 s.v. вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ§ЦёЧЁвҖҺ 2). The idea is not that a little folly is more valuable than much wisdom, but that a little folly can have more influence than great wisdom. It only takes one little mistake to ruin a life of great wisdom. The English versions understand it this way: вҖңso a little foolishness is weightier than wisdom and honorвҖқ (NASB); вҖңso a little folly outweighs massive wisdomвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңso a little folly outweighs an abundance of wisdomвҖқ (MLB); вҖңso a little folly outweighs wisdom and honorвҖқ (RSV, NRSV, NIV); вҖңso can a little folly make wisdom lose its worthвҖқ (NEB); вҖңso a little folly annuls great wisdomвҖқ (ASV); вҖңa single slip can ruin much that is goodвҖқ (NAB); вҖңso doth a little folly him that is in reputation for wisdom and honorвҖқ (KJV). The LXX rendered the line rather freely: вҖңa little wisdom is more precious than great glory of folly.вҖқ This does not accurately represent the Hebrew syntax.
sn Qoheleth creates a wordplay by using two Hebrew words for social honor or influence: вҖңweightyвҖқ = honorable (вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ§ЦёЧЁвҖҺ, yaqar) and вҖңheavyвҖқ = honor (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦёЧ‘Ч•Ц№Ч“вҖҺ, kavod).
n210104: tn The MT reads вҖҸЧһЦөЧ—ЦёЧӣЦ°ЧһЦёЧ” ЧһЦҙЧӣЦјЦёЧ‘Ч•Ц№Ч“вҖҺ (mekhokhmah mikkavod, вҖңmore than wisdom, more than honorвҖқ), but several medieval Hebrew mss read вҖҸЧһЦөЧ—ЦёЧӣЦ°ЧһЦёЧ” Ч•ЦјЧһЦҙЧӣЦјЦёЧ‘Ч•Ц№Ч“вҖҺ (mekhokhmah umikkavod, вҖңmore than wisdom and honorвҖқ). However the textual problem is resolved, the two nouns form a hendiadys: two terms joined by vav that describe one concept. The first noun retains its full nominal sense, while the second functions adjectivally: вҖңheavy wisdomвҖқ or better, вҖңgreat wisdom.вҖқ
n210105: tn Heb вҖңa wise manвҖҷs heart is at his right hand.вҖқ The phrase вҖңright handвҖқ is a Hebrew idiom for the place of protection (e.g., Pss 16:8; 110:5; 121:5). In ancient warfare, the shield of the warrior on oneвҖҷs right-hand side protected oneвҖҷs right hand. QohelethвҖҷs point is that wisdom provides protection (e.g., Eccl 7:12).
n210106: tn Heb вҖңand the heart of a fool is at his left hand.вҖқ The fool lacks the protection of wisdom which is at the right-hand side of the wise man (see note on вҖңright handвҖқ in the previous line). The wise manвҖҷs heart (i.e., good sense) protects him, but the fool is always getting into trouble.
n210109: sn A foolвҖҷs lack of wisdom is obvious to everyone, even when he is engaged in the simple, ordinary actions of life.
n2101013: tn Heb вҖңyour place.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧһЦёЧ§Ч•Ц№ЧқвҖҺ (maqom, вҖңplaceвҖқ) denotes a position, post or office (1 Kgs 20:24; Eccl 8:3; 10:4; BDB 879 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦёЧ§Ч•Ц№ЧқвҖҺ 1.c).
n2101014: tn The noun II вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧЁЦ°ЧӨЦјЦөЧҗвҖҺ (marpeКҫ, вҖңcalmnessвҖқ) is used in reference to keeping oneвҖҷs composure with a peaceful heart (Prov 14:30) and responding to criticism with a gentle tongue (Prov 15:4); cf. HALOT 637 s.v. II вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧЁЦ°ЧӨЦјЦөЧҗвҖҺ. It is used in reference to keeping oneвҖҷs composure in an emotionally charged situation (BDB 951 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦ·ЧЁЦ°ЧӨЦјЦөЧҗвҖҺ 2). The term вҖңcalmnessвҖқ is used here as a metonymy of association, meaning вҖңcalm response.вҖқ
n2101015: tn The term вҖңresponseвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarification (see preceding note on the word вҖңcalmвҖқ).
n2101016: tn The verbal root вҖҸЧ Ч•Ч—вҖҺ means вҖңto leave behind; to leave untouchedвҖқ (HALOT 680 s.v. I вҖҸЧ Ч•Ч—вҖҺ 2) in general, and in this passage, вҖңto undoвҖқ or вҖңto allayвҖқ offenses (HALOT 680 s.v. I вҖҸЧ Ч•Ч—вҖҺ 3; BDB 629 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ч•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ 5) or вҖңto avoidвҖқ offenses (BDB 629 вҖҸЧ Ч•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ 5). The point is either that (1) a composed response can calm or appease the anger of the ruler, or (2) a calm heart will help one avoid great sins that would offend the king. The root вҖҸЧ Ч•Ч—вҖҺ (вҖңto restвҖқ) is repeated, creating a wordplay: вҖңDo not leaveвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЦ·ЧңЦҫЧӘЦјЦ·Ч ЦјЦ·Ч—вҖҺ, Кҫal tannakh) and вҖңto avoid; to allayвҖқ (вҖҸЧҷЦ·Ч ЦјЦҙЧҷЧ—Ц·вҖҺ, yanniakh). Rather than resigning (i.e., leaving), composure can appease a king (i.e., cause the anger of the king to leave).
n2101017: tn The term вҖңanotherвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation to indicate that this is not the first вҖңmisfortuneвҖқ described by the Teacher. See 5:13, 16; 6:1-2.
n2101020: tn Heb вҖңlike an error that comes forth from the presence of a ruler.вҖқ
n2101023: tn Or вҖңservants,вҖқ so KJV, ASV, NCV, NLT (also in the following line).
n2101025: tn The four imperfect verbs in vv. 8-9 may be nuanced as indicatives (вҖңwillвҖҰвҖқ) or in a modal sense denoting possibility (вҖңmayвҖҰвҖқ). The LXX rendered them with indicatives, as do many English translations (KJV, RSV, NRSV, ASV, MLB, YLT, NJPS). However, it is better to take them in a modal sense (NEB, NAB, NASB, NIV, NCV, CEV, NLT). One who digs a pit does not necessarily fall into it, but he may under the right conditions.
n2101026: tn Heb вҖңa serpent will bite him.вҖқ The clause вҖңhe who breaks through a wallвҖқ (вҖҸЧ•ЦјЧӨЦ№ЧЁЦөЧҘ Ч’ЦјЦёЧ“ЦөЧЁвҖҺ, uforets gader) is a nominative absoluteвҖ”the casus pendens is picked up by the resumptive pronoun in the following clause вҖңa serpent will bite himвҖқ (вҖҸЧҷЦҙЧ©ЧҒЦјЦ°ЧӣЦ¶Ч ЦјЧ•Цј Ч ЦёЧ—ЦёЧ©ЧҒвҖҺ, yishekhennu nakhash). This construction is used for rhetorical emphasis (see IBHS 76-77 В§4.7c).
n2101027: tn The term вҖңax headвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity. The preceding noun вҖңironвҖқ functions as a metonymy of material (i.e., iron) for the object with which it is associated (i.e., ax head).
n2101028: tn Heb вҖңheвҖқ; the referent (the workman) is implied, and has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2101029: tn The verb вҖҸЧ§ЧңЧңвҖҺ in the Pilpel means вҖңto sharpen; to make a blade sharpвҖқ (HALOT 1104 s.v. вҖҸЧ§ЧңЧңвҖҺ 1).This denominative verb is derived from the rare noun II вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧңЦ·ЧңвҖҺ вҖңsmooth; shinyвҖқ (referring to bronze; Ezek 1:7; Dan 10:6; HALOT 1105 s.v.). Sharpening the blade or head of a bronze ax will make it smooth and shiny. It is not derived from I вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧңЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (qalal) вҖңto treat lightвҖқ or the noun I вҖҸЧ§Ц°ЧңЦёЧңЦёЧ”вҖҺ (qelalah) вҖңcurse.вҖқ Nor is it related to I вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧңЦ·ЧңвҖҺ вҖңto shakeвҖқ (Ezek 21:26); cf. HALOT 1104. BDB 886 s.v. вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧңЦ·ЧңвҖҺ 2 erroneously relates it to I вҖҸЧ§ЦёЧңЦ·ЧңвҖҺ, suggesting вҖңto whetвҖқ or вҖңto move quickly to and fro.вҖқ
n2101031: tn Heb вҖңstrength.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ•Ц·Ч—ЦІЧҷЦёЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (vakhayalim, conjunction + plural noun from вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧҷЦҙЧңвҖҺ, khayil, вҖңstrength; efficiencyвҖқ) is an example of a plural of intensification (GKC 397-98 В§124.e). The point is that it is a waste of a great deal of strength and energy. If a person is not smart, he will have to use a lot of energy and waste his efficiency.
n2101034: tn Heb вҖңhas no profitвҖқ; ASV, NAB, NRSV вҖңthere is no advantage.вҖқ
n2101036: tn The phrase вҖңwin himвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2101037: tn Or вҖңare gracious.вҖқ The antithetical parallelism suggests that вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧҹвҖҺ (khen) does not denote вҖңgracious characterвҖқ but вҖң[gain] favorвҖқ (e.g., Gen 39:21; Exod 3:21; 11:3; 12:36; Prov 3:4, 34; 13:15; 22:1; 28:23; Eccl 9:11); cf. HALOT 332 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧҹвҖҺ 2; BDB 336 s.v. вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧҹвҖҺ 2. The LXX, on the other hand, rendered вҖҸЧ—Ц¶ЧҹвҖҺ with ПҮО¬ПҒО№ПӮ (charis, вҖңgraciousвҖқ). The English versions are divided: вҖңare graciousвҖқ (KJV, YLT, ASV, NASB, NIV) and вҖңwin him favorвҖқ (NEB, RSV, NRSV, NAB, MLB, NJPS, Moffatt).
n2101039: tn Heb вҖңconsume himвҖқ; or вҖңengulf him.вҖқ The verb I вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧңЦ·ЧўвҖҺ (balaКҝ, вҖңto swallowвҖқ) creates a striking wordplay on the homonymic root II вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧңЦ·ЧўвҖҺ (вҖңto speak eloquentlyвҖқ; HALOT 134-35 s.v вҖҸЧ‘ЧңЧўвҖҺ). Rather than speaking eloquently (II вҖҸЧ‘ЧңЧўвҖҺ, вҖңto speak eloquentlyвҖқ), the fool utters words that are self-destructive (I вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧңЦ·ЧўвҖҺ, вҖңto swallow, engulfвҖқ).
n2101041: sn The terms вҖңbeginningвҖқ and вҖңendвҖқ form a merism, a figure of speech in which two opposites are contrasted to indicate totality (e.g., Deut 6:7; Ps 139:8; Eccl 3:2-8). The words of a fool are madness from вҖңstart to finish.вҖқ
n2101044: tn Heb вҖңand the fool multiplies words.вҖқ This line is best taken as the third line of a tricola encompassing 10:13-14a (NASB, NRSV, NJPS, Moffatt) rather than the first line of a tricola encompassing 10:14 (KJV, NEB, RSV, NAB, ASV, NIV). Several versions capture the sense of this line well: вҖңa fool prates on and onвҖқ (Moffatt) and вҖңYet the fool talks and talks!вҖқ (NJPS).
n2101046: tn The plural form of вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ°ЧЎЦҙЧҷЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (hakkesilim, from вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ°ЧЎЦҙЧҷЧңвҖҺ, kesil, вҖңfoolвҖқ) denotes (1) plural of number: referring to several fools or (2) plural of habitual character or plural of intensity (referring to a single person characterized by a habitual or intense quality of foolishness). The latter is favored because the two verbs in 10:15 are both singular in form: вҖңwearies himвҖқ (вҖҸЧӘЦјЦ°ЧҷЦ·Ч’ЦјЦ°ЧўЦ¶Ч ЦјЧ•ЦјвҖҺ, teyaggeКҝennu) and вҖңhe does [not] knowвҖқ (вҖҸЧңЦ№ЧҗЦҫЧҷЦёЧ“Ц·ЧўвҖҺ, loКҫ yadaКҝ); see GKC 440-41 В§135.p. The article on вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ°ЧЎЦҙЧҷЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ is used in the generic sense.
n2101047: tn This line may be interpreted in one of three ways: (1) вҖңthe labor of fools wearies him because he did not know enough to go to a town,вҖқ referring to the labor of the peasants who had not been able to find a place in town where life was easier; (2) вҖңthe labor of the fools so wearies everyone of them (singular pronoun taken in a distributive sense) so much that he even does not know how to go to town,вҖқ that is, he does not even know how to do the easiest thing in the world; (3) вҖңlet the labor of fools so weary him that he may not even know how to go to town,вҖқ taking the verb as a jussive, describing the foolish man described in 10:12-14. See D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:592-93.
n2101048: tn Heb вҖңhe does not know to go to the city.вҖқ
n2101049: tn Or вҖңa childвҖқ; or вҖңa servant.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ Ц·ЧўЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (naКҝar) has a wide range of meanings (HALOT 707 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ц·ЧўЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 654-55 s.v. II вҖҸЧ Ц·ЧўЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ). Used in reference to age, it may refer to an infant (Exod 2:6; Judg 13:5; 1 Sam 1:22; 4:21; 2 Sam 12:16), a child just weaned (1 Sam 1:24), an adolescent in puberty (1 Sam 16:11), or a young man of marriageable age (Gen 34:19; 2 Sam 14:21; 18:5, 12). Its technical or titular use denotes вҖңservantвҖқ (Num 22:22; Judg 7:10-11; 19:3; 1 Sam 3:9; 2 Sam 16:1; 2 Kgs 4:12, 25; 19:6), вҖңretainer; attendant; followerвҖқ (Gen 14:24; 1 Sam 25:5; 2 Sam 2:14; 2 Kgs 19:6; Isa 37:6; Job 1:15-17; Neh 4:10, 17) and вҖңsoldierвҖқ (1 Kgs 20:15-16). The parallel Ugaritic term is used in reference to physical age (lad; son; youth) and in a technical sense (guild members; servitors; soldiers); see UT 19.445. The LXX rendered it with ОҪОөбҪҪП„ОөПҒОҝПӮ (neЕҚteros, вҖңyouthfulвҖқ). The English versions vary: вҖңchild,вҖқ (KJV, ASV, NASB, MLB, RSV, NRSV margin, NIV margin); вҖңchildishвҖқ (NIV margin); вҖңservantвҖқ or вҖңslaveвҖқ (NEB, NAB, ASV margin, NRSV, NIV); and вҖңlackeyвҖқ (NJPS). When used in reference to rulers, it emphasizes incompetence, naivetГ©, inexperience, and immaturity (Isa 3:4, 9; 1 Kgs 3:7). This use must be understood in the light of the parallel antonym: вҖңson of freemenвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ¶ЧҹЦҫЧ—Ч•Ц№ЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, ben khorim). This suggests вҖңservant,вҖқ that is, one who was not well trained and prepared by noble birth to ascend to the throne.
n2101050: tn Heb вҖңson of noblesвҖқ; or вҖңson of freemen.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ—Ч•Ц№ЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (khorim) is from вҖҸЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ (khor, вҖңnoble one; freemanвҖқ); cf. HALOT 348 s.v. I вҖҸЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 359 s.v. I вҖҸЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ. It is related to the Aramaic noun вҖҸЧ—ЦІЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (kharar, вҖңfreemanвҖқ); Sabean вҖҸЧ—ЧЁвҖҺ (вҖңfreeman; nobleвҖқ); Old South Arabic вҖҸЧ—ЧЁвҖҺ and Arabic hurr (вҖңfreedomвҖқ); cf. HALOT 348 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 359 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ.
n2101051: tn The noun вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ (Кҝet, вҖңpoint in timeвҖқ) has a basic two-fold range of meanings: (1) вҖңtime of an eventвҖқ and (2) вҖңtime for an eventвҖқ (BDB 773 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ). The latter has four sub-categories: (a) вҖңusual time,вҖқ (b) вҖңthe proper, suitable or appropriate time,вҖқ (c) вҖңthe appointed time,вҖқ and (d) вҖңuncertain time.вҖқ Here it connotes вҖңa proper, suitable time for an eventвҖқ (HALOT 900 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 6; BDB 773 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 2.b). Examples of this use include: вҖңit was the time for rainвҖқ (Ezra 10:13); вҖңa time of judgment for the nationsвҖқ (Ezek 30:3); вҖңthere is an appropriate time for every occasionвҖқ (Eccl 3:1); вҖңthe rain in its seasonвҖқ (Deut 11:14; Jer 5:24); вҖңthe time for the harvestвҖқ (Hos 2:11; Ps 1:3); вҖңfood in its seasonвҖқ (Ps 104:27); вҖңthe right momentвҖқ (Eccl 8:5); cf. HALOT 900 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦөЧӘвҖҺ 6.
n2101052: tn Heb вҖңfor strength and not for drunkennessвҖқ; or вҖңas heroes and not as drunkardsвҖқ; or вҖңfor nourishment and not for drunkenness.вҖқ According to HALOT 172 s.v. вҖҸЧ’Ц°Ч‘Ч•ЦјЧЁЧ”вҖҺ 1.d the term вҖҸЧ’Ц°Ч‘Ч•ЦјЧЁЦёЧ”вҖҺ (gevurah, вҖңstrengthвҖқ) may here connote вҖңself-control.вҖқ This tactic is adopted by a few English versions: вҖңwith self-control, and not as drunkardsвҖқ (NEB) and вҖңwith restraint, not with guzzlingвҖқ (NJPS). On the other hand, most English versions render вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦҙЧ’Ц°Ч‘Ч•ЦјЧЁЦёЧ” Ч•Ц°ЧңЦ№Чҗ Ч‘Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦ°ЧӘЦҙЧҷвҖҺ (bigvurah veloКҫ vasheti) in a woodenly literal sense, вҖңfor strength and not for drunkennessвҖқ (YLT, KJV, ASV, NASB, RSV, NRSV, NIV). However, a few attempt to express the idiom clearly: вҖңas stalwarts and not as drunkardsвҖқ (MLB); вҖңstalwart men, not sotsвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңfor vigor and not in drinking boutsвҖқ (NAB); вҖңfor refreshment, and not for riotousnessвҖқ (Douay).
n2101055: tn Heb вҖңbread.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧңЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ (lekhem) is used literally of вҖңbreadвҖқ and figuratively (i.e., by metonymy) for a вҖңfeastвҖқ (BDB 536-37 s.v. вҖҸЧңЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ). BDB suggests that вҖҸЧўЦ№Ч©ЧӮЦҙЧҷЧ” ЧңЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ (Кҝosih lekhem) in Eccl 10:19 means вҖңmake a feastвҖқ (BDB 537 s.v. вҖҸЧңЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ 1.a). This obscure line has occasioned numerous proposals: вҖңa feast is made for laughterвҖқ (KJV, ASV, NIV); вҖңfeasts are made for laughterвҖқ (NRSV); вҖңmen feast for merrymakingвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңmen prepare a meal for enjoymentвҖқ (NASB); вҖңthe table has its pleasuresвҖқ (NEB); вҖңthey [i.e., rulers of v. 16] make a banquet for revelryвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңpeople prepare a banquet for enjoymentвҖқ (MLB); вҖңfor laughter they make bread and wine, that the living may feastвҖқ (Douay); вҖңbread is made for laughterвҖқ (RSV); вҖңbread [and oil] call forth merrimentвҖқ (NAB).
n2101056: tn The subject of the verb is not specified. When active verbs have an unspecified subject, they are often used in a passive sense: вҖңBread [feasts] are madeвҖҰ.вҖқ
n2101058: tn Or вҖңand [they think that] money is the answer for everything.вҖқ
n2101059: tn Perhaps the referent is people who are in authority because of their wealth.
n2101062: tn Heb вҖңmight carry the voice.вҖқ The article is used here with the force of a possessive pronoun.
n2101063: tn The Hebrew phrase вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·Чң Ч”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ°Ч ЦёЧӨЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧқвҖҺ (baКҝal hakkenafayim, вҖңpossessor of wingsвҖқ) is an idiom for a winged creature, that is, a bird (e.g., Prov 1:17; see HALOT 143 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ A.6; BDB 127 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ 5.a). The term вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (вҖңmaster; possessorвҖқ) is the construct governing the attributive genitive вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ°Ч ЦёЧӨЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧқвҖҺ (вҖңwingsвҖқ); see IBHS 149-51 В§9.5.3b.
n2101064: tn The term вҖңyourвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n210111: tn The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧңЦ·Ч—вҖҺ (shalakh, вҖңto send; to castвҖқ) refers to the action of sending something to someone (e.g., Neh 8:12; HALOT 1995 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧңЧ—вҖҺ). The term is traditionally rendered here as вҖңcastвҖқ (KJV, NAB, RES, ASV, NASB, NIV); however, some render it вҖңsendвҖқ (NJPS, NRSV, NEB). LXX uses бјҖПҖПҢПғП„ОөО№О»ОҝОҪ (aposteilon, вҖңsendвҖқ).
n210112: tn Heb вҖңyour bread.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧңЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ (lekhem) is traditionally rendered вҖңbreadвҖқ (KJV, NAB, RSV, NRSV, ASV, NASB, NIV, NJPS). However, 11:1-2 seems to deal with exporting goods overseas (D. R. Glenn, вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 1002-3). It is better to take вҖҸЧңЦ¶Ч—Ц¶ЧқвҖҺ (вҖңbreadвҖқ) as a metonymy of product, standing for the grain and wheat from which bread is produced (e.g., Gen 41:54-55; 47:13, 15, 17, 19; 49:20; Num 15:19; 2 Kgs 18:32; Isa 28:28; 30:23; 36:17; 55:10; Jer 5:17; Ezek 48:18; Job 28:5; Ps 104:14; Prov 28:3); see HALOT 526 s.v. 1; BDB 537 s.v. 1.b. It is taken this way by several translations: вҖңgrainвҖқ (NEB) and вҖңgoodsвҖқ (Moffatt). Qoheleth encouraged the export of grain products overseas in international trade.
n210113: tn Heb вҖңupon the surface of the waters.вҖқ This is traditionally viewed as extolling generosity from which a reward will be reaped. On the other hand, some scholars suggest that the imagery deals with commercial business through maritime trade. M. Jastrow took this verse as advice to take risks in business by trusting oneвҖҷs goods or ships that will after many days return with a profit (A. Cohen, The Five Megilloth [SoBB], 181). Sea trade was risky in the ancient Near East, but it brought big returns to its investors (e.g., 1 Kgs 9:26-28; 10:22; Ps 107:23); see D. R. Glenn, вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 1002-3. The verse is rendered thus: вҖңSend your grain across the seas, and in time you will get a returnвҖқ (NEB); or вҖңTrust your goods far and wide at sea, till you get a good return after a whileвҖқ (Moffatt).
n210116: tn The phrase вҖңseven or eightвҖқ is a graded numerical saying depicting an indefinite plurality: вҖңThe collocation of a numeral with the next above it is a rhetorical device employed in numerical sayings to express a number, which need not, or cannot, be more exactly specified. It must be gathered from the context whether such formulae are intended to denote only an insignificant number (e.g., Is 17:6 вҖңtwoвҖқ or at the most вҖңthreeвҖқ) or a considerable number (e.g., Mi 5:4). Sometimes, however, this juxtaposition serves to express merely an indefinite total, without the collateral idea of intensifying the lower by means of the higher numberвҖқ (GKC 437 В§134.s). Examples: вҖңoneвҖқ or вҖңtwoвҖқ (Deut 32:30; Jer 3:14; Job 33:14; 40:5; Ps 62:12); вҖңtwoвҖқ or вҖңthreeвҖқ (2 Kgs 9:32; Isa 17:6; Hos 6:2; Amos 4:8; Sir 23:16; 26:28; 50:25); вҖңthreeвҖқ or вҖңfourвҖқ (Jer 36:23; Amos 1:3-11; Prov 21:19; 30:15, 18; Sir 26:5); вҖңfourвҖқ or вҖңfiveвҖқ (Isa 17:6); вҖңsixвҖқ or вҖңsevenвҖқ (Job 5:19; Prov 6:16); вҖңsevenвҖқ or вҖңeightвҖқ (Mic 5:4; Eccl 11:2).
n210117: tn The word вҖңinvestmentsвҖқ is not in the Hebrew text; it is added here for clarity. This line is traditionally understood as an exhortation to be generous to a multitude of people (KJV, NAB, ASV, NASB, RSV, NRSV, NIV, NJPS); however, it is better taken as shrewd advice to not commit all oneвҖҷs possessions to a single venture (A. Cohen, The Five Megilloth [SoBB], 181). D. R. Glenn (вҖңEcclesiastes,вҖқ BKCOT, 1003) writes: вҖңIn view of the possibility of disaster, a person should make prudent investments in numerous ventures rather than put all his вҖҳeggs in one basketвҖҷ (e.g., Gen 32:7-8 for a practical example of this advice).вҖқ Several translations reflect this: вҖңDivide your merchandise among seven ventures, eight maybeвҖқ (NEB); вҖңTake shares in several venturesвҖқ (Moffatt).
n210118: sn The phrase you do not know is repeated throughout this section (11:2, 5-6). Human beings are ignorant of the future. This should motivate a person to invest their financial resources wisely (11:1-3) and to work diligently (11:4-6).
n210119: tn The term вҖҸЧЁЧўЧ”вҖҺ (lit. вҖңevilвҖқ) refers to calamity (e.g., Eccl 5:13; 7:14; 9:12).
n2101110: sn This proverb criticizes those who are overly cautious. The farmer who waits for the most opportune moment to plant when there is no wind to blow away the seed, and to reap when there is no rain to ruin a ripe harvest, will never do anything but sit around waiting for the right moment.
n2101111: tn Heb вҖңwhat is the way of the wind.вҖқ Some take these words with what follows: вҖңhow the spirit comes to the bones in the womb of a pregnant woman.вҖқ There is debate whether вҖҸЧ”ЦёЧЁЧ•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ вҖҸ ЧһЦ·Ч”ЦҫЧ“ЦјЦ¶ЧЁЦ¶ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ(mah derekh haruakh) refers to the wind (вҖңthe path of the windвҖқ) or the human spirit of a child in the motherвҖҷs womb (вҖңhow the spirit comesвҖқ). The LXX understood it as the wind: вҖңthe way of the windвҖқ (бјЎ бҪҒОҙбҪёПӮ П„ОҝбҝҰ ПҖОҪОөПҚОјОұП„ОҝПӮ, hД“ hodos tou pneumatos); however, the Targum and Vulgate take it as the human spirit. The English versions are divided: (1) spirit: вҖңthe way of the spiritвҖқ (KJV, YLT, Douay); вҖңthe breath of lifeвҖқ (NAB); вҖңhow a pregnant woman comes to haveвҖҰa living spirit in her wombвҖқ (NEB); вҖңhow the lifebreath passes into the limbs within the womb of the pregnant womanвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңhow the spirit comes to the bones in the womb of a woman with childвҖқ (RSV); вҖңhow the breath comes to the bones in the motherвҖҷs wombвҖқ (NRSV); and (2) wind: вҖңthe way of the windвҖқ (ASV, RSV margin); вҖңthe path of the windвҖқ (NASB, NIV); and вҖңhow the wind blowsвҖқ (MLB, Moffatt).
n2101112: tn The term вҖңformвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2101113: tn Heb вҖңthe one who is full.вҖқ The feminine adjective вҖҸЧһЦ°ЧңЦөЧҗЦёЧ”вҖҺ (meleКҫah, from вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ, maleКҫ, вҖңfullвҖқ) is used as a substantive referring to a pregnant woman whose womb is filled with her infant (HALOT 584 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ 2; BDB 571 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ). This term is used in reference to a pregnant woman in later Hebrew (HALOT 584 s.v. вҖҸЧһЦёЧңЦөЧҗвҖҺ). The LXX understood the term in this sense: ОәП…ОҝПҶОҝПҒОҝПҚПғО·ПӮ (kuophorousД“s, вҖңpregnant womanвҖқ).
n2101114: tn Heb вҖңdo not let your hand rest.вҖқ The Hebrew phrase вҖңdo not let your hand restвҖқ is an idiom that means вҖңdo not stop workingвҖқ or вҖңdo not be idleвҖқ (e.g., Eccl 7:18); cf. BDB 628 s.v. вҖҸЧ Ч•ЦјЧ—Ц·вҖҺ B.1. Several English versions capture the sense of the idiom well: вҖңdo not stop workingвҖқ (NEB); вҖңdo not be idleвҖқ (MLB); вҖңlet not your hand be idleвҖқ (NAB); вҖңlet not your hands be idleвҖқ (NIV); вҖңstay not your handвҖқ (Moffatt). The term вҖңhandвҖқ is a synecdoche of part (i.e., do not let your hand rest) for the whole person (i.e., do not allow yourself to stop working).
n2101115: tn The terms вҖңmorningвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ№Ч§Ц¶ЧЁвҖҺ, boqer) and вҖңeveningвҖқ (вҖҸЧўЦ¶ЧЁЦ¶Ч‘вҖҺ, Кҝerev) form a merism (a figure of speech using two polar extremes to include everything in between) that connotes вҖңfrom morning until evening.вҖқ The point is not that the farmer should plant at two times in the day (morning and evening), but that he should plant all day long (from morning until evening). This merism is reflected in several translations: вҖңin the morningвҖҰuntil eveningвҖқ (NEB, Moffatt).
n2101116: tn The term вҖңactivityвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2101117: tn The verb вҖҸЧӣЦјЦёЧ©ЧҒЦөЧЁвҖҺ (kasher, вҖңto prosperвҖқ) is used metonymically to denote вҖңwill succeed.вҖқ In 11:10, it means вҖңskill in work.вҖқ
n2101119: tn The term вҖңlightвҖқ (вҖҸЧҗЧ•Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ, Кҫor) is used figuratively (metonymy of association) in reference to вҖңlifeвҖқ (e.g., Job 3:20; 33:30; Ps 56:14 HT [56:13 ET]). By contrast, death is described as вҖңdarknessвҖқ (e.g., Eccl 11:8; 12:6-7).
n2101120: tn The Hebrew term вҖҸЧһЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№Ч§вҖҺ (matoq, вҖңsweetвҖқ) is often used elsewhere in reference to honey. The point is that life is sweet and should be savored like honey.
n2101121: tn Heb вҖңto the eyes.вҖқ The term вҖңeyesвҖқ is a synecdoche of part (i.e., eyes) for the whole person. Used with the idiom вҖңto see the sunвҖқ (i.e., to be alive), Qoheleth is simply saying that the experience of a life is a pleasant thing that should be savored.
n2101122: tn The expression вҖңto see the sunвҖқ (both вҖҸЧЁЦёЧҗЦёЧ” Ч”ЦёЧ©ЧҒЦјЦёЧһЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ, raКҫah hashamesh, and вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧ–ЦёЧ” Ч”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦёЧһЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ, khazah hashamesh) is an idiom meaning вҖңto be aliveвҖқ (e.g., Ps 58:9; Eccl 6:5; 7:11; 11:7); cf. BDB 1039 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ¶ЧһЦ¶Ч©ЧҒвҖҺ 4.b. The opposite idiom, вҖңthe sun is darkened,вҖқ refers to the onset of old age and death (Eccl 12:2).
n2101123: tn The phrase вҖңthe days of darknessвҖқ refers to the onset of old age (Eccl 12:1-5) and the inevitable experience of death (Eccl 11:7-8; 12:6-7). Elsewhere, вҖңdarknessвҖқ is a figure of speech (metonymy of association) for death (Job 10:21-22; 17:13; 18:18).
n2101124: tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel) here means вҖңobscure,вҖқ that is, unknown. This sense is derived from the literal concept of breath, vapor or wind that cannot be seen; thus, the idea of вҖңobscure; dark; difficult to understand; enigmaticвҖқ (see HALOT 236-37 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ; BDB 210-11 s.v. I вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ). It is used in this sense in reference to enigmas in life (6:2; 8:10, 14) and the future which is obscure (11:8).
n2101125: tn Heb вҖңin your youthвҖқ; or вҖңin your childhood.вҖқ
n2101126: tn Heb вҖңwalk in the ways of your heart.вҖқ
n2101128: tn Heb вҖңand know that concerning all these God will bring you into judgment.вҖқ The point is not that following oneвҖҷs impulses and desires is inherently bad and will bring condemnation from God. Rather the point seems to be: As you follow your impulses and desires, realize that all you think and do will eventually be evaluated by God. So one must seek joy within the boundaries of GodвҖҷs moral standards.
n2101129: tn The verb вҖҸЧЎЧ•ЦјЧЁвҖҺ (sur, вҖңto removeвҖқ) normally depicts a concrete action of removing a physical object from someoneвҖҷs presence (HALOT 748 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЧ•ЧЁвҖҺ 1). Here, it is used figuratively (hypocatastasis) of the emotional/psychological action of banishing unnecessary emotional stress from oneвҖҷs mind. The Hiphil usage means вҖңto remove; to abolish; to keep away; to turn away; to push asideвҖқ (HALOT 748 s.v. 1). The English versions render this term in a variety of ways, none of which is very poetic: вҖңremoveвҖқ (KJV, RSV, ASV, NASB); вҖңturn asideвҖқ (YLT); вҖңward offвҖқ (NAB); and вҖңbanishвҖқ (NEB, MLB, NIV, NRSV, NJPS, Moffatt).
n2101130: tn The root вҖңvexationвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧЎвҖҺ, kaКҫas) has a broad range of meanings: вҖңangerвҖқ (Deut 4:25; 9:18), вҖңirritationвҖқ (Deut 32:21), вҖңoffendвҖқ (2 Kgs 23:26; Neh 3:37 HT [4:5 ET]), вҖңvexationвҖқ or вҖңfrustrationвҖқ (Ezek 20:28), вҖңgriefвҖқ (1 Sam 1:6), and вҖңworryвҖқ (Ps 112:10; Eccl 7:9); cf. HALOT 491 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ·ЧўЦ·ЧЎвҖҺ. Here, it refers in general to unnecessary emotional stress and anxiety that can deprive a person of the legitimate enjoyment of life and its temporal benefits.
n2101132: tn In light of the parallelism, вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah) does not refer to ethical evil, but to physical injury, pain, deprivation or suffering (e.g., Deut 31:17, 21; 32:23; 1 Sam 10:19; Neh 1:3; 2:17; Pss 34:20; 40:13; 88:4; 107:26; Eccl 12:1; Jer 2:27; Lam 3:38); see HALOT 1263 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 4.b; BDB 949 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2. This sense is best captured as вҖңpainвҖқ (NASB, RSV, NRSV, MLB, Moffatt) or вҖңthe troubles [of your body]вҖқ (NEB, NIV), rather than вҖңevilвҖқ (KJV, ASV, YLT, Douay) or вҖңsorrowвҖқ (NJPS).
n2101135: tn Or вҖңyouthвҖқ; Heb вҖңblack hairвҖқ or вҖңthe dawn [of life].вҖқ The feminine noun вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ (hashakharut) is a hapax legomenon, occurring only here. There is debate whether it is from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ (shakhor) which means вҖңblackвҖқ (i.e., black hair, e.g., Lev 13:31, 37; Song 5:11; HALOT 1465 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ—ЧЁвҖҺ; BDB 1007 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ and вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ—Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ) or вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ·Ч—Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ (shakhar) which means вҖңdawnвҖқ (e.g., Gen 19:15; Job 3:9; Song 6:10; HALOT 1466-67 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ—Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ). If this term is from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ—Ц№ЧЁвҖҺ it is used in contrast to gray hair that characterizes old age (e.g., Prov 16:31; 20:29). This would be a figure (metonymy of association) for youthfulness. On the other hand, if the term is from вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦ·Ч—Ц·ЧЁвҖҺ it connotes the вҖңdawn of lifeвҖқ or вҖңprime of life.вҖқ This would be a figure (hypocatastasis) for youthfulness. In either case, the term is a figure for вҖңyouthвҖқ or вҖңprime of life,вҖқ as the parallel term вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°Ч“Ч•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ (hayyaledut, вҖңyouthвҖқ or вҖңchildhoodвҖқ) indicates. The term is rendered variously in the English versions: вҖңblack hairвҖқ (NJPS); вҖңthe dawn of youthвҖқ (NAB); вҖңthe dawn of lifeвҖқ (ASV, MLB, RSV, NRSV); вҖңthe prime of lifeвҖқ (NEB, NASB); вҖңvigorвҖқ (NIV); вҖңyouthвҖқ (KJV); and вҖңmanhoodвҖқ (Moffatt). The plural forms of вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ and вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°Ч“Ч•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ are examples of the plural of state or condition that a person experiences for a temporary period of time, e.g., вҖҸЧ–Ц°Ч§Ц»Ч ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (zequnim, вҖңold ageвҖқ); вҖҸЧ Ц°ЧўЧ•ЦјЧЁЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (neКҝurim, вҖңyouthвҖқ); and вҖҸЧўЦІЧңЧ•ЦјЧһЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (Кҝalumim, вҖңyouthfulnessвҖқ); see IBHS 121 В§7.4.2b.
n2101136: tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel, вҖңvanityвҖқ) often connotes the temporal idea вҖңfleetingвҖқ (e.g., Prov 31:30; Eccl 3:19; 6:12; 7:15; 9:9). This nuance is suggested here by the collocation of вҖңyouthвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧҷЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°Ч“Ч•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ, hayyaldut) and вҖңthe prime of lifeвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦ·Ч—ЦІЧЁЧ•ЦјЧӘвҖҺ, hashakharut).
n210121: tn The imperative вҖҸЧ–Ц°ЧӣЦ№ЧЁвҖҺ (zekhor, вҖңRemember!вҖқ) is a figurative expression (metonymy of association) for obeying God and acknowledging his lordship over oneвҖҷs life (e.g., Num 15:40; Deut 8:18; Pss 42:6-7; 63:6-8; 78:42; 103:18; 106:7; 119:52, 55; Jer 51:50; Ezek 20:43; Jonah 2:7; Mal 4:4). The exhortation to fear God and obey his commands in 12:13-14 spells out what it means to вҖңrememberвҖқ God.
n210122: tn The temporal adjective вҖҸЧўЦ·Ч“вҖҺ (Кҝad, вҖңbeforeвҖқ) appears three times in 12:1-7 (vv. 1b, 2a, 6a). Likewise, the temporal preposition вҖҸЧ‘вҖҺ (bet, вҖңwhenвҖқ) is repeated (vv. 3a, 4b). These seven verses comprise one long sentence in Hebrew: The main clause is 12:1a (вҖңRemember your Creator in the days of your youthвҖқ), while 12:1b-7 consists of five subordinate temporal clauses (вҖңbeforeвҖҰbeforeвҖҰwhenвҖҰwhenвҖҰbeforeвҖҰвҖқ).
n210123: tn The adjective вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ (raКҝah, вҖңevilвҖқ) does not refer here to ethical evil, but to physical difficulty, injury, pain, deprivation and suffering (e.g., Deut 31:17, 21; 32:23; 1 Sam 10:19; Neh 1:3; 2:17; Pss 34:20; 40:13; 88:4; 107:26; Eccl 11:10; Jer 2:27; Lam 3:38); see HALOT 1263 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 4.b; BDB 949 s.v. вҖҸЧЁЦёЧўЦёЧ”вҖҺ 2.
n210124: tn Heb вҖңthe light and the moon and the stars.вҖқ The phrase вҖңthe light and the moonвҖқ is a hendiadys (two separate terms denoting one idea) or perhaps even a hendiatris (three separate terms denoting one idea) for вҖңthe light of the moon and starsвҖқ (e.g., Gen 1:14).
n210125: tn The verb вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ (shuv, вҖңto returnвҖқ) here denotes вҖңto desistвҖқ (HALOT 1430 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ 3). It pictures the disappearance of the clouds as a result of the precipitation of their contents.
n210127: tn The verb вҖҸЧ–Ц°Ч•ЦјЧўЦ·вҖҺ (zeuaКҝ, вҖңto trembleвҖқ) probably does not refer to physical tremors but to trembling in fear (e.g., Esth 5:9; Hab 2:7; Sir 48:12); cf. HALOT 267 s.v. вҖҸЧ–Ч•ЧўвҖҺ). At the onset of old age, those who had been the most courageous during their youth suddenly become fearful.
n210128: tn The verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ•Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ (Кҝavat, вҖңto bend; to stoopвҖқ) means вҖңto be stoopedвҖқ (HALOT 804 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•ЧӘвҖҺ) rather than вҖңto bend themselvesвҖқ (BDB 736 s.v. вҖҸЧўЧ•ЧӘвҖҺ). The perfect tense may be taken in an ingressive sense (вҖңbegin to stoop overвҖқ).
n210129: tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧҳЦјЦ№Ч—ЦІЧ Ч•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (hattokhanot, Qal active participle feminine plural from вҖҸЧҳЦёЧ—Ц·ЧҹвҖҺ, takhan, вҖңto grindвҖқ) is a double entendre. In its literal sense, it refers to female mill-grinders; in its figurative sense, it refers to molar teeth (HALOT 374 s.v. *вҖҸЧҳЦ№Ч—ЦІЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ). The related Hebrew noun вҖҸЧҳЦ·Ч—ЦІЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (takhanah) refers to a вҖңmill,вҖқ and the related Arabic noun tahinat means вҖңmolar toothвҖқ (HALOT 374 s.v. *вҖҸЧҳЦ·Ч—ЦІЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ).
n2101210: tn The verb вҖҸЧ—ЦёЧ©ЧҒЦ·ЧҡЦ°вҖҺ (khashakh, вҖңto grow dimвҖқ) is used elsewhere in reference to failing eyesight (e.g., Ps 69:24; Lam 5:17); see HALOT 361 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ч©ЧҒЧҡвҖҺ 2. Therefore, the phrase вҖңthose who look through the windowsвҖқ is probably a figurative description of the eyes, picturing failing eyesight at the onset of old age.
n2101211: tn The noun вҖҸЧҳЦ·Ч—ЦІЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (takhanah) refers to a вҖңgrinding-millвҖқ where grain is ground into flour (HALOT 374 s.v. вҖҸЧҳЦ·Ч—ЦІЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ). The term is here used as a double entendre, figuratively describing the loss of oneвҖҷs teeth at the onset of old age. The figurative usage also draws upon the polysemantic nature of this noun; the related Arabic root tahinat means вҖңmolar toothвҖқ (HALOT 374 s.v. *вҖҸЧҳЦ№Ч—ЦІЧ ЦёЧ”вҖҺ).
n2101212: tn Heb вҖңrises up.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ§Ч•ЦјЧқвҖҺ (qum, вҖңto ariseвҖқ) refers to being awakened from sleep in the middle of the night by a sound (e.g., Exod 12:30; 1 Sam 3:6, 8) and waking up early in the morning (e.g., Gen 24:54; Judg 16:3; Ruth 3:14; Neh 2:12; Job 14:12; 24:14); see HALOT 1086 s.v. вҖҸЧ§Ч•ЦјЧқвҖҺ 1; BDB 877 s.v. вҖҸЧ§Ч•ЦјЧқвҖҺ 1.a. Here it describes one of the frustrations of old age: the elderly person is unable to get a full nightвҖҷs sleep because every little sound awakens him in the middle of the night or too early in the morning.
n2101213: tn The term вҖңtheirвҖқ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2101214: tn Heb вҖңall the daughters of song.вҖқ The expression вҖңthe daughters of songвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°Ч Ч•Ц№ЧӘ Ч”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦҙЧҷЧЁвҖҺ, benot hashir) is an idiom for вҖңsongs; musical sounds; melodious notesвҖқ (HALOT 166 s.v. I вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ 2; BDB 123 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ 5; GKC 418 В§128.v). The genitive вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч©ЧҒЦјЦҙЧҷЧЁвҖҺ (вҖңsongвҖқ) represents the nature, quality, character or condition of the construct вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ°Ч Ч•Ц№ЧӘвҖҺ (вҖңdaughtersвҖқ); see IBHS 149-51 В§9.5.3b. The idiom refers to the musical songs sung during oneвҖҷs youth or to the ability to hear songs that are sung. This line is lamenting the loss of hearing which occurs at the onset of old age.
n2101216: tn The Hebrew noun вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (khatkhattim) literally means вҖңterrorsвҖқ (HALOT 363 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ; BDB 369 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ). Here it is used as a metonymy (cause for effect) to refer to dangers that cause the elderly to be fearful of going outside or walking along the streets. The form вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ is a reduplicated noun stem from the root вҖҸЧ—ЧӘЧӘвҖҺ (вҖңterrorвҖқ); HALOT 363 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ; BDB 369 s.v. вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ. The reduplication of the noun stem intensifies its meaning: the noun вҖҸЧ—ЦҙЧӘЦјЦ·ЧӘвҖҺ (khittat) means вҖңterror,вҖқ so the intensified reduplicated form вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘвҖҺ (khatkhat) connotes something like вҖңgreat terrorвҖқ (see S. Moscati, Comparative Grammar, 78-79, В§12.9-13). The plural form вҖҸЧ—Ц·ЧӘЦ°Ч—Ц·ЧӘЦјЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (вҖңgreat terrorsвҖқ) denotes plural of number (more than one) or plural of intensity (which would further intensify the experience of fear); see IBHS 122 В§7.4.3a.
n2101217: tn The noun вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ§ЦөЧ“вҖҺ (shaqed) is used in the OT in reference to the вҖңalmond nutвҖқ (e.g., Gen 43:11; Num 17:23 HT [17:8 ET]) and metonymically (product for thing producing it) for the вҖңalmond treeвҖқ (e.g., Jer 1:11); cf. HALOT 1638 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ§ЦөЧ“вҖҺ; BDB 1052 s.v. вҖҸЧ©ЧҒЦёЧ§ЦөЧ“вҖҺ 2.
n2101218: tn The verb вҖҸЧ ЧҰЧҘвҖҺ (вҖңto blossomвҖқ) is a geminate verb (II = III) that, in this case, is written with a matres lectionis (plene spelling) rather than the normal spelling of вҖҸЧ•ЧҷЧ ЧҘвҖҺ (GKC 204 В§73.g). The Hiphil verb вҖҸЧҷЦёЧ ЦөЧҗЧҘвҖҺ (yaneКҫts) is from the root вҖҸЧ ЧҰЧҘвҖҺ вҖңto shine; to sparkle; to blossomвҖқ (HALOT 717 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЧҰЧҘвҖҺ; BDB 665 s.v. вҖҸЧ ЦёЧҰЦ·ЧҘвҖҺ). It is used in reference to almond blossoms whose color progresses from pink to white as they ripen (e.g., Song 6:11). This is an appropriate metaphor (comparison of sight) to describe white hair that often accompanies the onset of old age.
n2101220: tn The verb вҖҸЧЎЦёЧ‘Ц·ЧңвҖҺ (saval, вҖңto bear a heavy loadвҖқ) means вҖңto drag oneself alongвҖқ as a burden (BDB 687 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЦёЧ‘Ц·ЧңвҖҺ) or вҖңto become thick; to move slowly forward; to clear offвҖқ (HALOT 741 s.v. вҖҸЧЎЧ‘ЧңвҖҺ).
n2101221: tn The noun вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЦјЧ•Ц№Ч ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҫaviyyonah, вҖңcaper berry, caper fruitвҖқ) is a hapax legomenon, occurring only here in the Hebrew Bible. It refers to the Capparis spinosa fruit which was eaten as an aphrodisiac in the ancient Near East (HALOT 5 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЦјЧ•Ц№Ч ЦёЧ”вҖҺ; BDB 2-3 s.v. вҖҸЧҗЦІЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЦјЧ•Ц№Ч ЦёЧ”вҖҺ). There are two options for the interpretation of this figure: (1) At the onset of old age, the sexual virility that marked oneвҖҷs youth is nothing more than a distant memory, and even aphrodisiacs fail to stimulate sexual desire to allow for sexual intercourse. (2) The onset of old age is like the shriveling up of the caper berry fruit; the once virile youth has passed his prime just like a shriveled caper berry can no longer provide a sexual stimulant.
n2101222:
tc The MT vocalizes consonantal вҖҸЧ•ЧӘЧӨЧЁвҖҺ as вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦёЧӨЦөЧЁвҖҺ (vetafer, conjunction plus Hiphil imperfect third person feminine singular from вҖҸЧӨЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ, parar, вҖңto burstвҖқ). However, an alternate vocalization tradition of вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦ»ЧӨЦјЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (vetupar, conjunction plus Hophal imperfect third person feminine singular вҖңto be broken downвҖқ) is reflected in the LXX which reads ОәОұбҪ¶ ОҙО№ОұПғОәОөОҙОұПғОёбҝҮ (kai diaskedasthД“, вҖңis scatteredвҖқ) and Symmachus ОәОұбҪ¶ ОҙО№ОұО»П…ОёбҝҮ (kai dialuthД“, вҖңis broken upвҖқ) which is followed by the Syriac. On the other hand, AquilaвҖҷs ОәОұбҪ¶ ОәОұПҒПҖОөПҚПғОөО№ (kai karpeusei, вҖңare enjoyed,вҖқ of fruits) reflects вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦҙЧӨЦ°ЧЁЦ¶Ч”вҖҺ (Qal imperfect third person feminine singular from вҖҸЧӨЦёЧЁЦ·Ч”вҖҺ, вҖңto bear fruitвҖқ); this does not reflect an alternate reading but a translatorвҖҷs error in word division between вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦёЧӨЦөЧЁ Ч”ЦёЧҗЦІЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЦјЧ•Ц№Ч ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (vetafer haКҫaviyyonah, вҖңthe caper berry burstsвҖқ) and вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӘЦҙЧӨЦ°ЧЁЦ¶Ч” ЧҗЦІЧ‘ЦҙЧҷЦјЧ•Ц№Ч ЦёЧ”вҖҺ (vetifreh Кҫaviyyonah, вҖңthe caper berry bears fruitвҖқ).
tn Or вҖңfailsвҖқ; or вҖңbursts.вҖқ The meaning of the verb вҖҸЧӨЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ (parar, вҖңto break; to make ineffectualвҖқ) is debated: (1) вҖңto be ineffectual,вҖқ that is, to fail to provide sexual power as an aphrodisiac, or (2) вҖңto break; to burst,вҖқ that is, the caper berry fruit shrivels as it lingers on its branch beyond its period of ripeness (HALOT 975 s.v. вҖҸЧӨЧЁЧЁвҖҺ 2.f; BDB 830 s.v. I. вҖҸЧӨЦјЦёЧЁЦ·ЧЁвҖҺ 2.d).
n2101223: tn In the construct phrase вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦөЧҷЧӘ ЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧһЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (bet Кҝolamo, вҖңhouse of his eternityвҖқ), the genitive вҖҸЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧһЧ•Ц№вҖҺ (вҖңeternityвҖқ) functions as an attributive adjective: вҖңhis eternal home.вҖқ This is an idiom for the grave as the resting place of the body (e.g., Ps 49:12 [11]; Job 7:9; 14:10-12; Eccl 12:5) or Sheol as the residence of the dead (e.g., Job 17:13; 30:23); see HALOT 124 s.v. I вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧӘвҖҺ 2; 799 (5); BDB 109 s.v. вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦ·ЧҷЦҙЧӘвҖҺ 1.d. For example, the term вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦөЧҷЧӘвҖҺ (вҖңhouseвҖқ) is used in Job 30:23 in parallelism with вҖңdeathвҖқ (вҖҸЧһЦёЧ•Ц¶ЧӘвҖҺ, mavet). The same idiom appears in postbiblical Hebrew: вҖңthe house of eternityвҖқ (вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦөЧҷЧӘ ЧўЧ•Ц№ЧңЦёЧқвҖҺ, bet Кҝolam) is a euphemism for a burial ground or cemetery (e.g., Lamentations Rabbah 1:5); see Jastrow 1084-85 s.v. вҖҸЧўЦёЧңЦ·ЧқвҖҺ III. This idiom is also found in a Moabite text in reference to the grave (Deir Alla Inscription 2:6). A similar idiom is found in Phoenician and Palmyrene in reference to the grave (DISO 35). The idiom appears to have originated in Egyptian literature (H. A. Hoffner, TDOT 2:113). See F. Cumont, Afterlife in Roman Paganism, 48-50.
n2101225: tn The term вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°Ч’ЦјЦ·ЧңвҖҺ (galgal, вҖңwheelвҖқ) refers to the вҖңwater wheelвҖқ or вҖңpaddle wheelвҖқ for drawing water from a well (HALOT 190 s.v. I вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°Ч’ЦјЦ·ЧңвҖҺ 2; BDB 165 s.v. вҖҸЧ’ЦјЦ·ЧңЦ°Ч’ЦјЦ·ЧңвҖҺ 1.b). This Hebrew noun is related to the Akkadian term gulgullu (вҖңpotвҖқ), as well as Phoenician (?) вҖҸЧ’ЧңЧ’ЧңвҖҺ (вҖңwheel for drawing waterвҖқ). The Latin term girgillus (вҖңlever for the bucketвҖқ) is a late derivation from this term. See G. Dalman, Arbeit und Sitte in PalГӨstina, 2:225-28.
n2101226: tn Or вҖңspirit.вҖқ The likely referent is the lifeвҖҷs breath that originates with God. See Eccl 3:19, as well as Gen 2:7; 6:17; 7:22.
n2101227: tn Heb вҖңfutility of futilities.вҖқ The phrase вҖңabsolutely futileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”ЦІЧ‘Ц¶Чң Ч”ЦІЧ‘ЦёЧңЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ, havel havalim) is a superlative genitive construction (GKC 431 В§133.i). See note on вҖңfutileвҖқ at 1:2.
n2101228: tn Elsewhere in the book, the author is identified with the anarthrous term вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (qohelet, Eccl 1:1, 2, 12; 7:27; 12:9, 10); however, in 12:8 it is used with the article, indicating that it is a professional title rather than a personal surname: вҖҸЧ”Ц·Ч§ЦјЧ•Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (haqqohelet, вҖңthe TeacherвҖқ). Numerous English translations render вҖҸЧ§Ц№Ч”Ц¶ЧңЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ as a professional title: вҖңthe SpeakerвҖқ (NEB, Moffatt); вҖңthe PreacherвҖқ (KJV, RSV, YLT, MLB, ASV, NASB); вҖңthe TeacherвҖқ (NIV, NRSV); вҖңthe Leader of the AssemblyвҖқ (NIV margin); вҖңthe AssemblerвҖқ (NJPS margin). Others render it as a personal surname: вҖңKohelethвҖқ (JPS, NJPS) and вҖңQohelethвҖқ (NAB, NRSV margin).
n2101229: tn Heb вҖңEverything.вҖқ The term is rendered вҖңall of these thingsвҖқ for clarity. Although вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (kol, вҖңeverything; allвҖқ) is often used in an absolute or comprehensive sense (BDB 481 s.v. вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ 1), it is frequently used as a synecdoche of the general for the specific, that is, its sense is limited contextually to the topic at hand (BDB 482 s.v. 2). This is particularly true of вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (hakkol, BDB 482 s.v. 2.b) in which the article particularizes or limits the referent to the contextual or previously mentioned topic (e.g., Gen 16:12; 24:1; Exod 29:24; Lev 1:9, 13; 8:27; Deut 2:36; Josh 11:19 [see 2 Sam 19:31; 1 Kgs 14:26 = 2 Chr 12:9]; 21:43; 1 Sam 30:19; 2 Sam 17:3; 23:5; 24:23; 1 Kgs 6:18; 2 Kgs 24:16; Isa 29:11; 65:8; Jer 13:7, 10; Ezek 7:14; Pss 14:3; 49:18; 1 Chr 7:5; 28:19; 29:19; 2 Chr 28:6; 29:28; 31:5; 35:7; 36:17-18; Ezra 1:11; 2:42; 8:34-35; 10:17; Eccl 5:8). Thus, вҖңallвҖқ does not always mean вҖңallвҖқ absolutely or universally in comprehension. In several cases the context limits its reference to two classes of objects/issues being discussed, so вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ means вҖңbothвҖқ (e.g., 2:14; 3:19: 9:1, 2). Thus, вҖҸЧ”Ц·ЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (вҖңall; everythingвҖқ) refers only to what Qoheleth characterizes as вҖңfutileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ, hevel) in the context. This does not mean that everything is futile. For example, fearing God is not вҖңfutileвҖқ (2:26; 3:14-15; 11:9-10; 12:1, 9, 13-14). Only those objects/issues that are contextually placed under вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ are designated as вҖңfutileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ).
n2101230:
tn The term вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ (hevel, вҖңfutileвҖқ) is repeated three times within the six words of this verse for emphasis. See footnote on вҖңfutileвҖқ at 1:2.
sn Absolutely futile!вҖҰAll of these things are futile! This motto is the theme of the book. It occurs at the beginning (1:2) and end of the book (12:8), forming an envelope structure (inclusio). Everything described in 1:2вҖ”12:8 is the supporting proof of the thesis of 1:2. With few exceptions (e.g., 2:24-26; 3:14-15; 11:9-12:1, 9), everything described in 1:2вҖ”12:8 is characterized as вҖңfutileвҖқ (вҖҸЧ”Ц¶Ч‘Ц¶ЧңвҖҺ, hevel).
n2101231: sn Eccl 12:9-12 fits the pattern of a concluding colophon that draws from a conventional stock of ancient Near Eastern scribal practices and vocabulary. See M. A. Fishbane, Biblical Interpretation, 29-31.
n2101232: tn Heb вҖңhe weighed and studied.вҖқ The verbs вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҗЦҙЧ–ЦјЦөЧҹ Ч•Ц°Ч—ЦҙЧ§ЦјЦөЧЁвҖҺ (veКҫizzen vekhiqqer, вҖңhe weighed and he exploredвҖқ) form a hendiadys (a figurative expression in which two separate terms used in combination to convey a single idea): вҖңhe studiously weighedвҖқ or вҖңcarefully evaluated.вҖқ The verb вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧҗЦҙЧ–ЦјЦөЧҹвҖҺ (conjunction + Piel perfect third person masculine singular from II вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ–Ц·ЧҹвҖҺ (Кҫazan) вҖңto weigh; to balanceвҖқ) is related to the noun вҖҸЧһЦ№ЧҗЧ–ЦөЧҹвҖҺ (moКҫzen) вҖңbalances; scalesвҖқ used for weighing money or commercial items (e.g., Jer 32:10; Ezek 5:1). This is the only use of the verb in the OT. In this context, it means вҖңto weighвҖқ = вҖңto test; to proveвҖқ (BDB 24 s.v. вҖҸЧһЧҗЧ–ЧҹвҖҺ) or вҖңto balanceвҖқ (HALOT 27 II вҖҸЧҗЦёЧ–Ц·ЧҹвҖҺ). Cohen suggests, вҖңHe made an examination of the large number of proverbial sayings which had been composed, testing their truth and worth, to select those which he considered deserving of circulationвҖқ (A. Cohen, The Five Megilloth [SoBB], 189).
n2101233: tn The verb вҖҸЧӘЦјЦёЧ§Ц·ЧҹвҖҺ (taqan, вҖңto make straightвҖқ) connotes вҖңto put straightвҖқ or вҖңto arrange in orderвҖқ (HALOT 1784 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЧ§ЧҹвҖҺ; BDB 1075 s.v. вҖҸЧӘЦјЦёЧ§Ц·ЧҹвҖҺ).This may refer to QohelethвҖҷs activity in compiling a collection of wisdom sayings in an orderly manner, or writing the wisdom sayings in a straightforward, direct manner.
n2101234: tn In the construct phrase вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЦөЧҷЦҫЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ (divre khefets, вҖңwords of delightвҖқ) the noun вҖҸЧ—ЦөЧӨЦ¶ЧҘвҖҺ (вҖңdelightвҖқ) functions as an attributive genitive (вҖңdelightful wordsвҖқ) or a genitive of estimation or worth (вҖңwords viewed as delightful by QohelethвҖқ or вҖңwords that he took delight inвҖқ). For another example of a genitive of estimation of worth, see вҖҸЧ–ЦҙЧ‘Ц°Ч—ЦөЧҷ ЧҗЦұЧңЦ№Ч”ЦҙЧҷЧқвҖҺ (zivkhe Кҫelohim) вҖңsacrifices of GodвҖқ = вҖңsacrifices viewed as acceptable to GodвҖқ (Ps 51:19). In other words, Qoheleth wrote his proverbs so effectively that he was able to take moral and aesthetic delight in his words.
n2101235: tc The consonantal form вҖҸЧ•ЧӣЧӘЧ•Ч‘вҖҺ has been revocalized in three ways: (1) The Masoretes read вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ (vekhatuv, conjunction plus Qal passive participle masculine singular from вҖҸЧӣЦјЦёЧӘЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ, katav, вҖңto writeвҖқ): вҖңQoheleth sought to find pleasant words, what was written uprightly, namely, words of truth.вҖқ This is supported by the LXXвҖҷs ОәОұбҪ¶ ОіОөОіПҒОұОјОјОӯОҪОҝОҪ (kai gegrammenon, conjunction plus masculine accusative singular perfect passive participle from ОіПҒО¬ПҶПү, graphЕҚ, вҖңto write). (2) The BHS editors suggest the vocalization вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (vekhatov, conjunction plus Qal infinitive absolute). The infinitive вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (вҖңand to writeвҖқ) in the B-line would parallel the infinitive of purpose вҖҸЧңЦҙЧһЦ°ЧҰЦ№ЧҗвҖҺ (limtsoКҫ, вҖңto findвҖқ) in the A-line: вҖңQoheleth sought to find pleasant words, and to write accurately words of truth.вҖқ (3) Several medieval Hebrew mss preserve an alternate textual tradition of вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (vekhatav, conjunction plus Qal perfect third person masculine singular). This is reflected in the Greek versions (Aquila and Symmachus), Syriac Peshitta and Vulgate. The major English versions are divided among these three textual options: (1) вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•ЦјЧ‘вҖҺ (Qal passive participle): вҖңand that which was written was upright, even words of truthвҖқ (KJV); вҖңand that which was written uprightly, even words of truthвҖқ (ASV); вҖңand, written by the upright, words of truthвҖқ (YLT); вҖңbut what he wrote was the honest truthвҖқ (NEB); вҖңand what he wrote was upright and trueвҖқ (NIV). (2) вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ (Qal infinitive absolute): вҖңand to write words of truth correctlyвҖқ (NASB); вҖңand to write correctly the reliable words of truthвҖқ (MLB); вҖңand to write down true sayings with precisionвҖқ (NAB). (3) вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЦ·Ч‘вҖҺ (Qal perfect third person masculine singular): вҖңand uprightly he wrote words of truthвҖқ (RSV); вҖңand he wrote words of truth plainlyвҖқ (NRSV); вҖңeven as he put down plainly what was trueвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңand he wrote words most right, and full of truthвҖқ (Douay); and вҖңand he recorded genuinely truthful sayingsвҖқ (NJPS). The editors of the Jerusalem Hebrew Bible project favor вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№Ч‘вҖҺ вҖңand to writeвҖқ (option 2): see D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:596-97.
n2101236: tn The construct phrase вҖҸЧ“ЦјЦҙЧ‘Ц°ЧЁЦөЧҷ ЧҗЦұЧһЦ¶ЧӘвҖҺ (divre Кҫemet, вҖңwords of truthвҖқ) is a genitive of content (вҖңwords containing truthвҖқ) or an attributive genitive (вҖңtruthful wordsвҖқ). Depending upon the vocalization of вҖҸЧ•ЧӣЧӘЧ•Ч‘вҖҺ, the phrase functions in one of two ways: (1) as direct object of вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•Ц№Ч‘ ЧҷЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (vekhatov yosher) вҖңand he accurately wrote truthful wordsвҖқ; or (2) in apposition to вҖҸЧ•Ц°ЧӣЦёЧӘЧ•ЦјЧ‘ ЧҷЦ№Ч©ЧҒЦ¶ЧЁвҖҺ (vekhatuv yosher) вҖңand what is written uprightly, namely, truthful words.вҖқ
n2101237: tn Or вҖңgoadsвҖқ; NCV вҖңsharp sticks used to guide animals.вҖқ For further information see M. A. Fishbane, Biblical Interpretation, 29-32.
n2101238: sn The exhortation may be understood in two ways: (1) to avoid any so-called wisdom sayings beyond those mentioned in vv. 10-11: вҖңThe words of the wiseвҖҰare given from one shepherd. And of anything beyond these, my son, be warned!вҖқ (see RSV, NRSV, NAB, Douay, NIV). This is paraphrased well by Moffatt: вҖңMy son, avoid anything beyond the scriptures of wisdomвҖқ (Moffatt). (2) The exhortation refers to the concerns of v. 12b, namely, diligent study is wearisome, i.e., вҖңFurthermore, my son, be warned: there is no end to the making of books, and much study is wearisome to the bodyвҖқ (see NEB, ASV, NASB, MLB).
n2101239: tn The verb вҖҸЧўЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧ”вҖҺ (Кҝasah, вҖңto doвҖқ) may mean вҖңto makeвҖқ (HALOT 890 s.v. I вҖҸЧўЧ©ЧӮЧ”вҖҺ 3) or вҖңto acquireвҖқ (HALOT 891 s.v. I вҖҸЧўЧ©ЧӮЧ”вҖҺ 6). The LXX rendered it as ПҖОҝО№бҝҶПғОұО№ (poiД“sai, вҖңmakingвҖқ), as do most English versions: вҖңmakingвҖқ (KJV, YLT, RSV, NRSV, NAB, ASV, MLB, NIV, NJPS). However, several English versions reflect a different nuance: вҖңthere is no end to the buying of booksвҖқ (Moffatt); вҖңthe use of books is endlessвҖқ (NEB); and вҖңthe writing of many books is endlessвҖқ (NASB).
n2101240: tn Heb вҖңthe flesh.вҖқ The term вҖҸЧ‘ЦјЦёЧ©ЧӮЦёЧЁвҖҺ (basar, вҖңfleshвҖқ) refers to the body, functioning as a synecdoche or part (i.e., flesh, skin) for the whole (i.e., body), e.g., Gen 17:13; Ps 16:9; Prov 14:30 (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 642).
n2101241: tn Heb вҖңThe end of the matter, everything having been heard.вҖқ
n2101242: tn Heb вҖңThis is all menвҖқ; or вҖңThis is the whole of man.вҖқ The phrase вҖҸЧ–Ц¶Ч” ЧӣЦјЦёЧңЦҫЧ”ЦёЧҗЦёЧ“ЦёЧқвҖҺ (zeh kol haКҫadam, вҖңthis is all menвҖқ) features rhetorical elision of a key word. The ambiguity over the elided word has led to no less than five basic approaches: (1) вҖңthis is the whole duty of manвҖқ (KJV, ASV, RSV, NAB, NIV); (2) вҖңthis is the duty of all menвҖқ (MLB, ASV margin, RSV margin); (3) вҖңthis applies to all menвҖқ (NASB, NJPS); (4) вҖңthis is the whole duty of all menвҖқ (NRSV, Moffatt); and (5) вҖңthere is no more to man than thisвҖқ (NEB). The four-fold repetition of вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ (kol, вҖңallвҖқ) in 12:13-14 suggests that Qoheleth is emphasizing the вҖңbottom line,вҖқ that is, the basic duty of man is simply to fear and obey God: After вҖңallвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ) has been heard in the book, his conclusion is that the вҖңwholeвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ) duty of man is to obey God because God will bring вҖңallвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ) acts into judgment, including вҖңallвҖқ (вҖҸЧӣЦјЦ№ЧңвҖҺ) that is hidden, whether good or bad. See D. BarthГ©lemy, ed., Preliminary and Interim Report on the Hebrew Old Testament Text Project, 3:596.
n2101243: tn Heb вҖңwill bring every deed into judgment.вҖқ
